channel.title: Posts on Shit PH Said, Mostly By Anwar
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Shit PH Said, Mostly By Anwar

item.title: Anwar Preaches 'Integrity Culture' and Launches CeIO Programme — While His Government Shields MACC Chief and Interrogates Anti-Corruption NGOs
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-12

📌 What Happened

On March 12, 2026, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim chaired the Special Cabinet Committee on National Governance (JKKTN) and announced the launch of a Certified Integrity Officer (CeIO) programme. In a Facebook post and subsequent Bernama coverage, Anwar declared that “law enforcement alone is insufficient to combat corruption” and called for “a broader culture of integrity and sound governance practices.” He said issues like “leakage of public funds and abuse of power” undermine governance, public health, the environment, and national integrity. The committee agreed to implement CeIO as part of efforts to “strengthen national governance.”

🔥 Why It Matters

This statement lands in the same week that Anwar’s government delegated the Azam Baki shareholding report to the Chief Secretary instead of acting on it, refused to disclose the report’s findings, rejected calls for a Royal Commission of Inquiry into MACC, and allowed police to interrogate C4 Centre’s anti-corruption CEO under Section 124B — a law meant for threats to parliamentary democracy. The gap between Anwar’s rhetoric and his government’s actions on anti-corruption is no longer a gap. It is a canyon. Every new “integrity” initiative announced while the MACC chief remains in post and civil society gets hauled to Bukit Aman makes the rhetoric sound less like conviction and more like performance.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

You do not need a Certified Integrity Officer programme when the head of your anti-corruption commission is accused of breaching shareholding laws and your own coalition partners are publicly demanding an independent inquiry you refuse to grant. Integrity is not a certificate. It is not a programme. It is what you do when the political cost is high — and Anwar has consistently chosen the lowest-cost option on every accountability question put before him. The CeIO programme is bureaucratic window dressing: another acronym, another committee, another layer of process designed to look like action while the actual crisis — an MACC chief credibly accused of misconduct, still drawing a government salary — remains untouched.

📜 Previous Cases

This is not the first time Anwar has deployed reform language as a substitute for reform action. On March 9, he launched the Capital Market Masterplan and declared reform “non-negotiable” — the same week the PM tenure limit bill failed because his own MPs didn’t show up. On March 6, he told Bersih to stop criticising MACC “during Ramadan,” using the holy month as a shield. On March 3, he told Parliament about a “plot to topple the government” — a narrative now being used to justify police interrogation of anti-corruption activists. The pattern is unmistakable: announce a reform initiative, deflect criticism with moral language, and let the underlying problem persist.

🎯 PH Callout

You ran on Reformasi. You promised Malaysians that your government would be different — that integrity would be a practice, not a slogan. Three years in, your anti-corruption chief is credibly accused of misconduct, your police are questioning anti-corruption NGOs under sedition-era laws, and your response is a Facebook post about “integrity culture” and a new certification programme. Malaysians do not need more certificates. They need a Prime Minister who will suspend an MACC chief under investigation, grant the RCI his own coalition demands, and stop using process as a substitute for accountability. Every day Azam Baki remains in post is a day your integrity talk means nothing.

Sources: Bernama | NST

item.title: Economy Minister Celebrates 2025 Numbers and Maintains 4.5% Growth Target — On the Same Day Fuel Prices Explode
item.description:
Said by: Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir, Economy Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-12

📌 What Happened

Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir maintained Malaysia’s 2026 growth forecast at 4.0% to 4.5% on March 12, 2026, while launching the 13th Malaysia Plan implementation monitoring system (MyRMK) in Putrajaya. He celebrated 2025’s GDP growth of 5.2%, unemployment at a decade-low 2.9%, and inflation moderating to 1.4% — declaring that “the government’s continuous efforts in strengthening the economy and improving the well-being of the people are beginning to show positive results.” He acknowledged West Asia conflicts “could disrupt the stability of global energy supply and international trade” but insisted the growth target stands.

🔥 Why It Matters

This statement was made on the same day the Ministry of Finance announced the sharpest weekly fuel price hike in years: RON97 up 60 sen to RM3.85 (18%), unsubsidised RON95 up 60 sen to RM3.27, and diesel up 80 sen to RM3.92 (26%). The Strait of Hormuz is disrupted, oil is past $100 a barrel, and the government’s own special committee is scrambling to assess economic fallout — yet the Economy Minister’s response is a launch event for a monitoring dashboard and a celebration of last year’s numbers. When the house is on fire, you don’t show guests the blueprints.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Maintaining a 4.0-4.5% growth target while fuel prices explode, supply chains fracture, and inflation is about to surge is not confidence — it’s denial dressed up as stability. Bloomberg reported on March 6 that Malaysia was already “holding off on a growth upgrade” due to Middle East uncertainty. Now the crisis has escalated dramatically, and the best the Economy Minister can offer is backward-looking GDP figures and a new monitoring system. The gap between Putrajaya’s optimism and the rakyat’s reality at the fuel pump has never been wider.

📜 Previous Cases

This is a pattern. Deputy Economy Minister Mohd Shahar Abdullah made the exact same 4.0-4.5% claim at the ASEAN Banking and Finance Summit on March 9 — even as the summit’s own chairman warned that geopolitical tensions were “repricing risk across the entire region.” Before the Middle East crisis, Anwar himself was talking about upgrading the growth target. Now the government’s position has gone from “we’ll upgrade” to “we’ll hold” to “we’ll monitor” — each step a retreat disguised as prudence.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised data-driven governance and evidence-based policy. The data says fuel is up 18-26% in a single week, oil is past $100, and your supply buffer runs out in May. Your own special committee is meeting Friday because the situation is critical. Yet the Economy Minister is on stage celebrating 2025 numbers and launching dashboards. The rakyat don’t need a monitoring system — they need a government that tells the truth about what’s coming. Say it plainly: the growth target is at risk, the cost of living is about to get worse, and here is what we will do about it. Anything less is spin.

Sources: The Edge Malaysia | Bernama | Malay Mail

item.title: Ramanan Says Government 'Studying' WFH for Public Sector — Thailand Acted Immediately, Malaysia Still Forming a Study
item.description:
Said by: R. Ramanan, Human Resources Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-12

📌 What Happened

On March 12, 2026, Human Resources Minister Ramanan Ramakrishnan told reporters in Putrajaya that possible cost-saving measures — including work-from-home for public sector employees and the private sector — are being “studied” and will be presented at a special Cabinet meeting on Friday, March 14. “We have seen what Thailand has done, and we are currently conducting studies on this,” Ramanan said. He added that he would receive a briefing from deputy secretaries-general and then present findings to Cabinet. He acknowledged the prolonged Middle East conflict and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz as drivers of the crisis.

🔥 Why It Matters

Thailand’s Cabinet approved mandatory work-from-home for government officials immediately upon the escalation of the energy crisis. Malaysia’s government — facing the same crisis, with RON97 up 18%, diesel up 26%, and oil past $100 a barrel — is still “conducting studies.” The contrast is damning. Every day Malaysian workers commute to offices they could work from remotely is a day the government is forcing the rakyat to absorb fuel costs that could be avoided with a single directive. Ramanan’s own framing gives the game away: “If we ourselves do not practise savings but ask the people to do so, that would not be fair.” Correct. So why are you still studying instead of acting?

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

The COVID-19 pandemic proved — three years ago — that Malaysian public servants and private sector workers can work from home effectively. The infrastructure exists. The precedent exists. The legal framework for flexible work arrangements already exists under the amended Employment Act. There is nothing left to “study.” What Ramanan is really saying is that the government does not want to issue a directive without political cover from a Cabinet meeting that hasn’t happened yet. This is not governance. This is a bureaucracy waiting for permission to do the obvious. Thailand acted in hours. Malaysia is taking days — and counting.

📜 Previous Cases

On March 11, this database logged an entry noting that Anwar’s crisis response to $100+ oil included solidarity tweets and “monitoring” — but no WFH mandate, no toll waiver, no food price caps. Now, one day later, the government has graduated from “monitoring” to “studying.” During the ASEAN Summit in May 2025, Fahmi Fadzil spent weeks saying the Cabinet was “deliberating” WFH before eventually implementing it. The pattern is consistent: Malaysia’s government treats every decision as requiring maximum process and minimum urgency, even when the cost of delay is borne entirely by ordinary workers.

🎯 PH Callout

You told Malaysians you would lead by example. Ramanan said it himself: it would be “unfair” to ask the people to save while the government doesn’t. So act. Issue the directive today. Thailand didn’t need a Friday Cabinet meeting to decide that government workers should stop burning expensive fuel to sit in offices with perfectly functional internet connections. Every day you “study” is a day you’re choosing process over people. The rakyat cannot afford your deliberation speed.

Sources: The Star | Bernama

item.title: RON97 Up 18%, Diesel Up 26% — Government Lets Unsubsidised Fuel Prices Explode While Claiming It's 'Cushioning the Rakyat'
item.description:
Said by: Ministry of Finance / Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-12

📌 What Happened

The Ministry of Finance announced on March 11 that effective March 12-18, RON97 petrol will surge 60 sen per litre to RM3.85 — an 18% increase from RM3.25. Unsubsidised RON95 jumps 60 sen to RM3.27 — a 22% spike. Diesel in Peninsular Malaysia leaps 80 sen to RM3.92 — a staggering 26% increase. The government maintained the Budi95 subsidised rate at RM1.99 for eligible motorists and offered a RM100 top-up for diesel vehicle owners under existing schemes, raising interim cash assistance from RM200 to RM300 for March. The MOF said Malaysia’s prices “remain lower than neighbouring countries” and promised “strengthened enforcement” against fuel leakage.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is the sharpest single-week fuel price adjustment since the Middle East crisis escalated. Diesel at RM3.92 directly hits logistics, haulage, and food supply chains — costs that will be passed to consumers within days. The two-tier fuel system is now stretched to breaking point: subsidised RON95 sits at RM1.99 while unsubsidised RON95 is at RM3.27, a 64% gap that incentivises smuggling and gaming. The government’s entire fuel strategy is now a subsidy wall protecting one product while everything else burns. Businesses that run on diesel — fishermen, farmers, truckers, food manufacturers — are absorbing a 26% cost spike with no price controls downstream. This will feed directly into food inflation during Ramadan, when household spending is already elevated.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

The government’s framing is pure theatre. “The government will continue to bear part of the subsidy to cushion the impact” — they are subsidising one fuel grade and letting everything else rip. The RM100 diesel top-up is an insult: if you’re a lorry operator burning hundreds of litres a week, RM300 a month covers roughly one fill-up at the new price. Meanwhile, the MOF’s line about prices being “lower than neighbouring countries” is the oldest deflection in the subsidy playbook — it tells you nothing about whether Malaysian wages can absorb these costs. Anwar promised fuel subsidy rationalisation that would be “fair and gradual.” A 26% diesel spike in one week is neither fair nor gradual. It’s crisis management dressed up as policy.

📜 Previous Cases

This government has been kicking the fuel subsidy can since taking office. The targeted subsidy for RON95 (Budi95) was meant to replace blanket subsidies, but diesel rationalisation has been stop-start since June 2024 when diesel subsidies were first restructured. Every time global prices spike, the government scrambles with ad hoc cash handouts instead of structural reform. Just last week, Anwar admitted he could only hold the RON95 subsidy for “one or two months” — and now the prices around it are exploding. The pattern is clear: announce reform, delay implementation, throw cash when crisis hits, repeat.

🎯 PH Callout

You told Malaysians you would rationalise subsidies fairly. You told them the economy was strong and well-managed. You told them you had a plan. An 18% spike in RON97 and a 26% spike in diesel in a single week is not a plan — it’s a surrender to market forces with a press release attached. The rakyat who drive RON97 or run diesel-dependent businesses are not “cushioned” — they are exposed. And the fishermen, farmers, and truckers who feed this country are now subsidising your political survival with their margins. If the Budi95 wall cracks — and at these global prices, it will — you have no backup plan. Just more press statements about how prices are lower than Singapore.

Sources: The Edge Malaysia | MOF Official Statement

item.title: Anwar Bans Raya Open Houses for 'Fiscal Discipline' — Days After Announcing RM15 Billion in Cash Handouts and RM500 Bonuses
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

On March 11, 2026, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim announced that all government agencies, GLCs, and GLICs are banned from holding Aidilfitri open house celebrations this year — a decision agreed upon in Cabinet that morning. He framed it as a signal of fiscal discipline amid global uncertainty caused by the Middle East conflict. In the same breath, Anwar also announced tighter restrictions on overseas travel for ministers and government officials, saying only pre-scheduled and essential foreign engagements would be approved. A special Cabinet meeting has been called for March 13 to assess the fiscal situation.

🔥 Why It Matters

This austerity performance arrives just two days after the government announced it would maintain the RON95 subsidy at RM1.99 — and less than a week after Anwar brought forward RM15 billion in Sumbangan Tunai Rahmah (STR) cash handouts plus RM500 Special Financial Assistance for civil servants and mosque personnel, all conveniently timed before Hari Raya. The government is spending record sums on direct cash transfers — the most electorally potent form of spending — while cancelling community open houses that cost a fraction of that. This is not fiscal discipline. This is selective austerity: cut the visible, communal celebrations while flooding individual bank accounts with cash right before a rumoured snap election.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

You cannot announce the largest cash transfer programme in Malaysian history one week, then cancel Raya open houses the next week and call it prudence. Open houses are community events — they cost millions. The STR programme costs billions. If the Middle East crisis is so dire that civil servants can’t eat ketupat at a government function, why is it not dire enough to pause the RM15 billion in pre-election cash? Because open houses don’t buy votes. Cash in bank accounts before polling day does. The travel restriction is window dressing — ’except for those already scheduled’ is a loophole wide enough to fly the entire Cabinet through.

📜 Previous Cases

This is textbook Malaysian crisis theatre. Mahathir did it during the 1997 Asian financial crisis — austerity for the rakyat, bailouts for cronies. Najib talked fiscal discipline while 1MDB bled billions. Anwar himself mocked BN’s festive-season handouts when he was in opposition, calling them vote-buying. Now he’s doing the same thing, but bigger: record STR payouts, record BKK bonuses, and a Raya ban for optics. The pattern is always the same — cut the things that don’t win elections, spend lavishly on the things that do.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised reformasi, not repackaged BN-era electoral spending with a piety overlay. If you’re genuinely worried about the fiscal position, start with the RM15 billion in cash transfers you just announced, not the nasi lemak at government open houses. Cancel the political appointments bleeding FSBs dry. Freeze the patronage spending. Don’t lecture the rakyat about moderation while your government’s chequebook is wide open for every vote bank that matters before GE16. This is not discipline — this is performance.

Sources: Malay Mail | The Rakyat Post

item.title: Anwar Says Petroleum Supply 'Secure Until May' — While Strait of Hormuz Burns and Ships Get Attacked
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

On March 11, 2026, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim held a live-streamed press conference in Putrajaya to address the escalating Middle East energy crisis. He claimed that Malaysia’s petroleum product supplies are “sufficient at least until May 2026,” citing checks with Petronas. He reaffirmed the RM1.99 subsidised RON95 price under Budi Madani, called it a “difficult decision,” and announced a special task force of ministers to review economic impacts daily. He also tasked the Deputy Prime Minister and KPDN with cracking down on fuel smuggling.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is the Prime Minister of an energy-importing nation telling the public not to worry about supply — while the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly a fifth of the world’s oil transits, is under active military disruption. A Thai cargo ship was attacked near the Strait on the same day. Brent crude hit US$119 per barrel days earlier before easing to around US$90. Airlines across Asia are hiking fares. The IEA is recommending a record 400-million-barrel emergency reserve release. Anwar’s reassurance lands in a context where every major energy market signal is flashing red — and “secure until May” is only two months away.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

“Secure until May” is not reassurance — it’s a confession dressed up as comfort. Two months of supply in the middle of a shooting war near the world’s most important oil chokepoint is not energy security. It’s a countdown. Just days ago, on March 6, Anwar himself admitted he could only hold the RON95 subsidy for “one or two months.” Now the supply itself has a two-month shelf life. And the solution? More committees. A daily economic impact review panel. A smuggling task force. None of this addresses what happens in June if the Strait stays contested, if Petronas hedges unwind, or if global allocation priorities shift away from Southeast Asia. The press conference was a performance of control, not a demonstration of it.

📜 Previous Cases

Anwar has a well-documented pattern of using Petronas as a rhetorical shield. In 2023, he promised fuel subsidy rationalisation “within months” — it took until late 2024 to even introduce Budi Madani, and the targeted subsidy still doesn’t cover diesel properly. In 2024, he told Malaysians the economy was “on track” while inflation ate into household budgets. The “we checked with Petronas” line echoes previous administrations — Najib’s government used similar assurances during the 2014-2015 oil price crash while raiding Petronas dividends to plug fiscal holes. The playbook is always the same: invoke Petronas, announce a committee, and hope the crisis passes before the next press conference.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised Malaysians a government that tells the truth, even when it’s hard. So tell the truth: two months of supply is not security — it’s a ticking clock. Where is the contingency plan for June? Where is the strategic petroleum reserve policy you never built? Where is the long-term energy diversification strategy beyond “Petronas says we’re fine”? You banned Raya open houses for “fiscal discipline” the same week you announced RM15 billion in cash handouts. You formed three new committees in one press conference. Committees don’t produce oil. If the Strait stays shut and supply chains fracture, “we checked with Petronas” will not keep the lights on or the lorries moving. Stop performing calm and start building resilience.

Sources: Malay Mail | The Sun | The Edge Malaysia | NST

item.title: Cabinet Finally Discusses Azam Baki Report — Punts Decision to Chief Secretary, Refuses to Reveal Findings
item.description:
Said by: Fahmi Fadzil, Communications Minister / Cabinet

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

On March 11, 2026, Communications Minister Fahmi Fadzil announced that Cabinet had received and discussed the findings of the special committee — chaired by Attorney General Mohd Dusuki Mokhtar — investigating MACC chief commissioner Azam Baki’s shareholding controversy. Cabinet’s decision: hand the matter to Chief Secretary to the Government Tan Sri Shamsul Azri Abu Bakar to determine “next steps.” Fahmi refused to disclose the report’s contents. When asked directly whether Cabinet discussed the extension of Azam’s contract, Fahmi said: “It was not discussed.” Separately, Cabinet instructed the police, Securities Commission, MACC itself, and the Inland Revenue Board to continue investigating Bloomberg’s “corporate mafia” allegations — including seizing proceeds believed to have criminal elements.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is the Anwar administration’s formal institutional response to months of crisis over the MACC chief — and it amounts to a bureaucratic handoff. The Bloomberg exposé dropped weeks ago. DAP demanded an RCI. PKR backbenchers warned Anwar risks his legitimacy. Civil society has been summoned and interrogated. And after all of that, Cabinet’s big move is to delegate to a civil servant and refuse to tell the public what the AG’s committee actually found. The message is clear: accountability will be managed, not delivered. By routing the decision through the Chief Secretary rather than making a political call, Anwar insulates himself from direct responsibility for whatever happens next — whether Azam stays, goes, or quietly expires in May.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

The Cabinet is the highest executive decision-making body in the country. It received the report. It discussed the findings. And then it said: not our call. This is the equivalent of a CEO receiving an internal audit on a compromised compliance officer and telling HR to “handle it.” The Prime Minister has the power to act. He chose not to. The refusal to disclose the findings is especially damning — if the report cleared Azam, they would say so. If it condemned him, they would act. The silence tells you everything. Meanwhile, the instruction to probe “corporate mafia” allegations sounds tough until you remember MACC is being asked to investigate… itself. The fox is auditing the henhouse, and Cabinet just handed it the clipboard.

📜 Previous Cases

Azam Baki’s shareholding scandal has been a slow-rolling crisis since 2022, when the Securities Commission confirmed he held millions of shares in a public company — a potential breach of anti-corruption rules. Anwar, upon taking power, renewed Azam’s contract multiple times while promising reform. In February 2026, Bloomberg published detailed allegations of a “corporate mafia” operating within MACC. Anwar’s response has followed a consistent pattern: acknowledge the problem, set up a committee, delay action, and let the news cycle move on. The Straits Times reported on March 9 that Anwar had privately told Cabinet he would not renew Azam’s contract when it expires May 12 — but still refused to suspend him in the meantime.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised Malaysia “institutional reform” and “zero tolerance for corruption.” Your own manifesto said MACC would be made independent and accountable. Instead, your Cabinet received a report on the most compromised anti-corruption chief in Malaysian history and responded with: “Ask the Chief Secretary.” That is not reform. That is not accountability. That is not even governance. It is cowardice dressed in procedure. If you cannot fire the man running your anti-corruption agency while he is under investigation for the very corruption he is supposed to fight, then every word of your reform agenda is decoration. Act, or admit you never intended to.

Sources: Malay Mail | FMT | Bloomberg

item.title: Cabinet Greenlights Port Klang Third Terminal on Carey Island — A 5,000-Acre Megaproject With GLC Fingerprints All Over It
item.description:
Said by: Anthony Loke, Transport Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

On March 11, 2026, Transport Minister Anthony Loke announced that Cabinet has approved the development of Port Klang’s Third Terminal on Carey Island — a megaproject that has been in limbo for years. The project involves over 5,000 acres of land, with SD Guthrie (a GLC under PNB) and the Selangor State Development Corporation (PKNS) already staking claims on thousands of hectares of land and seabed. PKNS was allocated 1,011 hectares of seabed land and a 688-hectare Yayasan Selangor plot was placed under joint PKNS management. PM Anwar issued a directive in December 2025 to accelerate the project, and now Cabinet has formally greenlit it.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is one of the largest infrastructure approvals of the Anwar administration — and it has GLC and state-linked corporation fingerprints all over it. The land allocation to PKNS and the SD Guthrie partnership were locked in before any competitive tender for the port itself. Carey Island is home to the Mah Meri Orang Asli community, and environmental and displacement concerns have dogged this project for years. Anwar promised in December 2025 that the project would proceed “with safeguards for Orang Asli and the environment,” but no independent environmental review or community consent mechanism has been made public. The approval comes amid a Middle East crisis, fiscal austerity theatre (banning Raya open houses), and pre-election positioning — making the timing politically convenient for announcing a mega-development that benefits Selangor’s PH-aligned state machinery.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

PH ran on transparency, open tenders, and ending GLC capture of public assets. What we got instead is a 5,000-acre megaproject carved up between a PNB-linked plantation giant and the Selangor state GLC before the public even saw a development plan. The Selangor MB’s office admitted the land allocation was “decided a few weeks ago” — before Cabinet even approved the project. That’s not governance; that’s pre-positioning. And the Orang Asli? They get “safeguards” in speeches and displacement in practice. This is exactly the kind of opaque, GLC-driven megaproject that PH promised to end when it was in opposition.

📜 Previous Cases

Malaysia’s infrastructure megaproject history is littered with cost overruns, opaque procurement, and GLC enrichment. The East Coast Rail Link (ECRL) was renegotiated but still lacks full transparency. The Penang Transport Master Plan has faced years of environmental and governance controversy. Port Klang itself was the site of the Port Klang Free Zone (PKFZ) scandal — a RM12.5 billion fiasco involving inflated land costs, political patronage, and criminal charges. PH’s own 2018-2020 government inherited that mess and promised never to repeat it. Now they’re greenlighting another massive port project on the same stretch of coast, with pre-allocated GLC land deals and no visible competitive process.

🎯 PH Callout

You told voters you’d end the cosy GLC-state nexus that turns public land into private profit. You said open tenders and institutional reform would replace backroom deals. So where is the open tender for Port Klang Terminal 3? Where is the independent environmental impact assessment? Where is the Orang Asli consultation? A 5,000-acre development involving SD Guthrie, PKNS, and Yayasan Selangor land — all allocated before Cabinet even voted — is not reform. It’s the same game with a Madani label. If you can ban Raya open houses for “fiscal discipline,” you can publish the full procurement framework for a multi-billion ringgit port before you approve it.

Sources: Bernama | Malay Mail | The Edge Malaysia | NST

item.title: Cabinet Maintains RON95 at RM1.99 — Anwar Calls It a 'Difficult Decision' While Dodging Subsidy Reform for the Third Year Running
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim announced on March 11, 2026 — following a Cabinet meeting — that the price of RON95 petrol under the Budi95 targeted subsidy mechanism will remain at RM1.99 per litre. He called it a “difficult decision,” noting that the market price of RON95 has risen roughly threefold due to the escalating US-Iran conflict disrupting oil markets and threatening Gulf shipping routes. RON97 had already risen to RM3.25, unsubsidised RON95 to RM2.67, and diesel to RM3.12. Anwar framed the decision as a sacrifice for the rakyat.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is not generosity — it is the collapse of reform. Anwar’s government promised targeted fuel subsidy restructuring as a cornerstone of fiscal discipline. The Budi95 mechanism, launched as a watered-down compromise, was supposed to be the first step toward removing blanket subsidies. Instead, a geopolitical crisis has given the government the perfect excuse to shelve reform indefinitely while spending billions on untargeted fuel subsidies ahead of widely expected snap elections. Every week the subsidy is maintained at current market spreads, Malaysia hemorrhages fiscal resources that could go to healthcare, education, or infrastructure.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Anwar has been “about to reform” fuel subsidies since he took office in November 2022. In 2023, it was “next year.” In 2024, it was “soon.” In 2025, the Budi95 mechanism finally launched — then immediately became a political tool rather than a fiscal one. Just five days ago, Anwar admitted he could only hold the RM1.99 price for “one or two months.” Now, with Hari Raya approaching and elections rumoured for mid-2026, the Cabinet “decides” to maintain it — as if there was ever a chance they’d raise fuel prices weeks before a festive season and a potential snap poll. This isn’t a difficult decision. It’s the only decision a government terrified of voters would make.

📜 Previous Cases

Malaysia has been trapped in the fuel subsidy cycle for decades. Najib’s government removed RON95 subsidies in 2014 with a managed float, only for Mahathir’s PH 1.0 government to reintroduce price ceilings. Anwar promised to break the pattern with targeted subsidies — means-tested, fiscally responsible, reform-driven. Instead, Budi95 kept the same RM1.99 price for the vast majority of motorists and excluded only foreign-registered vehicles and luxury cars. The IMF, World Bank, and Malaysia’s own fiscal policy advisors have repeatedly warned that blanket fuel subsidies are regressive, benefiting the wealthy more than the poor. Anwar knows this. He cited these studies himself when he was in opposition.

🎯 PH Callout

You told Malaysians you’d be different. You said you’d make the hard calls Najib and Muhyiddin wouldn’t. You quoted fiscal responsibility in every speech, promised targeted subsidies in every manifesto, and lectured the opposition about populism from every podium. Now you’re spending billions to keep fuel cheap during Ramadan, ahead of Hari Raya, ahead of elections you’re quietly preparing for. This isn’t reform. This is the oldest trick in Malaysian politics — and you swore you’d never play it. Every ringgit spent on untargeted subsidies is a ringgit stolen from the structural reforms you promised the rakyat.

Sources: The Star — Cabinet decides to maintain subsidised RON95 price at RM1.99 | Malay Mail | The Star — PM: Fuel subsidy can last up to two months (March 7) | paultan.org — Budi95 petrol price maintained (March 6)

item.title: Fahmi Announces Special Committee to 'Assess' Economic Impact of US-Iran Conflict — Government's Crisis Playbook Is Another Committee
item.description:
Said by: Fahmi Fadzil, Communications Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

Communications Minister Fahmi Fadzil announced on March 11 that a special committee, led by Petronas chairman Amir Hamzah and including Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir, will evaluate the economic impact of the US-Iran conflict on Malaysia ‘on a daily basis.’ The committee’s findings will be presented at a special Cabinet meeting scheduled for Friday, March 13. Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim said Malaysia’s petroleum supply is ‘sufficient at least until May 2026,’ even as global oil markets reel from the Strait of Hormuz disruption and retaliatory strikes between Iran, the US, and Israel that began on February 28.

🔥 Why It Matters

The government’s instinctive response to a crisis that is already hitting Malaysian wallets — RON97 up 60 sen, diesel up 80 sen, food inflation incoming — is to form a committee to ‘assess’ the damage. Not to act. Not to freeze tolls, cap food prices, mandate work-from-home, or cut wasteful political spending. To study. This is the Madani crisis management playbook distilled to its essence: announce a committee, schedule a meeting, promise a report, and tell the rakyat to wait. The committee doesn’t even meet until March 12, two full weeks after the conflict began. Fuel prices have already spiked. Supply chains are already disrupted. A Thai cargo ship was attacked near the Strait of Hormuz. The time for ‘assessment’ was February 28. The time for action was last week.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

This is not governance. This is bureaucratic theatre dressed up as crisis management. The committee is led by the Petronas chairman — the same entity that benefits from higher oil prices — and includes the Economy Minister, who was publicly projecting 4-4.5% growth just days ago as if the region wasn’t on fire. Fahmi’s announcement came on the same day as a cascade of other Cabinet decisions: banning Raya open houses for ‘fiscal discipline’ while approving RM15 billion in cash handouts, greenlighting a 5,000-acre GLC megaproject, and announcing RM500 bonuses for civil servants and mosque staff. The committee is the government’s way of looking responsive without being responsive. It is a press release pretending to be policy.

📜 Previous Cases

Malaysia has a long and inglorious history of governance-by-committee. Anwar’s administration has appointed special committees for diesel smuggling, MACC reform, the Azam Baki shareholding issue, and now the economic impact of a war. The pattern is always the same: announce the committee, generate a headline, then quietly let the findings gather dust or punt the decision to someone else — as happened this same week when the Azam Baki committee report was handed to the Chief Secretary with no public disclosure. The committee reflex is not unique to PH, but PH was supposed to be different. They promised responsive, transparent, and accountable governance. Instead they’ve perfected the art of the bureaucratic stall.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised reformasi. You promised a government that acts, not one that studies the problem while the rakyat burns through their savings at the pump. Two weeks into the worst geopolitical energy crisis since the Gulf War, your response is a committee that hasn’t met yet, a report that hasn’t been written, and a special Cabinet meeting that hasn’t happened. The rakyat doesn’t need a committee to tell them diesel is more expensive. They need a government that acts like it understands urgency. Every day you ‘assess,’ someone skips a meal, absorbs a delivery surcharge, or watches their margins collapse. Stop studying the fire. Put it out.

Sources: Malay Mail | The Star | Borneo Post

item.title: Fahmi Says Cabinet Wants Stricter 3R Laws — PH Promised to Repeal Restrictive Speech Laws, Now They're Writing New Ones
item.description:
Said by: Fahmi Fadzil, Communications Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

On March 11, 2026, Communications Minister and government spokesperson Fahmi Fadzil announced that Cabinet has decided firm action must be taken on 3R issues — race, religion, and royalty — including on social media. Fahmi said the government is considering amending existing laws or introducing new legislation to close what it describes as loopholes that allow certain individuals to ignore enforcement measures. He said the Attorney General has been directed to act decisively, and that the review would examine gaps in current laws that prevent early enforcement actions such as detentions.

🔥 Why It Matters

Pakatan Harapan’s 2018 manifesto explicitly pledged to repeal or reform the Sedition Act 1948, the Printing Presses and Publications Act, and Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act — the very tools BN used for decades to police speech on race, religion, and royalty. Eight years later, PH has not repealed a single one. Instead, Fahmi is now announcing the government wants to write new restrictive laws on the same topics. This is not reform. This is a government that went from promising to dismantle the censorship apparatus to expanding it — while using the language of ‘harmony’ and ’national unity’ as cover.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Every authoritarian Malaysian government has used 3R as the justification for silencing critics. Mahathir did it. Najib did it. Now Anwar’s government is doing it — in the same week it’s using Section 124B to interrogate anti-corruption activists like C4’s CEO, and in the same month it invoked a ‘Zionist plot’ narrative to justify investigating civil society. The ’loopholes’ Fahmi wants to close are not loopholes — they are what remains of free expression in a system already stacked with repressive laws. When PH says ‘stricter enforcement,’ they don’t mean protecting minorities. They mean giving the state more power to detain first and ask questions later.

📜 Previous Cases

DAP’s own Ramkarpal Singh questioned the continued use of the Sedition Act as recently as January 2026, noting it contradicts PH’s reform agenda. In 2018, Lim Guan Eng called the Sedition Act ‘draconian.’ In opposition, Fahmi himself criticised BN’s use of speech laws to silence dissent. The Sedition Act remains on the books. The Printing Presses Act remains on the books. SOSMA remains on the books. Not one has been repealed. And now, instead of delivering on those promises, PH is proposing to add to the arsenal. The trajectory is clear: from reformers to enforcers in one parliamentary term.

🎯 PH Callout

You told Malaysians you would tear down the walls BN built around free speech. Instead, you’re adding barbed wire. If you cannot handle criticism about race, religion, and royalty without reaching for new detention powers, you have become the thing you said you were fighting. Every new law you pass to silence 3R speech will be inherited by the next government — including the ones you call extremists. Think about that before you hand them a sharpened tool. Your manifesto said repeal. The rakyat is still waiting.

Sources: Malay Mail

item.title: KPDN Scrambles on Fuel Smuggling After Oil Tops $100 — Armizan Promises Enforcement That Should Have Been in Place for Years
item.description:
Said by: Armizan Mohd Ali, Minister of Domestic Trade and Cost of Living (KPDN)

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

On March 10–11, KPDN Minister Armizan Mohd Ali announced that his ministry would “strengthen enforcement” against fuel smuggling and intensify Ops Tiris operations at national borders, after global oil prices topped US$100 per barrel due to the escalating US-Israel-Iran conflict. Armizan acknowledged that the widening gap between Malaysia’s subsidised fuel prices (RON95 at RM1.99) and market prices in neighbouring countries creates “high risk” of smuggling. He told the public there was “no need for concern or panic buying” and claimed contingency plans were in place. Separately, NST reported that Anwar appointed Deputy Prime Minister Fadillah Yusof to chair a special enforcement committee specifically targeting diesel smuggling (single-source: NST).

🔥 Why It Matters

Malaysia has known about fuel smuggling for years. The government restructured diesel subsidies in 2024 partly because of cross-border leakage. Op Tiris has been running since at least 2023. Yet when the biggest energy crisis in a decade hits — with oil past $100 and the Strait of Hormuz under threat — the government’s response is to announce that it will “strengthen” enforcement it should have had in place all along, and form yet another committee. This is reactive governance dressed up as decisive action. The gap between Malaysia’s RM1.99 RON95 and unsubsidised market prices of RM2.67+ makes Malaysia a fuel smuggling magnet, and every week of delay costs the rakyat.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Armizan’s reassurance that supply is “sufficient” and that “contingency plans are in place” is the same script every KPDN minister reads when things go sideways. No contingency plan has ever been made public. No enforcement statistics on Op Tiris outcomes have been shared. And the formation of a special committee chaired by the DPM — on a problem that already has an existing task force — is textbook bureaucratic theatre. If your enforcement was adequate, you wouldn’t need a new committee every time oil prices spike. The government is spending more time announcing what it will do than actually doing it.

📜 Previous Cases

This pattern goes back decades. In 2022–2023, diesel smuggling across the Thai and Singapore borders was so rampant that KPDN itself admitted losses of hundreds of millions of ringgit annually. The targeted diesel subsidy implemented in June 2024 was supposed to solve the problem — but Armizan’s own admission that “the risk of smuggling remains high” proves it didn’t. In 2025, Anwar boasted about saving RM4 billion in subsidy leakage — a claim that was never independently verified. Now, with oil at $100+ and the subsidy gap wider than ever, the same enforcement gaps are back, and the government’s playbook hasn’t changed.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised subsidy reform would be smart, targeted, and leak-proof. It’s none of those things. You’ve had three years to build border enforcement infrastructure, digitise fuel distribution tracking, and close the smuggling loopholes that every analyst warned you about. Instead, the moment a crisis hits, you scramble to announce “strengthened enforcement” that should already exist and form a committee that duplicates existing task forces. Armizan telling Malaysians “don’t panic buy” while admitting smuggling risk is “high” is not reassurance — it’s a contradiction. Stop forming committees. Start governing.

Sources: The Sun Malaysia | The Vibes | NST | SCMP | Straits Times

item.title: Mohamad Hasan Says Travel Agencies Must Bring Home Stranded Umrah Pilgrims — 'It Cannot Be Left to the Government Alone'
item.description:
Said by: Mohamad Hasan, Foreign Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

Foreign Minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan, speaking at a press conference in Putrajaya on March 11, declared that travel companies and agencies managing umrah packages must take full responsibility for bringing home pilgrims stranded by the West Asia conflict. He said airlines must also honour their tickets: ‘If the ticket is with Malaysia Airlines, MAS must bring them home. If it is an AirAsia ticket, AirAsia must do so.’ He added bluntly: ‘It cannot be left to the Malaysian government alone.’ This came the same day the government’s first evacuation flight — a chartered Malaysia Airlines A330-300 — landed at KLIA with just 163 Malaysians from Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Jordan and Syria, after days of airspace closures and flight disruptions across the Gulf.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is a textbook case of the government reframing a crisis-response failure as a question of private-sector accountability. On March 3, Mohamad Hasan told the Dewan Negara that the situation in the Gulf was ‘under control’ and there was ’no need for an evacuation plan.’ Eight days later, his ministry was coordinating emergency repatriation flights while hundreds of Malaysians — including umrah pilgrims who had been stranded for three to five days at airports in Dubai, Doha, Abu Dhabi and Bahrain — waited for someone to get them home. The pivot from ’no evacuation needed’ to ’travel agencies must handle it’ is not reassurance — it is damage control dressed as delegation.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

The Foreign Ministry exists precisely for moments like this: when Malaysian citizens are stranded abroad during a conflict and need their government to act. Telling travel agencies and airlines to sort it out is not crisis leadership — it is passing the bill. Many of these pilgrims are elderly, travelling on budget packages, and do not have the leverage to demand rescheduled flights from airlines whose operations are in chaos. The government evacuated 163 people on one chartered flight and then turned to the microphone to say, essentially, ’the rest of you are someone else’s problem.’ Meanwhile, the National Security Council coordinated the evacuation under PM Anwar’s directive — so the government clearly has the machinery. It just chose to limit its use and outsource the rest.

📜 Previous Cases

Malaysia has a long history of slow or inadequate evacuation responses. During the 2023 Sudan crisis, Malaysians were stranded for days before Wisma Putra mobilised. During COVID-19, repatriation flights were chaotic, with competing agencies and unclear responsibility chains leaving citizens in limbo. The playbook is familiar: delay, then deflect. Mohamad Hasan’s ‘under control’ assurance on March 3 echoes every Malaysian foreign minister who has ever downplayed a developing crisis until it became undeniable — then blamed someone else for the gaps.

🎯 PH Callout

PH came to power promising a government that puts rakyat first — ‘Kerajaan Madani,’ a compassionate, responsive administration. When your citizens are sleeping on airport floors in the Gulf because airspace is shut and missiles are flying, telling travel agencies to figure it out is not compassion. It is bureaucratic cowardice. Evacuate your people first. Sort out who pays later. That is what a government that means its slogans would do.

Sources: NST | The Star | The Sun | FMT (March 3 statement)

item.title: Oil Past $100, Strait of Hormuz Shut, Rakyat Burning Fuel to Commute — Anwar Won't Mandate WFH, Freeze Tolls, Cap Food Prices, or Cut GLC Fat
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

The Iran-US war has pushed global oil past $100 per barrel, effectively shut down the Strait of Hormuz — through which nearly 80% of Asia’s oil transits — and sent Malaysia’s FBM KLCI plunging 2.55% in a single session on March 9. RON97 is up to RM3.25, diesel to RM3.12, and Anwar himself admitted on March 7 that the government can only hold RON95 at RM1.99 for ‘one or two more months.’ Freight costs are surging, food prices are next, and experts from NST to Hong Leong IB are warning of imported inflation hitting the rakyat’s kitchen table. Anwar’s response so far: a strongly worded tweet about solidarity, an emergency parliamentary motion condemning Israel, and Zafrul saying Malaysia is ‘prepared’ — without naming a single concrete domestic measure to protect workers and families from the economic fallout.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is not a theoretical crisis. Malaysians are already paying more at the pump for RON97 and diesel. Freight costs feed directly into food, medicine, and essential goods. The stock market is bleeding. And yet the Prime Minister — who is also the Finance Minister — has not announced a single emergency domestic economic measure. No crisis-mode intervention. No relief. Just speeches about geopolitics while the rakyat’s cost of living climbs in real time. Malaysia proved during COVID that the government can act decisively on domestic economic protection when it wants to. The difference is political will — and right now, Anwar’s will is directed at Parliament speeches about Zionist plots, not at shielding the people he governs.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Four things Anwar could do tomorrow with existing executive power — and won’t:

1. Mandate work-from-home for all WFH-compatible jobs. Malaysia already has a Flexible Work Arrangement framework under the Employment Act. During COVID, the government mandated WFH and it worked. With oil past $100 and commuters burning RM300-500/month on fuel and tolls, a temporary WFH directive for office-based workers would immediately cut household fuel costs, reduce demand on subsidised petrol, and ease the fiscal pressure Anwar says he can only hold for two months. Instead, millions of KL workers sit in traffic burning fuel the government can barely afford to subsidise.

2. Freeze or waive highway tolls during the crisis. The government already broke its toll abolition promise — paying RM591 million just to freeze hikes rather than remove tolls. A temporary toll waiver during a war-driven energy crisis is basic economic relief. Singapore waived ERP during COVID disruptions. Malaysia won’t even pause toll collection while fuel prices spike.

3. Impose emergency price controls on essential food items. Freight costs from Hormuz disruptions are already feeding into import prices. Cooking oil, flour, rice, and protein prices will follow. The Price Control and Anti-Profiteering Act 2011 gives the government power to gazette maximum prices for essential goods. Anwar used this power selectively before. Now, when a global war is about to hit Malaysian dinner tables, silence.

4. Freeze non-essential GLC and political appointment spending. The IDEAS report found 238 political appointments to FSBs and GLCs under Anwar. Every one of those comes with salaries, allowances, and perks funded by public money. During a war-driven fiscal crunch — with the subsidy bill ballooning and oil revenue uncertain — a responsible government would freeze all discretionary patronage spending and redirect it to rakyat relief. Instead, the appointments continue.

📜 Previous Cases

During COVID-19, the Muhyiddin government — for all its democratic illegitimacy — implemented a blanket WFH directive, moratoriums on loan repayments, cash handouts (BPN), and wage subsidies within weeks of the crisis hitting. The Ismail Sabri government continued with hybrid WFH arrangements and extended moratoriums. These were imperfect governments acting under crisis pressure. Anwar, who controls both the PM’s office and the Finance Ministry, has more concentrated fiscal power than either predecessor — and has used less of it in response to a war that is already hitting Malaysian wallets harder than the early weeks of COVID hit fuel prices. The precedent exists. The tools exist. The power exists. The will does not.

🎯 PH Callout

You told us you were different. You told us Madani meant the people come first. You told us having the PM also serve as Finance Minister would mean faster, more decisive economic management. So where is it? Oil is past $100. The Strait of Hormuz is disrupted. Your own deputy economy minister admits growth projections are shaky. Your own mouth admitted the RON95 subsidy has weeks left. And your crisis response is… a parliamentary motion about Iran and a tweet about solidarity? Mandate WFH. Freeze tolls. Cap food prices. Cut the GLC fat. You have the power. You have the precedent. You have the tools. Use them — or admit that ‘Ekonomi Madani’ was never about protecting the rakyat. It was about protecting the coalition.

Sources: Xinhua — KLCI plunges 2.55% | SAYS — Oil spike impact on Malaysia | The Star — RON95 subsidy 1-2 months | NST — Oil price surge could hit Malaysia | CNBC — Strait of Hormuz impact | Straits Times — Malaysia subsidy bill pressure | Malaysiakini — 238 political appointments | Asia Media Centre — Hormuz buffer

item.title: PH Backbenchers Call for Snap Election Dressed Up as 'Reform' — Hold GE16 With All State Polls, Save Money, They Say
item.description:
Said by: Azli Yusof (Amanah-Shah Alam) / Jimmy Puah (PKR-Tebrau)

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

On March 11, two Pakatan Harapan backbenchers — Shah Alam MP Azli Yusof (Amanah) and Tebrau MP Jimmy Puah (PKR) — called for all state assembly elections to be held concurrently with the 16th General Election. Speaking to FMT, both MPs argued that separate elections lead to lower voter turnout, waste public money, and produce state governments with weaker mandates. Azli said separate polls are “unfair to voters, especially those who live far away from where they vote.” Puah called it “simply too tiring for people to come out and vote twice.” Putrajaya spent RM725 million on GE15 in 2022 and a further RM420 million on six subsequent state elections in 2023.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is not a neutral policy suggestion. It is PH’s latest move in a coordinated signal — following DAP secretary-general Anthony Loke’s identical proposal last year and PH Johor chairman Aminolhuda Hassan’s recent push — to align the political calendar for a snap general election. By calling for simultaneous polls, PH backbenchers are doing the government’s messaging work: normalizing the idea of an early dissolution while packaging it as cost-saving reform. The real audience is not voters who are tired of going to polling stations — it is UMNO, GPS, and other coalition partners who need to be brought on board before the PM pulls the trigger.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

PH did not care about “voter fatigue” when it suited them. In 2022, six PH-governed states chose to delay their state elections rather than hold them with GE15, citing monsoon concerns. Now, suddenly, the same coalition wants everything held together — but only because the timing benefits them. The opposition is fractured after the Bersatu-PAS split, the economy is holding, and Anwar’s approval is still intact. This is not reform. This is election engineering with a reformist paint job. If PH were serious about alignment, they would legislate fixed election dates — not send backbenchers to float trial balloons in FMT.

📜 Previous Cases

Malaysia has a long history of strategic election timing. Mahathir called snap elections when conditions favoured him. Najib dissolved Parliament early in 2013 and 2018. Even within the current cycle, the Sabah, Melaka, and Johor snap elections of 2020–2022 were all called for political advantage, not democratic principle. PH itself benefited from the chaos of misaligned polls — the August 2023 state elections became a de facto referendum on the unity government. The pattern is always the same: whoever controls the timing controls the narrative.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised institutional reform. You promised that governance would no longer be about political convenience. Yet here you are, using backbenchers to soften the ground for a snap election while pretending it is about saving the rakyat money. If you actually believe in synchronized elections, table a constitutional amendment to fix election dates — the way mature democracies do. But you will not, because the whole point is to keep the power to dissolve in the PM’s pocket. Stop dressing up electoral strategy as reform. Malaysians can tell the difference.

Sources: FMT — Hold GE16 with all state polls to ensure true representation, say MPs | The Vibes — Simultaneous polls could boost PH but test unity | NST — PH Johor proposes simultaneous elections

item.title: Police Question C4 Anti-Corruption CEO Under Section 124B — Anwar's Government Uses 'Plot to Topple' Probe to Interrogate Civil Society
item.description:
Said by: Bukit Aman (Royal Malaysia Police) / C4 Center (Pushpan Murugiah, CEO)

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

On March 10, 2026, the Center to Combat Corruption and Cronyism (C4) CEO Pushpan Murugiah was summoned to Bukit Aman and questioned for approximately 90 minutes under Section 124B of the Penal Code — activities detrimental to parliamentary democracy. The questioning was linked to the police investigation into an alleged plot to topple the government, reportedly because Pushpan’s name appeared in an email deemed relevant to the probe. But the interrogation went far beyond that email. Police questioned C4’s sources of funding, whether the organisation had received instructions to issue statements, and — remarkably — challenged C4’s right to comment on matters of national governance. C4 confirmed that several other political commentators and academics had also been summoned to Bukit Aman under the same investigation.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is not a routine police inquiry. This is the machinery of state — operating under a government that came to power on a reform mandate — being deployed to scrutinise, pressure, and delegitimise an independent anti-corruption watchdog. Section 124B is a serious charge. Using it to question a civil society leader about their organisation’s funding, autonomy, and standing to comment on public affairs is a textbook chilling effect. It signals to every NGO, academic, and political commentator in Malaysia: criticise the government’s handling of the MACC scandal, and you may find yourself in a police interview room explaining why you think you have the right to speak. This is the same playbook that Malaysians rejected when BN did it. The fact that PH is now running it should alarm every voter who believed the reformasi promise.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Anwar Ibrahim built his entire political career on the claim that he was persecuted by a government that weaponised institutions against critics. He spent years in prison. He rallied millions with the promise that under PH, the police would not be used to silence dissent. Now his government is hauling in the CEO of an anti-corruption NGO and asking — with a straight face — whether C4 has the standing to comment on corruption. The hypocrisy is breathtaking. C4 has been non-partisan since 2014. It criticised BN, it criticised PN, and now it criticises PH — and this is the administration that sends the police. The “plot to topple the government” narrative has become a catch-all justification for intimidating anyone who asks uncomfortable questions about Azam Baki, the MACC, and the corporate mafia allegations. If your anti-corruption credentials can’t survive scrutiny from an anti-corruption NGO, the problem is not the NGO.

📜 Previous Cases

This pattern is not new in Malaysian politics — it is simply new for PH. Under Najib, civil society leaders and journalists were routinely summoned under the Sedition Act, SOSMA, and the Communications and Multimedia Act for criticising 1MDB and government corruption. Under Muhyiddin, the Emergency Ordinance was used to suspend Parliament and silence dissent. Anwar himself was the most prominent victim of institutional weaponisation — twice jailed on charges widely seen as politically motivated. Now, under his own government, Section 124B is being stretched to cover the activities of NGOs whose only offence is demanding accountability from the MACC. Bersih was told to stop criticising MACC “during Ramadan.” DAP’s own youth wing warned of a “climate of fear.” And now C4’s CEO is sitting in Bukit Aman explaining why an anti-corruption organisation dares to talk about corruption.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised reformasi. You promised institutional independence. You promised that the police would never again be used as a political weapon. Now your police are questioning the CEO of an anti-corruption NGO about their right to speak — under the same “plot to topple” investigation that you’ve used to smear Daim’s family, attack Bloomberg, and deflect from the Azam Baki scandal. Every hour that Anwar’s government spends intimidating civil society instead of reforming the MACC is another hour that proves the Madani brand is hollow. If you want Malaysians to believe in your reform agenda, start by not behaving exactly like the governments you replaced.

Sources: FMT | BFM News

item.title: Sabah Rejected PH at the Ballot Box — Now Mustapha Sakmud Says He'll Be Their Voice in Putrajaya Anyway
item.description:
Said by: Mustapha Sakmud, Sabah PH Chairman / Sabah and Sarawak Affairs Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

On March 11, 2026, Sabah PH chairman and Sabah and Sarawak Affairs Minister Mustapha Sakmud told FMT that Sabah PH will serve as a “conduit” to ensure state issues are voiced at the federal level. He said he intends to leverage his ministerial appointment — made on February 15 — to bring Sabahan concerns to Cabinet meetings and government agencies. “This is the role we aim to play, and we hope the people of Sabah will appreciate it when their fundamental needs are addressed,” he said.

🔥 Why It Matters

PH was annihilated in the November 29, 2025 Sabah state election. PKR scraped through with a single seat. DAP won nothing. GRS took 29 seats, Warisan took 25, and Sabahans made it abundantly clear they did not want PH representing them at the state level. Yet barely three months later, Anwar handed Mustapha a federal ministerial portfolio — Sabah and Sarawak Affairs — effectively using the federal Cabinet to insert PH into Sabah’s political space through the back door. This is not democratic representation. This is patronage as a substitute for a mandate.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

You cannot lose an election this badly and then claim you are the people’s voice. PH won one seat in the entire Sabah state assembly. That is not a conduit — that is a rejection letter. Mustapha’s argument boils down to: “Sabahans didn’t vote for us, but we got a Cabinet post, so we’ll represent them anyway.” This is the exact kind of unearned political entitlement PH once accused BN of practising. The appointment rewards loyalty to Anwar, not service to Sabah. If Sabahans wanted PH to speak for them, they would have voted for PH. They didn’t.

📜 Previous Cases

This follows a long Malaysian tradition of using federal appointments to compensate for electoral losses at the state level. BN did it for decades in Sabah and Sarawak — parachuting federal figures into state politics through GLCs, agencies, and development corridors. PH, in opposition, railed against this exact practice. Now in government, they’ve adopted the same playbook. Anwar’s December 2025 Cabinet reshuffle — which created the Sabah and Sarawak Affairs portfolio — was widely seen as a damage-control exercise after PH’s Sabah wipeout. The pattern is clear: when voters reject you, buy your way back in through Putrajaya.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised reformasi. You promised that power would come from the people, not from Putrajaya. You told Sabahans for years that BN was using federal patronage to override their democratic choices. And now you’ve handed a Cabinet seat to a man whose coalition won one seat in an entire state election and told him to go represent the people who rejected him. If “the people of Sabah will appreciate it,” as Mustapha claims, then put it to a test — contest a by-election in Sabah on PH’s record. Otherwise, stop pretending a ministerial appointment is a mandate.

Sources: FMT — Sabah PH will bridge state needs with national policies, says Mustapha

item.title: 99-Year-Old Kampung Lee Kong Chian Residents Told to Get Out — RM50K Per Family, Hannah Yeoh 'Unable to Attend'
item.description:
Said by: Lo Su Fui, Deputy Minister (Federal Territories) / Hannah Yeoh, Minister (Federal Territories)

Category: Housing

Date: 2026-03-10

📌 What Happened

More than 150 residents of Kampung Lee Kong Chian in Setapak — Kuala Lumpur’s sole surviving private Chinese village, established in 1927 as quarters for Lee Rubber Group factory workers — held a peaceful rally on 8 March 2026 after 146 households received eviction notices in November 2025 ordering them to vacate by September. The land has been sold to a private company. Residents were offered RM50,000 per household in compensation, which they rejected, demanding People’s Housing Project (PPR) units instead. Federal Territories Minister Hannah Yeoh was absent from the village’s final Chap Goh Meh celebration on 10 March; Deputy Minister Lo Su Fui showed up, handed over RM10,000 for the dinner, and said he would “bring this matter back to the Federal Territories Department to explore how we can best provide assistance.” The government’s official position, per NST, is that it will “review the situation before deciding whether to intervene.”

🔥 Why It Matters

This is a textbook case of urban displacement dressed up as market transaction. Kampung Lee Kong Chian is not a squatter settlement — village chief Tan Lai Kee stressed the houses were built with formal approval from the original landowner. These are 230 households, many of them descendants of workers who built their lives around the Lee Rubber factory nearly a century ago. When the government allows a private sale to erase a community of over 1,000 people with six months’ notice and a RM50,000 cheque — roughly the price of a used Myvi — it tells you exactly where ordinary Malaysians sit in the hierarchy of development priorities. The Federal Territories Ministry, under PH’s Hannah Yeoh, is the body with direct jurisdiction over KL housing and land disputes. Yet the minister was absent, and the government’s posture is to “review” and “explore” — the bureaucratic language of stalling while the clock runs out on residents.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

PH campaigned as the coalition that would protect communities from rapacious developers and crony capitalism. Anwar himself has spoken repeatedly about housing affordability, people-first governance, and the dignity of the rakyat. So when a 99-year-old village — with documented, legitimate housing built under formal approval — is handed a six-month eviction notice and a compensation offer that wouldn’t cover a deposit on a flat in Setapak, where is the reformist government? Hannah Yeoh sent a deputy with a RM10,000 dinner cheque. The deputy said he’d “bring this back” to the department. That’s not governance — that’s a holding pattern. If PH cannot protect a century-old KL community with clear historical legitimacy from being bulldozed by a private developer, then every “people first” slogan they’ve ever uttered is decoration.

📜 Previous Cases

This follows the same pattern as the Kampung Sungai Baru evictions in 2025, where residents were forced out amid redevelopment pressure with inadequate compensation and minimal government intervention. KL’s Kampung Baru — the last Malay enclave in the city centre — has faced decades of similar redevelopment threats, with successive governments oscillating between grand masterplans and inaction. The PH government has also pushed Nga Kor Ming’s Urban Renewal Bill, which would grant sweeping ministerial powers over property acquisition — a bill so controversial that even PH’s own backbenchers revolted. The pattern is consistent: communities with deep roots but no political leverage get treated as obstacles to development, while the government oscillates between performative sympathy and institutional passivity.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised Malaysia Madani — a civilised, compassionate Malaysia. A 99-year-old village with 230 families, descendants of workers who built this city, is being told to leave with RM50,000 and a handshake. Your Federal Territories Minister didn’t even show up to the last Chap Goh Meh these families will ever celebrate in their homes. You sent a deputy with dinner money. If this is what “reviewing the situation” looks like, then your review is a eulogy. These residents are not asking for handouts — they’re asking for replacement housing under PPR, a programme your own government administers. Either use the power you have to intervene, or stop pretending you govern for the people.

Sources: The Star — Setapak Village Marks ‘Farewell’ Chap Goh Meh | NST — Over 150 Residents Rally Against Gombak Eviction Plan | NST — Govt to Review Gombak Eviction Dispute | BFM News (Threads)

item.title: DAP Finally Demands RCI on MACC — Weeks Late, and PAS Correctly Calls It Political Theatre
item.description:
Said by: DAP Leadership / Ahmad Fadhli Shaari, PAS Information Chief

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-10

📌 What Happened

On March 10, DAP — the largest party in the Pakatan Harapan coalition with 40 parliamentary seats — formally called for a Royal Commission of Inquiry (RCI) to investigate the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission over allegations first reported by Bloomberg, including that MACC chief Azam Baki breached civil servant shareholding limits and that the agency was involved in corporate intimidation. PAS information chief Ahmad Fadhli Shaari responded by calling the demand “political theatre,” noting that DAP had remained silent when the same Bloomberg reports alleged that Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim personally interfered in MACC investigations.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is the clearest example yet of DAP’s credibility gap on institutional reform. The party spent weeks after the Bloomberg exposé refusing to directly challenge Anwar’s handling of Azam Baki, even as eight PKR MPs led by Rafizi Ramli publicly demanded Azam’s suspension in mid-February. DAP’s belated RCI call comes only after its catastrophic Sabah election loss and growing voter fury — not from genuine conviction. When even PAS can accurately identify your reform posture as performative, something has gone deeply wrong with your party’s moral compass.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

DAP had the numbers to force the issue from day one. Forty seats. The largest bloc in government. Instead, they sat on their hands for weeks while Bloomberg’s reporting exposed potential corruption at the highest levels of the anti-graft agency — and Anwar’s alleged interference in it. Now they want credit for demanding an RCI? That’s not accountability. That’s damage control dressed up as principle. And the most damning part: DAP still hasn’t addressed the Bloomberg allegation that their own Prime Minister interfered in MACC affairs. They’re demanding an investigation into everyone except the man they answer to.

📜 Previous Cases

This pattern is pure DAP under the unity government. After the Sabah election wipeout in November 2025, DAP pledged to “accelerate the reform agenda over the next six months.” Months later, the PM tenure limit bill failed partly because government MPs were absent, and DAP’s response was to blame the opposition rather than hold their own coalition accountable. On the Azam Baki shareholding scandal — which first surfaced in 2021 when DAP was in opposition and demanded action — DAP is now the party in government protecting the same man they once wanted removed. The pattern is clear: loud in opposition, silent in power.

🎯 PH Callout

DAP, you promised reformasi. You promised institutional accountability. You promised you were different. But when Bloomberg dropped the biggest anti-corruption story in Malaysian politics since 1MDB, your first instinct was to protect the Prime Minister, not the public. An RCI demand that comes weeks late and deliberately avoids the PM’s role isn’t reform — it’s theatre. If you want to prove PAS wrong, start by addressing the Bloomberg allegations about Anwar’s interference in MACC. Until then, Ahmad Fadhli is right: you are not qualified to champion this.

Sources: FMT | The Edge Malaysia | Reuters | US News / Reuters

item.title: Dzulkefly Promises — Again — to Crack Down on Workplace Bullying in Government Hospitals
item.description:
Said by: Dzulkefly Ahmad, Health Minister

Category: Health

Date: 2026-03-10

📌 What Happened

Health Minister Dzulkefly Ahmad announced on March 10, 2026 that the Health Ministry will “bolster enforcement” against workplace bullying in government health facilities. He said public hospitals should be “professional and ethical workplace environments” and pointed to a Public Services Department (PSD) circular that bans coarse language and verbal abuse in the workplace. He pledged to “enhance implementation” of the circular to ensure “no ambiguity” in handling misconduct — language that strongly implies the existing rules are not being enforced.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is not a new announcement. Dzulkefly has been making near-identical promises since at least August 2025, when the Health Ministry recorded 430 bullying complaints with only four under investigation. He launched “zero tolerance” pledges, announced new guidelines classifying verbal threats and mockery as bullying (due in October 2025), and even threatened to fire senior officials who conceal cases. Months later, he is back at the podium saying the same things — which tells you everything about whether the previous rounds of promises produced results. Housemen and junior doctors continue to report systemic harassment, and the ministry’s response remains performative announcements rather than structural accountability.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

When a minister announces the same policy three times in under a year, the announcement is the policy — not the enforcement. Dzulkefly’s playbook is textbook PH governance: identify a real problem, hold a press conference, issue a circular, then do nothing until the next round of bad press forces another press conference. The circular he cited already exists. The guidelines were already launched. The zero-tolerance stance was already declared. If any of those measures had worked, he would not be standing up in March 2026 promising to “enhance implementation.” The problem is not ambiguity in the rules — it is impunity for senior medical staff who bully juniors, and a ministry that treats announcements as action.

📜 Previous Cases

In August 2025, the same minister made headlines for declaring zero tolerance on bullying after the case of “Dr S,” a houseman in Sabah allegedly bullied so severely she was confined to her room for years while the perpetrator moved abroad to pursue specialist training. In July 2025, the ministry announced new guidelines classifying insults, threats, and mockery as workplace bullying — guidelines supposedly launching in October 2025. Pressure group Hartal Doktor Kontrak has repeatedly called for systemic reform, not just guidelines, pointing to research showing high prevalence of bullying among Malaysian junior doctors across multiple government hospitals.

🎯 PH Callout

Anwar’s Madani government promised institutional reform and a government that works for the people. Dzulkefly is a senior PH minister in a critical portfolio, and the best he can offer after three years in power is re-announcing the same circular and calling it progress. Junior doctors — the backbone of Malaysia’s public health system — deserve more than recycled press releases. Either enforce the rules you already have, fire the people responsible, and publish the outcomes — or stop pretending the announcements mean anything.

Sources: Free Malaysia Today | The Malaysian Reserve (Aug 2025) | The Sun

item.title: Rafizi Quietly Drops NFCorp Bankruptcy Bid — The Anti-Corruption Crusader Makes Nice With the People He Once Exposed
item.description:
Said by: Rafizi Ramli, Pandan MP / Former Economy Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-10

📌 What Happened

Rafizi Ramli, the Pandan MP and former Economy Minister who built his political career on exposing the National Feedlot Corporation (NFCorp) scandal, has quietly dropped his bankruptcy and winding-up proceedings against NFCorp director Datuk Seri Dr Mohamad Salleh Ismail and the company itself. A Notice of Discontinuance was filed on March 3, and the High Court struck out the case on March 10 without an order as to costs. Rafizi’s lawyer Razlan Hadri Zulkifli confirmed the matter was “amicably resolved.” The case stemmed from court-ordered costs owed to Rafizi after the Federal Court upheld his appeal in April 2022 — NFCorp and Salleh had paid RM300,000 in December 2022 but stopped paying the outstanding interest balance.

🔥 Why It Matters

Rafizi’s entire political brand was built on the NFCorp exposé. In 2012, he blew the lid off a RM250 million government soft loan meant for a national cattle-rearing project that instead went toward luxury condominiums and personal expenses — a scandal that brought down UMNO Wanita chief Shahrizat Abdul Jalil and became a symbol of BN-era corruption. He was charged under the Official Secrets Act for it. He went to jail for it. The courts vindicated him all the way to the Federal Court. And now, in March 2026, with his own MACC investigation hanging over his head over the RM1.11 billion ARM Holdings chip deal, Rafizi quietly makes nice with the very people he once dragged through the courts. The timing is impossible to ignore.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

This is the man who told Malaysians he would never compromise on accountability. The man who said corruption must be pursued relentlessly, regardless of political cost. And now he’s walking away from money the courts said he was owed — not because he’s being magnanimous, but because the political calculus has changed. Rafizi is under MACC investigation. His former aide has fled to the UK. His political position within PKR is weakened after being dropped from Cabinet. When you’re no longer in a position of strength, you don’t pick fights — you settle. That’s not principle. That’s survival.

📜 Previous Cases

Rafizi has a pattern of selective aggression. He was relentless against BN-era scandals — NFCorp, 1MDB, Tabung Haji — when PH was in opposition. But once PH came to power, the fire dimmed. As Economy Minister, he championed the Central Database Hub (PADU) that drew criticism for data privacy concerns, and oversaw investment policies that favoured headline numbers over structural reform. His recent MACC trouble over the ARM Holdings chip deal, where he allegedly facilitated a RM1.11 billion investment in a company with questionable fundamentals, shows a man who has drifted far from his whistleblower roots. The NFCorp settlement is just the latest chapter in a long arc from crusader to career politician.

🎯 PH Callout
