The answer matters because the government is using the Daim case to justify expanded national security rhetoric, deflect from the Azam Baki scandal, and paint critics as foreign agents. If PKR’s own lobbying was legitimate, then the same standard must apply to others.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

Sivarasa’s defence — “we were trying to free a political prisoner” — is convenient but doesn’t hold up under scrutiny. The 84-page dossier didn’t just ask for Anwar’s release. It made sweeping allegations against Najib and explicitly sought US intervention in Malaysian domestic politics. That’s foreign lobbying. Period.

When PKR does it, it’s “advocacy.” When Daim’s family allegedly does it, it’s a “Zionist conspiracy” and a national security threat. The double standard is so blatant that even a casual observer can see through it.

Anwar’s government is essentially saying: foreign lobbying is patriotic when we do it, and treasonous when our opponents do it. That’s not a principle — it’s a convenience.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

Malaysian political parties have a long history of international lobbying. UMNO maintained Washington lobbyists for decades. Anwar himself cultivated deep relationships with US policymakers, think tanks, and media during his years in opposition. None of this was considered problematic when PH was the beneficiary.

The selective outrage follows the same pattern as PH’s treatment of press freedom (Bloomberg good when exposing Najib, Bloomberg bad when exposing Azam Baki) and institutional accountability (MACC is weaponised under BN, MACC is independent under PH).

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You hired a US firm to lobby Washington against a sitting Malaysian PM, produced an 84-page dossier of allegations, and sought American intervention in Malaysian politics. Your own former vice-president confirmed it. Now you’re telling Malaysians that when others do the same thing, it’s a Zionist-backed conspiracy to topple the government? Pick a lane. Either foreign lobbying is a legitimate part of political advocacy — in which case stop criminalising your opponents for it — or it’s a national security threat, in which case PKR’s own leaders should be investigated too. You can’t have it both ways.

Sources: MalaysiaNow | MalaysiaNow - Dossier | The Star | Malaysiakini | Malaysiakini - Sivarasa

item.title: Anwar Is Running Mahathir's Playbook 2.0 — Different Man, Same Authoritarian Instincts
item.description:
Said by: Political Analysts / ISEAS / Toda Peace Institute / CSIS

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Multiple independent analyses from ISEAS, CSIS, Toda Peace Institute, and East Asia Forum converge on the same conclusion: Anwar Ibrahim’s governance style increasingly mirrors that of Mahathir Mohamad — the man who imprisoned him. Centralisation of power (PM+FM), use of repressive laws, crackdown on media, selective prosecution, race-based politics, foreign policy grandstanding as domestic distraction, and coalition management through patronage.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Anwar spent his political life as the ANTI-Mahathir. His entire narrative was: I suffered under Mahathir’s authoritarian system, I understand its evils, and I will build something different. The convergence between Anwar’s governing style and Mahathir’s is not just ironic — it’s the final proof that Malaysian political culture, rather than individual leaders, produces authoritarian governance. Changing the face at the top doesn’t change the system.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Repressive laws? Mahathir had ISA, Anwar has SOSMA and the Sedition Act. Media control? Mahathir had PPPA, Anwar has MCMC blocks and journalist codes of ethics. Patronage? Mahathir had cronies, Anwar has 47.5 GLC appointees/year. Race politics? Mahathir had NEP, Anwar has ’non-negotiable’ Bumiputera policies. Concentration of power? Mahathir held PM+FM, Anwar holds PM+FM. Foreign policy preening? Mahathir had ‘Asian values,’ Anwar has Palestine speeches. The checklist is identical.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysian democracy has a pattern: opposition leaders promise reform, win power, then govern like their predecessors. Mahathir in 1981 promised reform, then built the system Anwar fought against. Anwar in 2022 promised to dismantle that system, then adopted it wholesale. The CSIS analysis titled it perfectly: ‘The Death of Reformasi.’ Not because the movement failed from outside — but because its leader killed it from within.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You are Mahathir 2.0 with better English and a more sympathetic backstory. The policies are the same. The power structures are the same. The patronage is the same. The authoritarianism is the same. The only difference is you KNOW it’s wrong because you spent 11 years in prison because of it. And you chose to do it anyway. That’s not just hypocrisy — it’s a betrayal of everyone who believed you were different.

Sources: CSIS — The Death of Reformasi | Toda Peace Institute — Report 184 | East Asia Forum — Reform Aspirations

item.title: The Scoreboard: Things That Were BETTER Under BN Than PH — An Uncomfortable Truth
item.description:
Said by: Comparative Economic Data / Various Sources

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: By multiple measurable metrics, ordinary Malaysians were objectively better off under certain BN policies than under PH: (1) GST was more efficient and less regressive than SST; (2) Diesel was RM2.15 vs RM3.35; (3) Festive toll-free periods existed; (4) PISA scores were 31 points higher in math; (5) Food prices were lower; (6) RON95 subsidy was universal without the anxiety of ’targeted’ reform; (7) Electricity for domestic use was GST-exempt.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The fundamental promise of changing government is that things will get BETTER, not worse. When multiple cost-of-living and quality-of-life indicators are worse under PH than under the government they replaced, the entire rationale for PH’s existence comes into question. Voters didn’t march for Reformasi so they could pay more for diesel.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH will argue context — global inflation, inherited debt, COVID aftermath. These are real factors. But they don’t explain why Malaysia’s food prices are the HIGHEST in Southeast Asia (surpassing richer Singapore), why PISA decline was DOUBLE the OECD average, or why diesel had to rise 56% in one shot while JAKIM kept RM2.6 billion. Context explains some decline; it doesn’t excuse all of it. Choices were made, and those choices made life worse.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: BN was voted out for corruption, abuse of power, and 1MDB — all legitimate reasons. But the implicit promise was that PH would maintain or improve the baseline while cleaning up governance. Instead, governance remains corrupt (Shamsul Iskandar, MACC scandal, GLC patronage) AND daily life costs more. You got the worst of both worlds: BN-level corruption with higher costs.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Here’s your report card. Diesel: WORSE. Food prices: WORSE. Education outcomes: WORSE. Press freedom: SAME or WORSE. Corruption: SAME. Cost of living: WORSE. Toll abolition: BROKEN. EPF protection: WEAKER. Healthcare: WORSE. The one metric where you’re better? PR and speechwriting. Congratulations — your press conferences are excellent. Too bad Malaysians can’t eat press conferences.

Sources: Paul Tan — RON95 Subsidy Analysis | ISIS Malaysia — PISA Score Decline

item.title: Azam Baki's RM100M Defamation Suit Against Bloomberg — MACC Chief Fights Media Instead of Corruption
item.description:
Said by: Bloomberg / Azam Baki, MACC Chief Commissioner

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-02-27

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 27 February 2026, it was reported that MACC Chief Commissioner Azam Baki filed a RM100 million defamation suit against Bloomberg. The suit came after Bloomberg published an investigation alleging that Azam owned millions of shares in publicly listed companies — potentially breaching civil servant ownership limits. Bloomberg further alleged that MACC officials had colluded with businessmen to orchestrate hostile takeovers of listed firms, using raids and intimidation to force executives into selling shares to groups dubbed the “corporate mafia.” Rather than resigning or submitting to an independent investigation, Azam chose to sue the messenger. Anwar’s response was to announce a task force — not an independent inquiry, not a Royal Commission. Even DAP, his own coalition partner, said a task force “does not go far enough.”

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The head of Malaysia’s anti-corruption agency is suing the world’s most prominent financial news outlet for reporting on his alleged financial misconduct. This is not a case of a private citizen defending their reputation — this is the chief enforcer of anti-corruption law using the legal system to intimidate a media organisation that investigated him. If Azam’s role is to fight corruption, and a credible international outlet alleges he is compromised, the correct institutional response is a transparent, independent investigation — not a defamation suit designed to chill future reporting. The RM100M figure is not about compensation; it is about deterrence. Bloomberg has stood by its reporting. The anti-corruption agency fighting corruption journalism instead of actual corruption is peak Madani irony.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar’s response — a task force — is the bureaucratic equivalent of thoughts and prayers. A task force reports to the government. An independent inquiry reports to the public. Anwar chose the one that protects him. DAP publicly said a task force “does not go far enough,” meaning even Anwar’s own coalition partners don’t trust his anti-corruption mechanisms. CNA reported that Anwar faces a “graft crisis” with “PKR party fractures” and “PH coalition jitters.” The man who built Reformasi on the promise of clean governance now leads a government where the anti-corruption chief sues journalists, the PM’s aide gets charged with bribery, and the DPM’s 47 charges get dropped. At some point, the pattern is the policy.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Azam Baki’s share ownership scandal first surfaced in early 2022, when Malaysiakini reported he held shares in two public-listed companies exceeding the civil service ownership cap. A special parliamentary committee cleared him — prompting widespread outrage. Bloomberg’s 2025-2026 investigation went further, alleging systemic collusion between MACC officials and corporate raiders. The MACC-media adversarial pattern is not new either: the commission has previously threatened legal action against outlets that reported on its internal affairs. Under Azam’s leadership, MACC has become less an anti-corruption body and more a political instrument — selectively prosecuting opposition figures while shielding coalition allies.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You promised MACC independence. Instead, the MACC chief is suing Bloomberg for doing MACC’s job. An independent inquiry — not a task force — is the minimum acceptable response. If Azam is innocent, an inquiry clears him. If he is not, the country’s anti-corruption infrastructure needs to be rebuilt from scratch. Your task force is a stalling mechanism, not an accountability mechanism. Even your own coalition partner sees through it. Do better, or stop pretending Reformasi means anything.

Sources: CNA | SCMP

item.title: Every Cabinet Member Meeting KPIs, Says Anwar — Critics Ask: What KPIs?
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-02-25

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 25 February 2026, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim told Parliament that every cabinet member is meeting their KPIs and performing with integrity. He did not disclose what those KPIs were, how they were measured, or who evaluated them. Anwar was responding to an opposition MP who asked if ministers would be forced to resign for poor performance. His reply: “Accountability is not only about punishment but also correction, continuous improvement, and the moral responsibility ministers held for the people.” No KPIs have ever been publicly disclosed for any minister in Anwar’s cabinet. No minister has been penalised, demoted, or publicly reprimanded for underperformance. Two ministers were removed in the December 2025 reshuffle — including Rafizi Ramli — but Anwar framed it as “portfolio realignment,” not performance accountability.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Claiming 100% KPI compliance without disclosing the KPIs is the corporate equivalent of giving yourself a perfect performance review and refusing to show the form. It is not accountability — it is self-certification. If every minister is meeting their KPIs, then either the KPIs are so low that failure is impossible, or the assessment is dishonest. Given that 77% of Malaysians report struggling with cost of living, university rankings are dropping, the CPI is stuck at 1MDB-era levels, and reform promises remain unmet, the claim is laughable on its face. Transparency means publishing the KPIs, the methodology, and the results — and letting the public judge. “Trust me, everyone is great” is not reform governance.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: A PM who holds both the PM and Finance Minister portfolios is essentially grading himself on fiscal management. There is no independent check on the most powerful office in the country. The Najib government also claimed ministers were performing. The Mahathir government also claimed results were being delivered. Without published metrics, every government is equally excellent — which means the exercise is meaningless. Singapore publishes ministerial performance metrics and links them to public service delivery targets. South Korea conducts annual government performance evaluations with published results. Malaysia’s approach under the “reformist” PM: trust his word. The Merdeka Center’s February 2026 poll showed public satisfaction with economic management at 38% — suggesting that whatever KPIs exist, the public disagrees they are being met.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Every Malaysian PM has claimed their cabinet is performing well. Najib claimed his ministers delivered results — while 1MDB bled billions. Muhyiddin claimed pandemic management was effective — while thousands died and billions in stimulus were unaccounted for. The pattern is universal: self-assessment without external verification always produces perfect scores. Anwar promised to be different. He isn’t. The KPI charade is a bipartisan tradition that PH has enthusiastically adopted.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Publish the KPIs. Go through every minister, every metric, every target, and every result. Put it online. Let the rakyat see whether their Education Minister’s KPIs include declining PISA scores, whether the Health Minister’s KPIs include 33,000 contract doctors still waiting for permanent posts, whether the Economy Minister’s KPIs include 77% of Malaysians struggling with costs. You won’t — because the scorecard would be devastating. A government that grades itself in secret has no credibility claiming accountability.

Sources: FMT

item.title: Fahmi Confirms MCMC Blocked Grindr and Blued — PH's 'Reform Government' Now Runs Digital Censorship Against Minorities
item.description:
Said by: Fahmi Fadzil, Communications Minister

Category: Social

Date: 2026-02-25

📌 What Happened

On February 25, 2026, Communications Minister Fahmi Fadzil confirmed in a written parliamentary reply that the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) has blocked the websites of Grindr and Blued — two of the world’s largest dating and networking platforms used by gay and bisexual men. Fahmi said MCMC is now “studying several legislative measures” to curb the apps from Google Play and the Apple App Store, though he acknowledged that control over foreign-owned platforms is “subject to regulations and policies set by the said platform providers.” By early March, both apps were removed from Malaysian app stores entirely. Deputy Prime Minister Ahmad Zahid Hamidi separately announced a proposed Cyber Crime Bill to expand the government’s digital enforcement powers, including over “immoral” content.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is the Madani government using state infrastructure to systematically erase digital safe spaces for a persecuted minority community — and doing it quietly through written parliamentary replies and regulatory action rather than open policy debate. Fahmi framed it under the catch-all of “lewd or immoral content, exploitation, abuse, scams, exploiting children or threats towards public safety” — a masterclass in conflating consenting adults with criminals. The blocking comes alongside Deputy Religious Affairs Minister Marhamah Rosli’s directive to replace all references to “LGBT” with “budaya songsang” (deviant culture) across government communications and social media, and a multi-agency committee targeting Muslim LGBTQ individuals with so-called “psychospiritual therapy.” This is not morality policing on the margins — it is a coordinated state apparatus targeting a specific community using censorship, surveillance, and institutional erasure.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Pakatan Harapan ran on civil liberties, institutional reform, and the promise that Malaysia would stop using the state to persecute its own citizens. Fahmi Fadzil — a former civil society figure and one-time press freedom advocate — is now the minister signing off on internet censorship targeting a minority. The same government that screams about “freedom of expression” when its critics get summoned under Section 124B is perfectly comfortable ordering MCMC to block websites and pressure Apple and Google to delist apps. The Cyber Crime Bill that Zahid is pushing will replace the Computer Crimes Act 1997 with broader powers — the exact kind of legislative overreach PH promised to roll back. This is not conservative governance; it is a reformist government that has decided persecuting minorities is electorally convenient.

📜 Previous Cases

This follows a well-documented pattern of escalation under the Anwar administration. In May 2023, the Home Affairs Ministry raided Swatch stores nationwide and confiscated over 160 rainbow-coloured watches. In June 2025, Kelantan police raided a private property described as a “gay party” and arrested 20 men. In November 2025, 202 people were arrested in raids on men-only venues in Kuala Lumpur — detainees reported being denied legal counsel, subjected to degrading treatment, and having their identities publicly exposed. Justice for Sisters documented systematic procedural violations. In January 2026, a community organisation cancelled its “Glamping with Pride” event after receiving death threats. Each time, the government either endorsed the action, stayed silent, or expanded the legal tools available for the next crackdown.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised Malaysians a government that would defend civil liberties, roll back repressive laws, and protect the vulnerable from state overreach. Instead, you are building new censorship infrastructure, proposing broader cyber legislation, and running conversion programmes through state religious departments — all while Fahmi Fadzil, who once championed media freedom, signs the blocking orders. Every digital rights group, every international press freedom watchdog, and every human rights organisation monitoring Malaysia is watching this and drawing the obvious conclusion: Madani reform was never meant for everyone. If you cannot even leave people’s phones alone, stop pretending you are a reform government.

Sources: CNA | Bloomberg | NST | Malay Mail | Straits Times | SCMP | Washington Blade | SAYS | Lowyat

item.title: MACC 'Corporate Mafia' Allegations — DAP Demands Independent Inquiry, Anwar Resists
item.description:
Said by: DAP Leaders / SCMP

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-02-25

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In late February 2026, DAP leaders publicly stated that Anwar’s proposed task force to examine alleged misconduct at MACC “does not go far enough.” They demanded an independent inquiry instead. SCMP reported that the push came after Bloomberg’s investigation alleged MACC officials had colluded with businessmen to orchestrate hostile takeovers of listed firms — the so-called “corporate mafia” allegations. Anwar’s response — a government-controlled task force rather than an independent commission — put his “reform agenda under renewed scrutiny.” Even his own coalition allies don’t trust his anti-corruption mechanisms.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When your own coalition partner — the party most associated with governance reform — publicly says your anti-corruption response is inadequate, the credibility gap is no longer deniable. DAP is not the opposition. DAP is Anwar’s ally. If DAP doesn’t trust the task force to deliver accountability, why should anyone else? The distinction between a task force and an independent inquiry is not semantic — it is structural. A task force reports to the PM. An independent inquiry reports to the public. Anwar chose the mechanism that keeps control in his hands, not the one that maximises transparency.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar’s entire political brand is built on fighting corruption. Bloomberg alleges his anti-corruption chief is compromised. His own coalition partner says his response is too weak. And his answer is… a task force. This is a man who spent years in prison over politically motivated corruption charges. He knows exactly what happens when anti-corruption institutions are captured by politics. Yet when faced with credible allegations of MACC capture, he reaches for the weakest possible tool. The task force is not designed to find the truth — it is designed to manage the news cycle until the story dies.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: This follows a pattern. When Shamsul Iskandar was caught issuing support letters, Anwar’s response was a “reprimand.” When Zahid’s 47 charges were dropped, the response was silence. When Farhash Wafa’s Sabah mining scandal broke, MACC cleared him while Bloomberg alleged Anwar ordered MACC not to investigate. Every time corruption allegations touch Anwar’s inner circle, the response is always the weakest available option — never an independent investigation, never structural reform, never real consequences.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your own coalition partner is telling you publicly that your response is inadequate. Listen to them. Establish an independent inquiry with subpoena powers, public hearings, and a mandate to investigate MACC from top to bottom. If you won’t listen to DAP, listen to the principle you once went to prison for: that anti-corruption institutions must be independent of political control. A task force controlled by the PM investigating the PM’s anti-corruption chief is not independence — it is theatre.

Sources: SCMP

item.title: MACC in Crisis with No Credible Leadership — The Anti-Corruption Watchdog Has Become the Scandal
item.description:
Said by: DAP / Bloomberg / CNA Analysis

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-02-25

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) is embroiled in its worst-ever credibility crisis. Chief Commissioner Azam Baki faces Bloomberg allegations of owning millions in shares (breaching civil servant limits) and colluding with a ‘corporate mafia.’ Rather than resign, Azam filed a RM100 million defamation suit against Bloomberg. DAP, PH’s own coalition partner, has demanded an independent inquiry, saying the government’s task force ‘does not go far enough.’ CNA called it part of Anwar’s ‘perfect storm.’

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: If the anti-corruption agency itself is corrupt, the entire governance framework collapses. MACC is supposed to be the institution that holds everyone else accountable. When MACC is the subject of corruption allegations — and the government’s response is a toothless task force rather than an independent inquiry — it signals that no institution in Malaysia is trustworthy.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar’s signature governance promise was strengthening anti-corruption institutions. He proposed separating the AG and Public Prosecutor roles (stalled). He promised MACC independence (the agency’s own chief is now the scandal). He promised zero tolerance for corruption (his own aide was charged with bribery). Every anti-corruption promise has not just been broken — it’s been inverted. The anti-corruption infrastructure is worse under PH, not better.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Under BN, MACC was widely perceived as politically compromised, used to target opposition politicians while ignoring ruling party corruption. Under PH 1.0, MACC briefly had credibility when it investigated 1MDB. Under PH 2.0, MACC’s credibility has hit rock bottom — the chief is fighting Bloomberg in court while his own agency faces allegations of running a corporate extortion ring. The trajectory: compromised → briefly credible → worse than ever.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your own coalition partner says your response to the MACC crisis is inadequate. Bloomberg — one of the world’s most respected financial news outlets — is standing by its reporting against Azam Baki. Your anti-corruption commission is running a RM100 million lawsuit against journalism instead of fighting corruption. And your response is a ’task force.’ A task force. For the most serious corruption allegation in MACC’s history. That’s not reform — that’s managing the optics while the institution burns.

Sources: SCMP — Pressure Mounts on Anwar Over MACC | CNA — Anwar Faces Graft Crisis

item.title: Penang Exco Sacked After Charged for Assaulting Wife — How Did DAP Vet This Person?
item.description:
Said by: Chow Kon Yeow / Fahmi / Penang DAP

Category: Social

Date: 2026-02-24

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 24 February 2026, a Penang executive councillor was detained for allegedly assaulting his wife. He contacted Chow Kon Yeow to apologise. He was subsequently charged in court and sacked from his exco position. The incident drew attention to DAP’s leadership vetting processes — how a state-level cabinet member with alleged domestic violence issues was appointed to a senior position.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: An exco member is a state-level cabinet minister — a person entrusted with public authority and decision-making power. When an exco is charged with domestic violence, it raises two questions: did DAP know about any prior incidents before the appointment, and what vetting process exists to prevent this? Domestic violence is not a private matter for public officials — it speaks to character, judgment, and fitness for office.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: DAP positions itself as progressive, rights-based, and modern. The party has championed women’s rights in opposition. But when one of its own exco members is charged with assaulting his wife, the response was reactive — sack him after the charges, apologise, move on. No public review of how the appointment was made. No announced reforms to the vetting process. No commitment to mandatory background checks for future appointments. The progressivism is for press conferences, not for personnel decisions.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: This isn’t the first DAP appointee controversy. Penang DAP has faced internal conflicts over councillor appointments, with disputes between Chow and Guan Eng’s camps. The party’s appointment processes have been criticised as opaque and personality-driven rather than merit-based. The exco assault case is the most extreme example of what happens when vetting is inadequate.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You champion women’s rights in Parliament. Your exco member was charged with assaulting his wife. You sacked him after the fact — which is the minimum. What are you doing to prevent the next case? Implement mandatory background checks, character references, and a formal vetting committee for all state-level appointments. Or just keep reacting to scandals after they happen.

Sources: NST | The Sun

item.title: Rawang Hindu Temple Demolished at Midnight — No Court Order, Police Assisted, PH State Government Silent
item.description:
Said by: Selangor PH State Government / Police

Category: Social

Date: 2026-02-24

📌 WHAT HAPPENED

On 24 February 2026, the Sri Uchimalai Muniswaran temple in Rawang, Selangor was demolished in the middle of the night — hours after deities were allegedly relocated following pressure on the temple committee. The demolition was carried out with police assistance and without any court order.

Lawyers Rajesh Nagarajan and Sachpreetraj Singh issued a statement: “We strongly deny the claims by Yayasan Kubra representative Ariff Omar that the temple committee voluntarily removed the deities. That is false and ridiculous since the temple chairman lodged a police report only yesterday complaining that the temple was being pressured to vacate the land without any court order.”

Four men, including activist Tamim Dahri, were arrested but the Shah Alam High Court quashed the remand order and ordered their immediate release. Police classified it as a land dispute, not a racial or religious conflict.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

A Hindu temple was demolished at midnight, with police present, without a court order, in a state governed by PH. This is not something that happens in a country with rule of law. It’s something that happens when institutions have failed and political will is absent.

The timing — amid a broader crackdown on unauthorised Hindu temples in Selangor following the Sultan’s statements — is not coincidental. Malaysia Hindu Sangam identified 687 unauthorised Hindu temples in Selangor alone. The question isn’t whether they’re authorised — it’s whether the government’s response is to demolish community heritage or find solutions.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

PH promised to be different. To protect minorities. To uphold rule of law. Instead, under a PH state government:

  • A temple was demolished without a court order
  • It happened at midnight — the time you choose when you don’t want witnesses
  • Police assisted rather than prevented it
  • The arrested men were released by the High Court, suggesting the lower court’s remand was improper If this happened under BN, DAP would have called it ethnic cleansing. Under PH, it’s a ’land dispute.'

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

Temple demolitions have a long and painful history in Malaysia. The Seafield Sri Maha Mariamman temple riot of 2018 occurred during PH 1.0’s watch. The pattern repeats: minority religious sites are treated as disposable when they stand on land someone else wants. The only thing that changes is which coalition looks away.

Controversial Muslim preacher Zamri Vinoth has been targeting Hindu temples with inflammatory rhetoric, seemingly with impunity. Malaysiakini columnist Andrew Sia asked: “Why do agitators like Zamri Vinoth seem to have immunity while insulting Hindu temples?”

🎯 PH CALLOUT

Anwar Ibrahim himself acknowledged the sensitivity: “I know this will invite controversy. Some people have gone to extremes, even launching campaigns to demolish temples.” He warned against vigilantism. Then a temple was demolished at midnight in his coalition’s state. Your warning means nothing if your own state government cannot prevent midnight demolitions without court orders. The Indian community voted for you because they believed you would protect them. This is what that protection looks like?

Sources: FMT | Harapan Daily | Bernama | CNA

item.title: Zafrul vs Rafizi — Former MITI Minister Tells Rafizi to 'Stop Spinning' Over Double Tariff Claims
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, MIDA Chairman / former MITI Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-02-23

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 23 February 2026, Tengku Zafrul — now MIDA chairman — publicly rebuked former Economy Minister Rafizi Ramli for claiming Malaysia faced nearly double the tariff rate under the US trade deal. Zafrul accused Rafizi of ‘distorting the facts’ and told him to ‘stop spinning — it’s exhausting to keep responding.’ Rafizi had argued the ART locked Malaysia into unfavourable terms. Zafrul countered that the 19% tariff no longer applied after the US Supreme Court struck down Trump’s tariff framework.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Two former PH ministers are publicly fighting over a trade deal the government signed. Zafrul negotiated it; Rafizi — who was ousted from Cabinet — is now attacking it. This isn’t policy debate; it’s a proxy war between PH factions using trade policy as ammunition. When your own former ministers can’t agree on whether a deal you signed was good or bad, the rakyat has every right to ask: who was actually in charge of trade policy?

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Both men are right and wrong simultaneously. Zafrul is right that Rafizi’s specific tariff claim was technically inaccurate. Rafizi is right that the broader deal surrendered too much. But neither is addressing the real problem: the deal was rushed, poorly communicated, and published on the White House website before Malaysian MPs saw it. The public spat between two PH elites is entertainment, not accountability.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: The Zafrul-Rafizi rivalry predates this deal. Rafizi publicly warned that Farhash allegations undermined the PM’s credibility; Zafrul stayed silent. Now Rafizi attacks Zafrul’s signature achievement. PH’s internal dysfunction is becoming its defining feature — they fight each other more effectively than they fight corruption.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: When your former ministers are publicly calling each other liars over a trade deal you signed four months ago, the problem isn’t the tariff rate — it’s the coalition. Fix your internal communications before you negotiate with superpowers. And stop making the rakyat watch your dirty laundry air on Facebook.

Sources: FMT | The Vibes | Bernama

item.title: Rafizi Alleges Anwar Is Fueling Social Media Attacks Against Him — Rift Goes Public
item.description:
Said by: Rafizi Ramli, former Economy Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-02-22

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 22 February 2026, former Economy Minister Rafizi Ramli publicly alleged that Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim was behind coordinated social media campaigns targeting him. Business Today reported Rafizi accused Anwar of “fueling his social media influence” — using coordinated accounts to attack his credibility and undermine his political standing within PKR. SCMP followed up on 18 February, framing Rafizi as seizing “the banner of reform and clean government that was once Anwar’s own” and asking whether he was now “the conscience of the Reformasi movement.” Anwar dismissed the rift as a “distraction.” Rafizi had previously warned Anwar that the CPI flatline at 50 points was “extremely dangerous” for the government’s credibility. His reward: being sidelined from cabinet in the December 2025 reshuffle. The Economy Ministry portfolio sat vacant for six months after his departure.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When your most competent former minister accuses you of running influence operations against him, the reform brand is in terminal decline. This is not opposition noise — this is the former Economy Minister, a PKR founding member, saying the PM is using dirty tricks against his own people. If the allegations are true, the PM’s office is diverting resources to internal political warfare instead of governing. If they are false, the rift is still real and the party is fracturing publicly ahead of the next election. Either way, PKR’s internal democracy is collapsing — the party built on challenging authoritarian leadership is now led by a man who allegedly punishes dissent with coordinated social media attacks.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Rafizi is not opposition. He is PKR. He is Anwar’s own party. If the PM is using social media to discipline internal critics, that is the behaviour of an authoritarian party boss, not a democratic leader. It is the kind of thing Najib was accused of — and which Anwar once condemned. The SCMP framing Rafizi as “the conscience of Reformasi” should alarm Anwar deeply — it means international media has noticed the title no longer fits the PM. When your protégé inherits your brand because you abandoned it, the verdict is clear. The man who told the truth about corruption risks within the government was punished for it. That is not reform — that is retaliation.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Rafizi’s allegations of coordinated social media attacks echo patterns in other authoritarian-leaning governments where leaders use cyber troops to discipline internal dissent. Within PKR itself, Rafizi had previously clashed with the party establishment over candidate selection and economic policy. His warning about the Farhash Wafa scandal was ignored; Farhash was later charged with bribery. His warning about the CPI score was dismissed; the score remains at 1MDB-era levels. The pattern is consistent: Rafizi raises legitimate concerns, the leadership ignores or punishes him, and the concerns later prove justified.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: The minister who warned you about corruption in your own office was right. The minister who told you the CPI score was “extremely dangerous” was right. Your response was to sideline him and allegedly run social media campaigns against him. If you want to prove Rafizi wrong, publish a full accounting of the PMO’s social media spending and contractor relationships. If you can’t do that, the allegation stands — and the “Reformasi” movement’s conscience now belongs to the man you discarded, not the man in Putrajaya.

Sources: Business Today | SCMP

item.title: DAP Special Congress July 12 — Party Questions Its Own Role in Government While Staying in Government
item.description:
Said by: Chow Kon Yeow, Penang Chief Minister / DAP

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-02-21

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 21 February 2026, Penang CM Chow Kon Yeow announced that DAP would hold a special congress on 12 July 2026 to decide the party’s role in Anwar’s government. Chow insisted DAP was ’not withdrawing support from the Madani government’ but was discussing ‘whether to continue holding ministerial posts and certain positions.’ DAP’s 40 MPs would continue supporting the government. All members, including leaders, must abide by the congress decision.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: DAP is threatening to leave government without actually leaving government. The special congress is political theatre — a way to pressure Anwar for better terms while maintaining the safety of coalition membership. If DAP genuinely had policy disagreements, they’d articulate them publicly. Instead, they’re holding an internal vote on ‘whether to hold ministerial posts’ — which is a negotiation tactic dressed up as democratic deliberation.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: This is the political equivalent of threatening to quit your job while continuing to cash the paycheck. DAP wants credit for independence (‘we’re questioning our role!’) while enjoying the benefits of power (‘we’re not actually leaving!’). The special congress gives DAP negotiating leverage for the next cabinet reshuffle without any real risk. If the congress votes to stay — which it will — DAP gets to say ’the members decided.’ If they vote to leave cabinet — which they won’t — it would collapse the government. Both outcomes benefit DAP; neither benefits the rakyat.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: DAP has a history of internal drama over its role in coalition governments. In the Mahathir 2.0 era, DAP faced similar tensions over compromises with PPBM. The pattern is consistent: DAP enters coalitions, compromises on principles, faces internal backlash, holds dramatic internal events, then stays. The cycle repeats because the alternative — opposition — is worse for the party apparatus.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: If you have policy disagreements with the government, state them publicly. If you think the coalition isn’t delivering for your voters, say why. But don’t hold a special congress to ‘decide your role’ while simultaneously promising to keep supporting the government. That’s not democratic deliberation — it’s a negotiating ploy. The rakyat can see through it.

Sources: Malay Mail | Bernama

item.title: MAHB Delisted After GDA Takeover — Five Directors Resign in Protest
item.description:
Said by: Bursa Malaysia / GDA Consortium

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-02-18

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 18 February 2026, Malaysia Airports Holdings Berhad (MAHB) — the public airport operator managing 39 airports nationwide — was officially delisted from Bursa Malaysia after the GDA consortium completed its takeover at RM11 per share. The consortium was led by Khazanah Nasional and EPF, with BlackRock’s Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP) as a key partner. All five independent directors resigned in protest, having deemed the offer “unfair and unreasonable” to minority shareholders. Hong Leong Investment Bank’s independent assessment concluded the offer was “not fair but reasonable” — a diplomatic way of saying shareholders were getting shortchanged but had no better option. Civil society groups protested BlackRock’s GIP involvement amid Palestine boycott concerns.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: A strategic national asset — the operator of every commercial airport in Malaysia — is now privately controlled with drastically reduced public accountability. When MAHB was listed, its financials, board decisions, and performance were subject to Bursa disclosure rules and public scrutiny. That transparency is gone. The mass resignation of all five independent directors is extraordinary and nearly unprecedented in Malaysian corporate history. These are professionals who staked their reputations on saying the deal was bad for minority shareholders. Yet the government-linked consortium pushed it through anyway. When the people whose job is to protect shareholders resign in protest, the message is unambiguous.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government used public money (Khazanah and EPF are government-controlled) to take private a public asset — reducing accountability while using rakyat’s retirement savings to fund the deal. EPF manages the savings of millions of Malaysian workers. Those workers are now involuntary investors in a delisted company with no market price transparency and no public reporting requirements. The “not fair but reasonable” assessment is corporate speak for “you’re getting a bad deal but you can’t stop it.” And the Palestine boycott angle — BlackRock’s GIP involvement drew protests from the same communities Anwar courts on Gaza solidarity. The government that lectures the world about Palestine partnered with BlackRock to privatise Malaysian airports. The hypocrisy is breathtaking.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has a long history of controversial privatisations and delistings of strategic assets. Tenaga Nasional, Telekom Malaysia, and various highway concessionaires have all been subject to ownership restructuring that reduced public oversight. The MAHB delisting follows the same playbook: government-linked entities acquire public companies at prices independent assessors deem unfair, minority shareholders are squeezed out, and accountability disappears behind private boardroom doors. Under BN, this was called cronyism. Under PH, it is called “strategic restructuring.”

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Five independent directors resigned saying the deal was unfair. Hong Leong said it was “not fair.” You used EPF — workers’ retirement money — to fund a deal that independent voices said was bad for shareholders. MAHB manages every airport in Malaysia; Malaysians deserve transparency over how it is run. Commit to publishing MAHB’s financials annually even after delisting. Commit to independent board oversight. Or admit that this was a deal designed to benefit the consortium at the expense of the public — exactly the kind of crony capitalism you promised to end.

Sources: The Edge | Malay Mail

item.title: Unity Coalition 'Faltering' — Reform Mandate Unmet, Power Struggles Naked
item.description:
Said by: UCA News Analysis

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-02-15

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: ‘Malaysia’s Unity Government is beginning to look less like a grand coalition forged in the national interest and more like a fragile ceasefire among parties that no longer trust one another.’ PH campaigned on clear, specific, publicly measurable pledges — most remain unmet.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When even sympathetic international observers describe your coalition as ‘faltering,’ the narrative has shifted from ‘can they deliver?’ to ‘how long can they survive?’ The unity government was sold as a necessary compromise to keep extremists out. But compromise without delivery is just power-sharing for its own sake.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Unmet manifesto pledges — UUCA repeal, toll abolition, anti-hopping law enforcement, institutional reform — are piling up. Each broken promise erodes the moral distinction between PH and the governments it replaced. A coalition that exists only to prevent the alternative from governing is not governing — it is occupying.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: PH 1.0 under Mahathir also promised sweeping reform and delivered almost nothing before collapsing in the Sheraton Move. The pattern is identical: form a coalition, promise reform, get bogged down in internal power struggles, deliver nothing, and hope the fear of the alternative keeps voters loyal. Malaysia’s voters have now watched two PH governments fail to deliver on the same promises. At some point, the excuse runs out.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You told voters the unity government was necessary for stability and reform. You got the stability (barely). Where’s the reform? Every month that passes without delivering on your own manifesto, you look less like a reform government and more like a power-sharing arrangement with no purpose beyond self-preservation.

Sources: UCA News — Unity Coalition Falters

item.title: DAP Internal War: Chow vs Guan Eng Over Penang Councillor Appointments
item.description:
Said by: Chow Kon Yeow / Lim Guan Eng / Penang DAP

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-02-14

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Penang DAP was forced to publicly clarify an ‘alleged misunderstanding’ between CM Chow Kon Yeow and his predecessor Lim Guan Eng over the appointment of councillors for both city councils. DAP publicity secretary Joshua Woo insisted the appointments followed ‘required conditions and processes’ and were consistent with ‘institutional precedent.’ The statement was damage control after reports suggested a factional power struggle between Chow’s camp and Guan Eng’s allies over who controls appointments in Penang.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When a party has to issue public statements clarifying that its CM and former CM are not fighting over councillor appointments, they are definitely fighting over councillor appointments. Penang councillors control local planning approvals, licensing, and land use decisions worth billions. This isn’t a personality clash — it’s a fight over patronage distribution. Who gets appointed to councils determines who controls development in Penang.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: DAP’s internal appointment process is described as following ’established method long practised by Penang DAP.’ Translation: the same opaque, personality-driven selection process that the party has always used. There is no open application, no independent vetting committee, no published criteria. Councillors are appointed by party leaders based on factional loyalty. CNA’s analysis described DAP’s Penang politics as ‘acrimonious’ and driven by ‘personality politics’ rather than policy.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: The Chow-Guan Eng tension has been building since DAP’s party elections in September 2024, when Chow lost the state DAP chairmanship — effectively making him a CM without control of the state party machinery. The Straits Times noted Chow ‘vowed work continues’ even though he’s ’not state’s DAP chief’ — a ’not a lame duck’ declaration that only lame ducks need to make.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your party can’t even agree on who appoints councillors without a public spat. Your CM and former CM are in a factional war. Your councillor appointments follow an ’established method’ that nobody outside the party understands. Implement transparent, merit-based appointment criteria. Publish them. And resolve your internal power struggle before it starts affecting planning approvals and development decisions that impact Penangites.

Sources: The Vibes | CNA | Straits Times

item.title: Anwar Defends MACC Chief Azam Baki Over Second Shareholding Scandal, Tells Critics to 'Read His Explanation'
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-02-12

📌 WHAT HAPPENED

In February 2026, Bloomberg reported that MACC chief Azam Baki was a key shareholder in a financial services company — Velocity Capital Partners Berhad — based on corporate filings. This was Azam’s second shareholding scandal, following the 2022 controversy over shares in Excel Force MSC Berhad.

Civil society groups including C4 Center demanded Azam’s immediate suspension pending investigation. They noted concerning links between Velocity Capital Partners and MMAG Holdings, where controversial former Anwar aide Farhash Wafa Salvador had acquired a 20% stake and was appointed chairman.

Anwar’s response? He told critics to “read his [Azam’s] explanation” and said the special committee led by the Attorney General would handle it. As of March 2026, the government had not extended Azam’s contract, but he was never suspended during the investigation.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

The head of Malaysia’s anti-corruption agency is facing allegations that he held shares exceeding limits for public officials — for the second time. The first scandal in 2022 saw massive public protests demanding his removal. PH, then freshly in power, inherited Azam but chose to keep him. Now, under PH’s watch, the same pattern has repeated.

The deeper concern is the Farhash connection. If the MACC chief held shares in a company linked to the PM’s former aide, that raises serious conflict-of-interest questions about why MACC never investigated Farhash despite multiple public calls to do so.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

“Read his explanation.” That’s the Prime Minister’s response to allegations that the head of the anti-corruption commission may be personally corrupt. Not “we take this seriously.” Not “he will be suspended pending investigation.” Just… read his explanation.

This is the same Anwar who, in opposition, demanded Azam’s head during the first shareholding scandal. PH supporters marched in the streets in January 2022 demanding Azam’s removal. Anwar’s allies called it a governance crisis. Now that Anwar is PM, the same governance crisis gets a committee, a delay, and a suggestion to “read his explanation.”

C4 Center called it “Shareholding Scandal 2.0” and demanded immediate suspension. Bersih accused MACC of being used as a political tool. Anwar told Bersih they were being “unfair.” The reformist PM is now defending the institution he once wanted to tear down.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

The first Azam Baki shareholding scandal in early 2022 was one of the biggest anti-corruption protests in Malaysian history. Thousands marched demanding his removal. Azam claimed his brother used his trading account — a defence widely ridiculed. The Securities Commission eventually cleared him in a decision that drew widespread criticism.

PH came to power in November 2022 promising to clean up institutions. They kept Azam. His contract has been extended three times — in 2023, 2024, and 2025. Each extension was a choice. Each extension told Malaysians that MACC independence was less important than political convenience.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You marched against Azam Baki. Your supporters filled the streets demanding his removal. Your allies called the first shareholding scandal a disgrace. Then you took power, kept him, extended his contract three times, and when the second scandal broke, told the public to “read his explanation.” This isn’t reform. This is capture. You own Azam Baki now — every scandal, every conflict of interest, every failure of accountability at MACC. You had the power to replace him and you chose not to. That choice has consequences, and the rakyat can see them clearly.

Sources: SCMP | Malay Mail | C4 Center | FMT | The Vibes

item.title: Selangor Bans All Pig Farming — Amirudin Caves to Sultan's Decree, Chinese Farmers Left With Nothing
item.description:
Said by: Amirudin Shari, Selangor Menteri Besar

Category: Social

Date: 2026-02-12

📌 WHAT HAPPENED

On 12 February 2026, Selangor Menteri Besar Amirudin Shari announced that the state government has stopped issuing pig farming licences and will accelerate the closure of all existing pig farms. The decision followed a decree from Sultan Sharafuddin Idris Shah, who declared that no pig farms should operate in any district in Selangor.

An earlier plan to relocate pig farms to Bukit Tagar — a sparsely populated area in northern Selangor — was shelved after Malay residents objected due to proximity to Sungai Selangor, a major water treatment source. Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim intervened in January to halt the relocation.

Selangor will now fully import all pork to meet non-Muslim demand. Chinese pig farmers, many of whom have operated multigenerational farms in Tanjong Sepat, face the destruction of their livelihoods.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

Selangor is Malaysia’s richest and most developed state — and it’s governed by PH. The pig farming ban affects a specific ethnic community’s livelihood and dietary needs. While environmental concerns about water pollution are legitimate, the total ban with no viable alternative is a capitulation, not a solution.

The message to Chinese voters is clear: when the palace decrees and Malay sentiment pushes back, PH will not fight for you. The Bukit Tagar relocation — which could have been a modern, regulated solution — was abandoned the moment it became politically uncomfortable.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

PH governs Selangor. PH had years to develop a proper, regulated, environmentally sustainable pig farming solution. Instead, they proposed a relocation, got pushback, abandoned it, and then caved to a royal decree with a total ban.

The state that brands itself as progressive and inclusive just eliminated an entire industry that primarily serves its Chinese community. Pork importers — who stand to profit massively — are already backing the ban. One importer estimated imports already cover 80% of demand. So who benefits? Not the farmers. Not the consumers who prefer fresh local pork. The importers.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

This follows a pattern of PH state governments failing to protect minority communities when the political cost gets too high. In Penang, DAP has governed for 16 years but still faces criticism for development that displaces communities. In Selangor, Amirudin has repeatedly been caught flat-footed — from flood management failures to the water leak crisis. Every time, the response is reactive, never proactive.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You are the government of Selangor. You had the power to regulate, modernise, and relocate pig farming. Instead, you banned it entirely. You didn’t solve a pollution problem — you eliminated a community’s livelihood. Where is the DAP that promised to protect all Malaysians regardless of race? Where is the Madani government that talks about inclusivity? The Chinese farmers in Tanjong Sepat would like to know.

Sources: Straits Times | NST | CodeBlue | The Star

item.title: Tiong King Sing Pulls 'Do You Know Who I Am?' Card While Lecturing Staff About Rudeness
item.description:
Said by: Tiong King Sing, Tourism, Arts and Culture Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-02-12

📌 WHAT HAPPENED

On 12 February 2026, Tourism Minister Tiong King Sing launched the ‘Mesra Malaysia’ (Friendly Malaysia) campaign at KL Sentral — a campaign about being welcoming and courteous to tourists. During the launch, Tiong complained about rudeness from public and private sector employees, saying: “Some officers think they have a lot of power. But when people don’t understand certain issues, we must explain.”

The South China Morning Post ran the story with the devastating headline: “Malaysian minister slams rude staff hindering tourism: ‘do you know who I am?’” — capturing the irony of a powerful politician lecturing others about arrogance while exuding precisely the same energy.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

Visit Malaysia Year 2026 is supposed to be a national priority. The tourism sector is critical to Malaysia’s economy. And the minister responsible for making Malaysia welcoming to the world is making headlines for his own attitude problem.

This isn’t Tiong’s first controversy. In 2025, Anwar had to personally warn him after alcohol was served at a Tourism Malaysia gala dinner — a sensitivity failure in a Muslim-majority country. The pattern is a minister who generates more negative headlines than tourist arrivals.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

You’re launching a campaign called ‘Friendly Malaysia’ while acting like the least friendly person in the room. The cognitive dissonance is spectacular. A minister complaining about public servants who “think they have a lot of power” — while wielding the power of a federal ministry to publicly shame unnamed workers — is peak Malaysian political irony.

The ‘do you know who I am’ energy is exactly the attitude tourists and citizens hate about Malaysian bureaucracy. And the minister tasked with fixing it is the embodiment of it.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

Tiong King Sing is a businessman-politician from Sarawak’s GPS coalition. The alcohol controversy at the gala dinner in 2025 already showed a minister out of touch with sensitivities. Malaysia’s tourism sector has also been plagued by scam taxi drivers, overcharging at tourist spots, and visa problems — systemic issues that require systemic solutions, not press conferences about attitude.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You put Tiong King Sing in charge of tourism. He’s now making international headlines for arrogance while launching a friendliness campaign. The minister responsible for Malaysia’s image is damaging Malaysia’s image. If you want Visit Malaysia Year 2026 to succeed, start by ensuring your ministers don’t become the joke tourists tell when they get home.

Sources: SCMP | CNA

item.title: Nga Kor Ming's MyKiosk Project: No Needs Assessment, No KPIs, MACC Calls It a Governance Failure
item.description:
Said by: Nga Kor Ming, Housing and Local Government Minister

Category: Housing

Date: 2026-02-10

📌 WHAT HAPPENED

On 10 February 2026, the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) revealed that the Housing and Local Government Ministry (KPKT) under Nga Kor Ming failed to carry out a proper needs assessment before launching the MyKiosk initiative — a nationwide programme to provide traders with modern kiosks.

MACC issued five governance improvement recommendations and stated: “The absence of a needs assessment and a comprehensive outcome-monitoring mechanism, including KPIs, hindered the evaluation of the project’s impact at the local authority level.”

Former MCA vice-president Ti Lian Ker immediately called for Nga’s resignation, saying: “When billions in public funds are at stake, failing to ask fundamental questions — who needs this, where it should be placed, and how success will be measured — is not just careless but irresponsible.”

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

This is basic governance 101. You don’t launch a nationwide programme with public money without first asking: Does anyone need this? The MyKiosk project was described as a white elephant after failing to meet government objectives, with poorly placed kiosks causing public inconvenience rather than helping traders.

Ti Lian Ker noted this added to “a growing record of questionable judgments and misplaced priorities” — including Nga’s directive to turn four public toilets into smart toilets and public unease over the Urban Renewal Bill. The pattern is clear: tone-deaf decision-making, weak policy grounding, and public backlash after the fact.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

Here’s the best part. In July 2025, when the MyKiosk project was first flagged as a white elephant, Nga invited MACC to investigate and then demanded an apology from critics when MACC found no corruption. He said: “To those who threw stones, don’t hide your hands.”

But no corruption doesn’t mean no failure. The MACC’s February 2026 report confirmed what everyone already knew: the project was poorly planned, poorly monitored, and poorly executed. Nga celebrated the absence of criminal intent while ignoring the presence of spectacular incompetence. That’s not governance — that’s a participation trophy.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

Nga’s ministry has a pattern. The smart toilet initiative was mocked nationally. The Urban Renewal Bill drew public protest. Each episode follows the same arc: launch first, study later, apologise when caught, then demand credit for not being corrupt. PAS researcher Dr Nurul Islam called it the “execute first, study later” work culture of the Madani Government.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You promised clean, competent governance. The MACC just told your ministry it didn’t even bother to check if anyone needed MyKiosk before spending public money on it. That’s not reform — that’s the same BN-era waste you used to condemn from the opposition bench. If a BN minister had done this, DAP would have held press conferences for weeks. When it’s your own minister? Silence.

Sources: FMT | FMT - Ti Lian Ker | The Star | Focus Malaysia

item.title: Students Flash Red Cards at Adam Adli Over Broken UUCA Promise — PH's Own Poster Boy Turns Reneger
item.description:
Said by: Adam Adli Abdul Halim, Deputy Higher Education Minister

Category: Education

Date: 2026-02-09

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 9 February 2026, university students marched to Parliament demanding the abolition of the Universities and University Colleges Act 1971 (UUCA). Their target? Deputy Higher Education Minister Adam Adli Abdul Halim — once Malaysia’s most famous student activist, now the living embodiment of PH’s broken promise. Abolishing UUCA was a key manifesto promise by Pakatan Harapan in both GE14 (2018) and GE15 (2022). In late January 2026, Higher Education Minister Zambry Abd Kadir told Parliament the government had “no plans” to abolish UUCA — a policy reversal delivered without fanfare. Students flashed red cards at Adam Adli and called him a “reneger.” Zambry refused to receive their memorandum. Adam Adli, 33, was arrested in 2013 for lowering a flag at a student rally — an act that made him a symbol of the student movement. PH championed his case as proof that UUCA was unjust. Now he sits in the ministry that enforces it.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: PH promised to repeal UUCA in two consecutive elections. Four years into government, the Higher Education Minister says there are “no plans” to abolish it. This is not a delay — it is a lie. The promise was never intended to be kept; it was designed to harvest student votes. Adam Adli’s personal arc is the most devastating metaphor for the PH government: the student who was arrested for lowering a flag in protest now sits in the ministry that maintains the law that criminalises student protest. The students who voted for PH based on this promise are now protesting PH’s own deputy minister. Zambry’s refusal to receive the memorandum is a symbolic gut punch — when the government won’t even accept a piece of paper from student protesters, the message is clear: your vote was useful, your voice is not. UUCA determines whether 1.3 million Malaysian university students can participate in the political life of their country. Keeping it in place means PH believes students should vote for reform but not advocate for it.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Under UUCA, students can face suspension or expulsion for participating in political activities. The law was enacted in 1971 after student protests against rural poverty — it was designed from the start to suppress politically conscious youth. PH knew this. PH campaigned against it. PH’s own deputy minister was a victim of it. And now PH is keeping it. The Straits Times noted the protest “adds to Anwar’s pre-election reform challenges” — framing UUCA as part of a broader pattern of stalled promises eroding youth support. If you cannot keep promises to students who believed in you, the reform brand is dead.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: UUCA reform has been promised by every opposition coalition since the 2008 political tsunami. PH 1.0 (2018-2020) promised reform but collapsed before acting. PH 2.0 has now had over three years and explicitly reversed the promise. The pattern is identical to toll abolition, AG-PP separation, and the Sedition Act repeal: promise boldly in opposition, delay in government, quietly abandon. UUCA joins a growing list of manifesto pledges that were campaign decorations, not governance commitments.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your deputy minister was arrested under this law. He built his career fighting it. Now he defends the ministry that enforces it. If you wrote this as fiction, it would be criticised as too on-the-nose. Abolish UUCA or amend it to remove restrictions on student political activity — as you promised twice. The 1.3 million students in Malaysian universities are watching. And they vote.

Sources: Straits Times | Malaysiakini | FMT

item.title: Fahmi Fadzil: Free Speech Doesn't Mean Freedom to Offend Online
item.description:
Said by: Fahmi Fadzil, Communications Minister

Category: Social

Date: 2026-02-07

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 7 February 2026, Communications Minister Fahmi Fadzil told the public that free speech does not mean freedom to offend or defame online. This came after the Federal Court reinstated Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act (CMA), which criminalises content deemed “grossly offensive” — a provision PH once opposed. Fahmi said the 2024 amendments raised the threshold from “offensive” to “grossly offensive,” claiming this made it harder to misuse. The Online Safety Act 2025 (ONSA) took effect on 1 January 2026, giving MCMC powers to penalise platforms up to RM10 million for failing to remove harmful content. MCMC — the body that decides what is “grossly offensive” — reports directly to Fahmi’s ministry.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: “Grossly offensive” is a subjective and vague standard that can mean anything the government wants it to mean. The combination of the reinstated CMA provision and the new ONSA gives the Malaysian government one of the most powerful speech regulation frameworks in Southeast Asia. Platforms face RM10M fines; individuals face prosecution. The chilling effect is by design. PH in opposition repeatedly condemned the CMA as a tool of authoritarian suppression. In 2018, PH’s manifesto called for “reviewing” the CMA. In 2022, PH Youth called Section 233 “draconian.” In government, they amended it slightly, renamed “offensive” to “grossly offensive,” and called it progress.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Who decides what is “grossly offensive”? MCMC, which answers to Fahmi. Fahmi, who answers to Anwar. There is no independent judicial review before content is flagged or removed. The minister who champions “free speech with limits” is also the minister who controls the limits. The fox is guarding the henhouse and telling the chickens it is for their own protection. PH’s own track record demolishes Fahmi’s argument: when the government arrests a journalist for asking a question (Rex Tan), investigates activists for social media posts, and uses the Sedition Act 90+ times in three years, the “we only target real harm” defence collapses. Renaming “offensive” to “grossly offensive” is not reform — it is rebranding.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Since taking power, the Anwar government has used the CMA, Sedition Act, and other speech laws to arrest journalists, activists, and social media users — including FMT journalist Rex Tan, activist Heidy Quah, and multiple online commentators critical of the government. The Sedition Act — which PH promised to repeal — has been used over 90 times since 2024. The CMA was wielded against critics under Najib, under Muhyiddin, and now under Anwar. The tool changes hands; the abuse continues. Every Malaysian government has used speech laws selectively: targeting critics while ignoring allies who say equally provocative things.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You promised to reform the CMA. Instead, you strengthened it. You promised to repeal the Sedition Act. Instead, you used it more than Najib did. You promised press freedom. Instead, your MCMC blocks news outlets and your police arrest journalists at dawn. Fahmi’s statement — “free speech doesn’t mean freedom to offend” — is the same argument made by every government that has ever restricted speech, from Singapore’s POFMA to China’s internet regulations. The formula is universal: affirm the right, define it so narrowly it becomes meaningless. PH voters expected the architecture of speech suppression to be dismantled. Instead, you added new floors to the building.

Sources: NST | Malaysiakini

item.title: MACC Probes Senior Penang State Official Over Land Deal — Chow Says 'We Are Not Investigators'
item.description:
Said by: Chow Kon Yeow, Penang Chief Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-01-31

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 31 January 2026, Penang CM Chow Kon Yeow confirmed that MACC was investigating allegations of abuse of power involving a senior state official over a land deal. Chow said the state government would ‘give full space’ to MACC and would not conduct its own internal probe. ‘We are not investigators, but we will provide full cooperation,’ he told reporters. The official was reportedly continuing to perform duties as usual during the investigation.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: A senior state official is under MACC investigation for abuse of power over a land deal — and the Penang government’s response is to let the official keep working while refusing to conduct any internal review. ‘We are not investigators’ is technically true but morally bankrupt. The state government is the employer. It has a duty to ensure its officials aren’t abusing their positions. Outsourcing accountability entirely to MACC while the official keeps making decisions is not cooperation — it’s wilful blindness.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Every private company in Malaysia would suspend an employee under MACC investigation pending the outcome — especially if the investigation involves the employee’s job functions. DAP’s Penang government lets the official keep working. The justification — ‘we are not investigators’ — is a cop-out. You don’t need to be investigators to implement basic governance protocols: suspend pending investigation, secure documents, conduct an internal review. These are standard corporate governance practices that the ‘competent administration’ party apparently hasn’t heard of.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: This is not Penang’s first MACC investigation under DAP. The Guan Eng tunnel trial is ongoing. Zakat fund allegations were investigated. Land deals have been questioned repeatedly. The pattern is consistent: MACC investigates, Penang says ‘we’re cooperating,’ officials keep working, and nothing changes internally until (and unless) charges are filed.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your senior official is under MACC investigation for abuse of power over a land deal and you won’t even suspend them? ‘We are not investigators’ is not a governance philosophy — it’s an abdication of responsibility. Implement an internal review. Suspend the official pending investigation. Secure the relevant land deal documents. Or admit that DAP’s Penang is no better at self-policing than any BN state.

Sources: FMT | Bernama

item.title: River Water Quality Declining — More Polluted Rivers in 2025 Than 2024
item.description:
Said by: Natural Resources Ministry

Category: Other

Date: 2026-01-28

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: According to data released by the Natural Resources Ministry in late January 2026, Malaysia’s river water quality deteriorated year-over-year. Clean rivers dropped from 486 to 468. “Moderate” quality rivers rose from 161 to 171. “Slightly polluted” rivers jumped from 25 to 33. The numbers are moving in the wrong direction across every category. This decline occurred as the government aggressively pushed rare earth mining expansion — one site in Perak already turned a river visibly blue from mining discharges — and continued to promote Malaysia as a “green economy” leader.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Rivers are the canary in the coal mine for environmental governance. If water quality declines while the government claims environmental progress, the claims are hollow. This is happening under a government that announced a Climate Resilience Plan, set green economy targets, and positioned Malaysia as environmentally conscious on the world stage. The data says otherwise. More polluted rivers mean worse drinking water sources, degraded ecosystems, higher water treatment costs, and health risks for communities living near affected waterways — disproportionately rural and lower-income Malaysians.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government boasts about green economy targets while presiding over measurable environmental degradation. You cannot simultaneously push aggressive rare earth mining expansion, approve data centre developments that strain water resources, and allow industrial discharge standards to go unenforced — then claim environmental leadership with a straight face. The river quality data is not subjective. It is measured. It is published. And it shows decline. The gap between the government’s environmental rhetoric and its environmental outcomes is not a communication problem — it is a governance failure. When your rivers are getting dirtier under your watch, your green targets are fiction.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s environmental enforcement has been chronically weak across administrations. The Sg Kim Kim toxic dumping in Johor (2019) hospitalised hundreds. The Perak River turned blue from rare earth mining (2025). Annual monsoon floods — worsened by deforestation and poor drainage planning — displace tens of thousands. The Department of Environment has been consistently underfunded and understaffed. Under BN, environmental protection was sacrificed for development. Under PH, the same sacrifice continues under a greener brand. The pattern is bipartisan: extract now, clean up never.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Publish the river-by-river breakdown. Name the polluters. Enforce discharge standards with real penalties — not warning letters that get filed and forgotten. If rare earth mining is turning rivers blue and water quality is declining nationally, your “green economy” narrative is a lie backed by your own ministry’s data. A government that measures environmental decline and does nothing about it is not governing — it is documenting its own failure. Fund the Department of Environment properly. Enforce industrial discharge standards. Or stop pretending you care about the environment.

Sources: Asian News Network

item.title: Malaysia Study: Cost-Benefit Analysis of US Trade Deal Shows 'Concerns' But Government Says Don't Quit
item.description:
Said by: Malaysian Government

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-01-27

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In January 2026, Bloomberg reported that the Malaysian government announced a formal cost-benefit analysis of the controversial US-Malaysia Agreed Reciprocal Trade (ART) agreement — following months of mounting criticism from former AG Tommy Thomas (who called it “the worst agreement since independence”), former PM Mahathir, think tanks, and opposition politicians. But in the same breath, the government warned lawmakers not to quit the pact. East Asia Forum published a detailed analysis titled “The Art of the Deal Lost in US-Malaysia Pact,” arguing Malaysia surrendered critical mineral export controls, digital services taxation rights, and independent trade policy in exchange for a tariff reduction from 25% to 19% — still punitive by any standard. The deal’s details were first published on the White House website before being shared with Malaysian Parliament.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Announcing a cost-benefit analysis while pre-empting its conclusion is not governance — it is political theatre. “Study but don’t quit” tells you everything about this administration’s approach: acknowledge problems, change nothing. If the analysis shows the deal hurts Malaysia, will the government act? The answer is already in the framing. The study exists to absorb pressure, not to inform decisions. The fact that Parliament learned about the deal from the White House website is a governance failure of the first order — trade agreements that constrain national sovereignty should require parliamentary approval before signing, not after.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: “Study but don’t quit” is the Madani playbook in miniature: acknowledge the problem, promise a process, change nothing. It is the same approach applied to the AG-PP separation, UUCA repeal, toll abolition, and every other stalled reform. The cost-benefit analysis will either be buried, selectively quoted, or used to justify minor adjustments while the core agreement remains untouched. Anwar bypassed the legislature on the most consequential economic agreement in decades. When your own former AG calls your trade deal the worst since independence and your response is “we’ll study it but we’re keeping it,” the study is performative.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has a history of trade agreements where the government commits first and reviews later. The TPPA under Najib faced similar criticism — Malaysia signed on to provisions critics said undermined sovereignty, pharmaceutical pricing, and labour rights. PH 1.0 withdrew from the TPPA in 2018, calling it unfair. PH 2.0 signed the ART — which critics say contains even more restrictive provisions — and now wants to “study” whether it was a good idea. The pattern of committing to deals under pressure, then conducting post-hoc reviews that change nothing, is a bipartisan Malaysian tradition.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: If you’re serious about the cost-benefit analysis, publish the full terms of the ART — not the White House summary, but the actual agreement. Let independent economists, not government-appointed task forces, evaluate the deal. If the analysis shows Malaysia got a bad deal, have the courage to renegotiate. “Study but don’t quit” is not a policy — it is a confession that you already know the answer and don’t want to act on it. Parliament deserves a vote on trade agreements that constrain national sovereignty. Give them one.

Sources: Bloomberg | East Asia Forum

item.title: Selangor's Illegal Waste Dumping Crisis — Years of Promises, Zero Results Under Amirudin
item.description:
Said by: Amirudin Shari, Selangor Menteri Besar

Category: Social

Date: 2026-01-26

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 26 January 2026, Amirudin Shari declared ’no more finger pointing’ and ordered a ’total crackdown’ on illegal waste dumping across Selangor. He said he would personally visit sites with local authorities and ’take firm action.’ He instructed the Land Office and local authorities to focus on cleanliness. This follows a similar declaration in early 2026 where the Selangor government announced intensified efforts to ’eradicate’ illegal waste dumping.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Amirudin has been Menteri Besar since July 2018. He has made similar promises about illegal dumping every single year. Each year, the announcements get bolder — ’total crackdown,’ ‘firm action,’ ’no more excuses.’ Each year, illegal dump sites proliferate. Residents in areas like Kuala Langat, Jenjarom, and Ijok continue to suffer from toxic waste, contaminated water, and respiratory problems. The gap between Amirudin’s rhetoric and Selangor’s reality is now a canyon.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: ‘I will personally go to the ground’ is what politicians say when they’ve run out of policy ideas. After 7+ years as MB, if Amirudin hasn’t fixed illegal dumping, it’s not because he hasn’t visited enough sites — it’s because the enforcement infrastructure, penalties, and political will don’t exist.
