For a PM who champions non-alignment and Global South solidarity, signing a deal that an international think tank describes as a “loyalty test” is a contradiction that cannot be spun away.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s trade deal history is littered with agreements that looked good in press conferences and bad in practice. The TPPA under Najib was shelved after public fury over sovereignty clauses. The RCEP was signed but implementation has been slow. Each agreement follows the same pattern: government celebrates the signing, analysts identify the problems, public anger builds, and the government pivots to damage control. The ART follows this pattern exactly — except this time, the critics include a respected international institution, not just domestic opposition.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: The Lowy Institute called your trade deal a “loyalty test.” Independent analysts identified “poison pill” clauses. The tariff stayed at 19%, not zero. Your own narrative doesn’t match the analysis. Publish the full text of the ART with a clause-by-clause explanation of what Malaysia committed to. Commission an independent economic impact assessment. Explain why Malaysia accepted “loyalty test” provisions that other countries rejected. Or keep celebrating a deal that international analysts describe as a strategic defeat.

Sources: Lowy Institute | MalaysiaNow

item.title: Parliament Reduced to Rubber Stamp — PM Whips Ensure Bills Pass Without Genuine Debate
item.description:
Said by: Opposition MPs / Parliamentary Reform Advocates

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-11-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Despite PH’s promise to strengthen parliamentary democracy, the Dewan Rakyat continues to function as a rubber stamp rather than a genuine deliberative body. Government bills pass with minimal amendment, committee work is perfunctory, and the government whip ensures coalition MPs vote in bloc. The PM term limit bill — one of PH’s signature reform promises — failed in Parliament because PH couldn’t even muster enough of its own MPs to support it. Fahmi Fadzil blamed “opposition confusion” for the failure, when 8 of PH’s own MPs didn’t show up to vote.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Parliament is supposed to be the check on executive power. When parliament is reduced to a venue for the PM’s speeches and pro-forma bill passage, democratic governance degrades into elected authoritarianism. Important legislation passes without meaningful debate. Bad policies go unchallenged. The PM term limit bill failure is the most damning example: PH had the numbers, had the mandate, and still couldn’t deliver on one of the simplest democratic reforms imaginable — because its own MPs couldn’t be bothered to attend.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH explicitly promised parliamentary reform: stronger select committees, an independent Speaker, greater allocation for opposition MPs, and meaningful debate on legislation. Some incremental changes were made, but the fundamental dynamics haven’t changed. The government controls the agenda, determines which bills are debated, and uses coalition arithmetic to ensure outcomes. Under BN, Najib’s government once passed 11 bills in a single day without proper debate. PH criticised this as democratic malpractice. Under PH, the pace is slightly slower but the outcomes are equally predetermined.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Parliamentary rubber-stamping is a Malaysian tradition that predates PH. Under Mahathir 1.0, parliament was a formality. Under Najib, the OSA, SOSMA, and NSC Act were rammed through with minimal debate. PH 1.0 (2018-2020) made some improvements — reviving select committees and increasing opposition funding — but these reforms were incomplete and have stalled under PH 2.0. The East Asia Forum documented how “Malaysia’s reform aspirations clashed with political realities in 2024,” noting that parliamentary independence remained a promise rather than a practice.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You control the Dewan Rakyat. You have a supermajority with BN’s support. You couldn’t even pass a PM term limit bill — your own signature reform. You had the numbers, you had the mandate, and you still couldn’t deliver. If you can’t limit your own power when you have the votes to do it, you were never going to limit your own power at all. Strengthen select committees with real investigative authority. Give the Speaker genuine independence. Allow free votes on reform bills. Or keep running a parliament that functions exactly like the one you inherited — just with different faces in the government benches.

Sources: East Asia Forum | Rosa Luxemburg

item.title: Personal Bankruptcies Rise 5.7% — Self-Declared Bankruptcy Trend 'Growing Concern'
item.description:
Said by: Department of Insolvency Malaysia

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In the first nine months of 2025, Malaysia recorded 4,875 personal bankruptcy cases — a 5.7% increase from the same period in 2024. The Insolvency Department Director-General flagged a “growing concern”: the rise in self-declared bankruptcies, where Malaysians are so financially distressed they voluntarily declare themselves bankrupt rather than waiting for creditors to force the process. The data indicates that financial distress is not concentrated among business owners — it is spreading to salaried workers, young professionals, and gig economy participants.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When people voluntarily declare bankruptcy, it means they have given up hope of recovery. Self-declared bankruptcy is an act of financial surrender — it signals that the individual sees no path to repaying debts through income alone. A 5.7% rise in total cases, combined with a rising self-declared trend, suggests that Malaysia’s cost-of-living crisis is pushing ordinary households past breaking point. Official inflation is 1.3%. But bankruptcies are rising 5.7%. Those two numbers cannot both be telling the truth about the economy.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government celebrates GDP growth, FDI records, and low headline inflation while an increasing number of Malaysians are literally declaring themselves bankrupt. The disconnect between macro indicators and micro reality is the defining feature of the Madani economy: the numbers look good in Putrajaya, but the rakyat is drowning in debt. The bankruptcy threshold was raised from RM50,000 to RM100,000 in 2020 — meaning current figures actually undercount the problem, since people with debts under RM100,000 can no longer be declared bankrupt by creditors. The real financial distress is worse than the data shows.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Personal bankruptcy has been rising in Malaysia for years. Under BN, the government raised the bankruptcy threshold to mask the trend. Under PH 1.0, a moratorium on bankruptcy proceedings was introduced during COVID. Under PH 2.0, the threshold remains at RM100,000 and bankruptcy cases are climbing again. Each government has managed the optics rather than addressing the root causes: stagnant real wages, rising living costs, easy access to consumer credit, and inadequate financial literacy. The cycle continues because addressing it would require admitting that the economy is not working for ordinary people.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Bankruptcies are up 5.7% while you claim inflation is 1.3%. Self-declared bankruptcies are rising — people are so desperate they’re surrendering voluntarily. Your GDP growth is meaningless if it doesn’t translate into household financial security. Expand financial counselling services through AKPK. Regulate predatory lending practices, especially in the buy-now-pay-later sector. Publish household debt-to-income data by income quintile so the rakyat can see the real picture. Or keep quoting macro numbers while more Malaysians walk into the Insolvency Department to declare themselves bankrupt.

Sources: Bernama | Malay Mail | The Star

item.title: Rakyat Says 1.3% Inflation is a Lie — 'Don't Know Where Madanon Gets These Numbers'
item.description:
Said by: Public Sentiment / Social Media

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Malaysia’s official inflation rate for October 2025 was reported at 1.3% by the Department of Statistics (DOSM). Malaysians responded with open mockery on social media, with comments like “don’t know where Madanon gets these numbers.” The disconnect between headline inflation and lived experience is stark: food-away-from-home inflation ran at 4.3-4.7%, far exceeding the headline rate. Rental costs, healthcare, and education expenses — categories that disproportionately affect middle and lower-income households — all rose faster than the official composite figure suggests.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When citizens openly mock their government’s official statistics, it signals a collapse in institutional credibility. The 1.3% headline figure is technically accurate — it measures the CPI basket as defined by DOSM. But the CPI basket doesn’t reflect how ordinary Malaysians actually spend their money. A hawker meal that cost RM5 two years ago now costs RM7-8. Mamak teh tarik has gone from RM1.80 to RM2.50. These are the prices people feel daily — and they bear no resemblance to 1.3%. The gap between official statistics and lived reality erodes public trust in all government data.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government uses the 1.3% figure to claim economic stability while ignoring the composition of that figure. Headline CPI is dragged down by administered prices (fuel subsidies, price controls) that mask underlying inflation. When you subsidise RON95 and control flour and sugar prices, headline inflation looks low — but the moment subsidies are withdrawn, the suppressed inflation hits all at once. The government is not controlling inflation — it is hiding it behind subsidies it can’t afford to maintain. Meanwhile, the items that aren’t price-controlled — food, services, healthcare, education — are rising at 3-5x the headline rate.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Every Malaysian government has used headline inflation figures to paint a rosier picture than reality. Under Najib, the GST introduction in 2015 caused a spike that was partially masked by rebates and handouts. Under Muhyiddin, pandemic-era price controls suppressed official inflation while actual costs soared. The pattern is consistent: governments manipulate the visible number while ordinary households bear invisible cost increases. PH promised transparency and honesty in governance — but its handling of inflation data is indistinguishable from its predecessors.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: The rakyat is laughing at your inflation numbers. That should worry you more than the numbers themselves. Publish a “cost of living index” that tracks the actual basket of goods and services used by B40 and M40 households — not the CPI composite that includes imported luxury goods and administered prices. Show Malaysians you understand what they’re actually paying. Or keep quoting 1.3% while mamak meals cost RM8 — and wonder why nobody believes anything you say about the economy.

Sources: DOSM | FMT | Malaysiakini

item.title: Youth Unemployment Stuck at 12.28% — One in Eight Young Malaysians Can't Find Work
item.description:
Said by: DOSM / ILO / World Bank Data

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: As of late 2025, Malaysia’s youth unemployment rate (ages 15-24) remains stuck at 12.28% — roughly 297,500 unemployed young Malaysians. This is three to four times higher than the overall national unemployment rate of 3.2%. DOSM, the ILO, and World Bank data all confirm the structural nature of the problem: Malaysia produces graduates the economy doesn’t need, while industries that are hiring can’t find workers with the right skills. The “Madani economy” that Anwar celebrates apparently doesn’t include young people.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: One in eight young Malaysians can’t find work. This isn’t a cyclical downturn — it’s a structural failure of the education-to-employment pipeline. Universities churn out graduates in fields with limited demand while industries face acute shortages in technical, digital, and skilled trades. The result: a generation of educated young Malaysians who are overqualified for available jobs and underqualified for the jobs that exist. Youth unemployment at 12.28% is a social time bomb — it drives brain drain, delays household formation, increases mental health burdens, and erodes faith in democratic institutions.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government touts GDP growth above 5% and record FDI as proof the economy is thriving. But an economy that grows while leaving one in eight young people unemployed is not thriving — it is extracting value without distributing opportunity. PH promised TVET reform, graduate employability programmes, and industry-aligned education. Three years later, the numbers haven’t moved. The programmes exist on paper. The graduates remain unemployed. The gap between government rhetoric (“Malaysia is booming”) and youth reality (“I can’t find a job”) is the defining economic contradiction of the Anwar era.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Youth unemployment has been structurally elevated in Malaysia for over a decade. Under BN, the rate hovered around 10-11%. Under PH 1.0, it briefly dipped before COVID. Under Muhyiddin and Ismail Sabri, it spiked to 14%+ during the pandemic. Under PH 2.0, it has settled at 12.28% — better than the pandemic peak but worse than pre-pandemic levels. No government has solved it because no government has been willing to reform the education system that produces the mismatch. Universities are political institutions in Malaysia — restructuring them means challenging entrenched interests.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: One in eight young Malaysians can’t find work. Your GDP is growing above 5%. Your FDI is at record highs. And your youth are unemployed. The disconnect is structural — and it’s your responsibility now. Reform TVET funding so it matches industry demand. Publish graduate employability data by university and programme so students can make informed choices. Partner with industries on apprenticeship programmes with guaranteed employment pathways. Or keep celebrating GDP growth while a generation of young Malaysians leaves the country to find work elsewhere.

Sources: DOSM Labour Force Survey | World Bank Malaysia Economic Monitor | ILO

item.title: Zafrul: 'We Had No Choice But to Negotiate' — The Most Honest and Damning Defence of the US Trade Deal
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, MITI Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-29

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Speaking in Parliament on 29 October 2025, Tengku Zafrul delivered what he intended as a defence of the ART but what critics heard as an admission of weakness: ‘We had no choice but to negotiate. Whether we like it or not, we need to defend our access to the US markets, even when under pressure.’ He called the deal the ‘best case scenario’ and accused detractors of ‘ignorance regarding the intense, behind-the-scenes negotiations.’

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: ‘We had no choice’ is the most revealing statement any PH minister has made. It confirms what critics suspected: Malaysia negotiated from a position of weakness, accepted terms it didn’t like, and signed because the alternative — US tariffs — was worse. That may be true. But ‘we had no choice’ is not a defence; it’s an epitaph for Malaysia’s negotiating leverage. When your minister tells Parliament the country had no options, you are telling every future trading partner that Malaysia will take whatever deal is offered.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Other countries had choices. Vietnam, Indonesia, and Thailand all face US tariffs but haven’t signed agreements with the same provisions. Indonesia negotiated its own terms. Vietnam leveraged its manufacturing base. Malaysia, under Zafrul, signed first and defended later. The ’no choice’ framing is particularly damaging because it removes agency — as if Malaysia is a passive recipient of geopolitical forces rather than a sovereign nation with options.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s trade negotiators have historically been among ASEAN’s most skilled. Mahathir — for all his flaws — refused to be pushed around on trade. Even Najib’s TPPA negotiations extracted significant carve-outs. Zafrul’s admission that Malaysia ‘had no choice’ represents a historic low in trade negotiation confidence.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: ‘We had no choice’ is not a reform slogan. It’s a surrender statement. If you truly had no choice, that’s a failure of diplomatic preparation, not a defence of the outcome. The rakyat deserves to know: what choices did you consider and reject? What alternatives were evaluated? Or did you just sign what Washington put in front of you?

Sources: Malay Mail | CNA

item.title: Selangor Water: 27% Lost to Leaks — Amirudin Touts Smart Meters While Pipes Burst
item.description:
Said by: Amirudin Shari, Selangor Menteri Besar

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-27

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 27 October 2025, Amirudin told Parliament that pilot smart water meter projects in Selangor had proven effective — citing Bukit Jelutong where non-revenue water (NRW) was recorded at 27%. He urged the federal government to adopt smart meters nationwide. Separately, the Selangor government announced it would absorb increased water costs for over one million users and accelerate pipe replacement at 300km per year, targeting a 20% water reserve margin by 2030.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Selangor has been PH-governed since 2008. After 17 years, 27% of treated water is still lost to leaks and inefficiencies in a pilot area. Across the state, NRW rates are higher. Air Selangor serves 9.62 million consumers in Selangor, KL, and Putrajaya — the largest water utility in the country. The decades-long failure to fix leaking pipes while water disruptions regularly affect millions of consumers is one of PH’s most tangible governance failures.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Amirudin is lecturing the federal government about smart meters while his own state can’t keep pipes from bursting. Selangor has had repeated water crises — pollution shutdowns, supply disruptions, and rationing — under his watch. The RM320 million Sungai Selangor Alternative Raw Water scheme (SJAM) is being framed as a solution, but the fundamental problem is infrastructure decay that PH has failed to address in 17 years of state government.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: The 2023 and 2024 water disruptions in the Klang Valley affected millions and were caused by river pollution shutting down treatment plants — a problem that proper investment in alternative sources would have prevented. Amirudin’s water master plan was only announced in 2025, after 17 years of PH governance.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You’ve governed Selangor’s water infrastructure for 17 years. NRW is still at 27%. Pipes still burst. Treatment plants still shut down from pollution. And your solution is to tell the federal government to buy smart meters? Fix your own pipes first. The rakyat doesn’t need a masterplan announced in year 17 — they needed it in year 1.

Sources: NST | Yahoo News

item.title: Malaysia Committed to Never Banning Rare Earth Exports to the US — Under Zafrul's Trade Deal
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, MITI Minister / USTR

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-26

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Under the US-Malaysia Agreement on Reciprocal Trade signed by Tengku Zafrul on 26 October 2025, Malaysia committed to not imposing ‘quantitative restrictions’ (bans or quotas) on exports of critical or rare-earth minerals to the United States. The USTR fact sheet explicitly highlights this as a US victory. Meanwhile, Zafrul dismissed Mahathir’s claim that Malaysia agreed to supply unprocessed rare earth at low returns, saying existing policy mandates domestic processing before export.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Rare earth minerals are the 21st century’s most strategic resource — essential for semiconductors, defence systems, EVs, and renewable energy. China controls roughly 60% of global rare earth mining and 90% of processing. The US desperately needs alternative supplies, and Malaysia — with Lynas’s Kuantan plant — is a key node. By committing in writing to never restrict exports to the US, Malaysia surrendered one of its most powerful geopolitical leverage points for nothing more than maintaining the status quo tariff.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Indonesia, Australia, and other mineral-rich nations are using export controls as negotiating leverage to attract downstream processing and technology transfer. Malaysia gave that leverage away. The commitment means that even if a future government wants to use rare earth as a bargaining chip — as China has done effectively — Malaysia is contractually bound not to. Zafrul traded a strategic asset for a tariff rate that didn’t even improve.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: China’s rare earth export restrictions have been its most effective trade weapon for decades. Indonesia’s nickel export ban forced companies to build smelters domestically, creating jobs and value-add. Malaysia had the same opportunity — and signed it away in a trade deal.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You just gave the United States a written guarantee that Malaysia will never use its rare earth resources as leverage. Not for technology transfer. Not for downstream jobs. Not for better trade terms. For nothing. Your minister says Malaysia’s processing-first policy is intact — but the agreement says you can’t restrict exports. When Washington comes knocking for raw minerals, your ‘policy’ will fold against a treaty obligation.

Sources: USTR Fact Sheet | FMT

item.title: RTM Misidentifies THREE ASEAN Leaders on Live TV — Calls Singapore's PM 'Lee Hsien Loong' at Summit Malaysia Hosted
item.description:
Said by: RTM / Communications Ministry under Fahmi Fadzil

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-10-26

📌 WHAT HAPPENED

On 26 October 2025, during the live broadcast of the opening ceremony of the 47th ASEAN Summit — which Malaysia was hosting — the national broadcaster Radio Televisyen Malaysia (RTM) misidentified three Southeast Asian leaders:

  • Singapore PM Lawrence Wong was called Lee Hsien Loong (his predecessor)
  • Thailand PM Anutin Charnvirakul was called Srettha Thavisin (his predecessor)
  • Indonesia President Prabowo Subianto was called Jokowi (his predecessor) RTM issued two separate apology statements. The broadcasting department said it “took the matter seriously” and that “appropriate action had been taken.”

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

This was Malaysia’s moment on the world stage. The ASEAN Summit is the region’s premier diplomatic event, and Malaysia was the host. The entire region was watching. And Malaysia’s national broadcaster couldn’t get the names of three neighbouring heads of state right.

This isn’t a minor slip. These weren’t obscure officials — they were the leaders of Singapore, Thailand, and Indonesia, Malaysia’s closest neighbours. The fact that RTM’s commentators were working from outdated information suggests nobody at the broadcaster bothered to update their briefing materials. In the age of Google, this is inexcusable.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

RTM falls under the Communications Ministry, led by Fahmi Fadzil. This is the same ministry that talks about digital transformation, AI regulation, and building Malaysia’s media capabilities. They can’t even brief their commentators on who the current leaders of neighbouring countries are.

The apology — issued in two separate statements because they initially only acknowledged two of the three errors — was the cherry on top. You don’t get to embarrass Malaysia on live television at the summit Malaysia is hosting and then say “appropriate action has been taken” without telling anyone what that action was.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

RTM has been a running joke for decades. Underfunded, poorly managed, and treated as a government mouthpiece rather than a professional broadcaster. Under PH, there was talk of reforming state media. Instead, they got a Communications Minister who focuses on regulating TikTok for teenagers while RTM can’t name three world leaders correctly.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You hosted the ASEAN Summit. You wanted the world to see Malaysia as a serious, modern, competent nation. And your national broadcaster called three neighbouring leaders by their predecessors’ names on live TV. The entire region laughed at us. Mothership in Singapore ran it. CNA ran it. SCMP ran it. This is your communications infrastructure. This is your Madani brand. Fix it.

Sources: SCMP | Bernama | FMT | CNA

item.title: Tengku Zafrul Negotiated the US Trade Deal Critics Call 'Worst Agreement Since Independence' — Then Got Promoted
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, former MITI Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-26

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 26 October 2025, then-MITI Minister Tengku Zafrul signed the Agreement on Reciprocal Trade (ART) with the United States at the 47th ASEAN Summit in Kuala Lumpur. The deal committed Malaysia to providing preferential market access for US products across chemicals, machinery, vehicles, dairy, poultry, pork, rice, and fuel ethanol. Malaysia also committed to not imposing bans or quotas on the export of critical or rare-earth minerals to the US. In return, the US maintained a 19% reciprocal tariff on Malaysian goods — with only 60.4% of tariff lines zero-rated. Critics from across the political spectrum, including Mahathir and Rafizi Ramli, called it a one-sided surrender.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The USTR fact sheet was published on the White House website before it was shared with Malaysia’s Parliament — meaning Washington told the world what Malaysia agreed to before Malaysia’s own legislators knew. The deal requires Malaysia to mirror US sanctions, act against ‘unfair practices’ by third-country firms (read: China), and open markets in sectors where Malaysian producers are vulnerable. Eurasia Review noted the deal was ‘hastily put together to please the US president during his visit’ and was ’not intended to catch much attention.’ It caught plenty.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Zafrul defended the deal as the ‘best case scenario’ — as if negotiating from weakness is an achievement. He told Parliament: ‘We had no choice but to negotiate.’ That is not a defence; it’s a confession. Malaysia’s neutral foreign policy — maintained for decades — was compromised in one signing ceremony. Zafrul’s reward for this? A promotion to Senior Political Advisor to the PM and appointment as MIDA chairman. In PH’s Malaysia, you get promoted for signing away sovereignty.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has signed trade agreements before without this level of controversy. The CPTPP was negotiated over years with extensive parliamentary scrutiny. The ART was rushed through in weeks, signed at a summit, and defended after the fact. The pattern of PH economic decisions — from 5G dual network to ECRL to this — is consistent: make the politically convenient choice, then spend months defending it.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your minister signed a deal that commits Malaysia to US sanctions compliance and mineral export guarantees, and your defence is ‘we had no choice.’ You always have a choice — you chose to capitulate. Then you promoted the man who did it. If this was really the ‘best case scenario,’ Malaysia’s negotiating position under PH is worse than anyone imagined.

Sources: CNA | USTR | Eurasia Review

item.title: US Trade Deal Details Published on White House Website Before Malaysian Parliament Was Told
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, MITI Minister / White House

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-26

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: When the US-Malaysia Agreement on Reciprocal Trade was signed on 26 October 2025, the USTR published a detailed fact sheet on the White House website outlining Malaysia’s commitments — including preferential market access for US goods, commitments on rare earth minerals, and tariff schedules. Malaysian MPs and the public learned the details of their own country’s trade agreement from an American government website. The deal’s terms were published in Washington before they were presented in Putrajaya.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: A sovereign nation’s trade commitments should be communicated to its own parliament and citizens before they are announced by the other party. The fact that Malaysians had to read the White House website to understand what their government had agreed to is a humiliation of parliamentary sovereignty. It also meant the US controlled the narrative — the terms were framed as an American victory before Malaysia could spin its own story.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH promised transparent governance and parliamentary supremacy. Then Zafrul signed a trade agreement and let Washington publish the terms first. Fahmi Fadzil is the Communications Minister — where was the proactive Malaysian disclosure? The sequence tells you everything about the power dynamics: the US dictated the terms, dictated the timeline, and dictated the narrative. Malaysia’s role was to sign and defend.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s TPPA negotiations under Najib were also criticised for opacity — but at least the government attempted to control its own narrative. Under PH, the narrative was literally ceded to the White House. Eurasia Review noted the deal was ‘hastily put together’ and ’not intended to catch much attention.’ The haste is the point — rushed deals favour the stronger party.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You let the White House tell Malaysians what their own government agreed to. That is not a partnership of equals. That is a relationship where one party writes the press release and the other party nods. If you can’t even control the communications around your own trade deals, how do you expect Malaysians to believe you controlled the negotiations?

Sources: USTR Fact Sheet | Eurasia Review

item.title: US-Malaysia Trade Deal Called 'Worst Agreement Since Independence' and 'Act of Surrender'
item.description:
Said by: Tommy Thomas, Former Attorney General (and critics)

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-26

The Deal That Sold Malaysia’s Sovereignty

On 26 October 2025, Anwar Ibrahim and Donald Trump signed the US-Malaysia Agreement on Reciprocal Trade (ART) at the ASEAN Summit. Former Attorney-General Tommy Thomas called it the worst agreement Malaysia has entered since independence in 1957. He was not exaggerating.

The Facts

Fact: The deal maintains a 19% US tariff on Malaysian goods — down from 25%, but still punitive by any standard. Malaysia’s effective tariff on US goods before this deal was roughly 6%. The US got the better end by a factor of three.

Fact: Malaysia committed to: enforce US tariff evasion rules against third countries, align with US export controls and sanctions regimes, not ban or restrict exports of critical minerals or rare earths to the US, refrain from imposing digital services taxes on US tech companies, and purchase additional American goods and services.

Fact: A termination clause allows the US to reimpose the original 25% tariff if Malaysia enters a trade deal that “jeopardizes essential US interests.” This effectively gives Washington veto power over Malaysia’s independent trade policy — including with China, Malaysia’s largest trading partner.

Fact: The deal’s full text was first published on the White House website before being shared with Malaysian Parliament. Malaysian lawmakers learned the terms of their own trade agreement from a foreign government’s press release.

Fact: Former PM Mahathir said the provisions amounted to “handing over” the country’s independence. Even for a man who oversaw his own share of questionable deals, this one was a bridge too far.

Fact: East Asia Forum’s analysis, titled “The Art of the Deal Lost in US-Malaysia Pact,” argued Malaysia surrendered critical mineral export controls, digital services taxation rights, and independent trade policy in exchange for a tariff cut that still left Malaysia paying three times what the US pays.

Fact: The Guardian reported that Anwar “defended” the deal by arguing it was “the best Malaysia could get under the circumstances” — an argument that concedes the deal is bad while claiming it could have been worse.

Fact: SCMP reported Anwar “slammed” critics for accusing him of surrendering sovereignty — but never addressed the substance of their arguments, only their tone.

Why It Matters

  • This is not a reciprocal deal by any definition of the word. Malaysia makes extensive, binding concessions across minerals, digital taxation, sanctions alignment, and procurement. The US reduces a punitive tariff by 6 percentage points and retains the right to reimpose it at will. In any negotiation textbook, this is called capitulation.
  • The sovereignty clause is the most dangerous provision. If Malaysia wants to deepen trade ties with China — its largest trading partner — the US can terminate the ART and reimpose 25% tariffs. Malaysia has effectively given Washington a veto over its foreign economic policy.
  • Publishing the details on the White House website before informing Parliament is not just embarrassing — it is a constitutional affront. Trade agreements that constrain national sovereignty should require parliamentary debate and approval before signing. Anwar bypassed the legislature on the most consequential economic agreement in decades.
  • Every ASEAN neighbour is watching. Indonesia walked away from a similar US proposal. Vietnam negotiated harder. Thailand delayed. Malaysia signed first and asked questions later. This weakens Malaysia’s negotiating position in every future bilateral and multilateral trade discussion.
  • The “best we could get” defence is an admission of weakness, not a justification. If this was truly the best outcome, it means Malaysia’s diplomatic leverage is weaker than the government has claimed — which raises questions about the entire Madani foreign policy brand. *Sources: The Guardian · East Asia Forum · SCMP · *Straits Times

item.title: Malaysia's Schooling System in 'Decline' — Students Ruder, Less Curious, Teachers Overwhelmed
item.description:
Said by: FMT Opinion / Teachers

Category: Education

Date: 2025-10-18

📌 WHAT HAPPENED

On 18 October 2025, Free Malaysia Today published an opinion piece by frontline teachers describing a schooling system in visible decline. The accounts were blunt: students scrolling TikTok under desks during lessons, snapping back at teachers when corrected, refusing to cooperate with classroom activities, and showing little to no curiosity about what’s being taught.

Teachers described fighting a losing battle — not against students, but against the algorithm. TikTok’s dopamine loop has become the real classroom authority. And structurally, teachers have nothing to fight back with: no updated pedagogy, no device management policy, no mental health support, and no institutional acknowledgment that the problem even exists.

The piece painted a picture of a profession in quiet crisis — underpaid, under-respected, and now outgunned by a phone screen.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

Malaysia’s education system was already in trouble before TikTok. PISA scores have been stagnant or declining for over a decade. The 2022 PISA results placed Malaysian students below the OECD average in reading, mathematics, and science — a pattern that has not meaningfully improved since Malaysia first participated.

What the FMT piece describes is not a new problem. It is the visible symptom of decades of systemic neglect:

  • Overcrowded classrooms with 35–40 students per teacher in many urban and semi-urban schools
  • Outdated curricula that prioritise rote memorisation over critical thinking
  • Teacher burnout driven by administrative overload, low pay, and zero autonomy
  • No digital literacy framework — students have devices but no structured guidance on using them productively
  • Parental abdication — the phone has become the babysitter, and schools are expected to fix what homes have abandoned The decline isn’t coming. It’s here. And no one in Putrajaya is treating it as a crisis.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek has spent her tenure announcing feel-good initiatives — coding programmes, STEM hubs, “joy of learning” campaigns — while the structural foundations of the system rot underneath.

On teacher welfare:

  • Teachers are buried in clerical work, data entry, and co-curricular admin that has nothing to do with teaching. The ministry’s response has been to promise “reducing teacher workload” — a promise made by every education minister since at least 2015. Nothing has changed. On classroom discipline:

  • There is no coherent national framework for managing device use in schools. Some schools ban phones outright; others ignore the issue entirely. The ministry has issued no binding directive, leaving every teacher to fend for themselves against a billion-dollar attention economy. On curriculum reform:

  • The national curriculum (KSSM) was last updated in 2017. It still emphasises exam performance over genuine learning. The system produces students who can pass SPM but cannot write a coherent paragraph, evaluate a source, or hold a sustained conversation about anything that isn’t on a screen. On funding:

  • Malaysia spends roughly 3.2% of GDP on education — below the global average of 4.3% and well below regional peers like Vietnam (4.1%) and Thailand (3.8%). PH promised to prioritise education spending. The 2025 and 2026 budgets tell a different story. The ministry’s approach is cosmetic. Announce a pilot programme. Take photos. Move on. Meanwhile, a generation of students is being failed in real time.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

  • 2022 PISA Results: Malaysia scored 398 in mathematics, 388 in reading, and 398 in science — all below the OECD average. Performance has flatlined since 2012 despite billions in education spending.
  • 2019: Teacher workload crisis. The National Union of the Teaching Profession (NUTP) publicly warned that teachers were spending more time on paperwork than teaching. The ministry promised reform. Teachers report no meaningful change.
  • 2023: Phone ban debate. After multiple viral incidents of students using phones in class, calls for a national phone ban gained traction. The ministry opted for “guidelines” rather than a directive, leaving enforcement to individual schools.
  • 2024: Graduate unemployment. Bank Negara and DOSM data showed that roughly 1 in 4 Malaysian graduates under 30 were either unemployed or underemployed. The education system is producing graduates the economy doesn’t need with skills the market doesn’t want.
  • Malaysia Education Blueprint 2013–2025. The blueprint promised to bring Malaysia into the “top third” of international education benchmarks by 2025. The deadline has passed. Malaysia is nowhere near the target.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

Fadhlina, your teachers are telling you — publicly, in national media — that they are losing their classrooms. Students are ruder. Less curious. Less engaged. And the system offers teachers nothing to fight back with.

Your response has been pilot programmes, photo ops, and vague promises about “21st-century learning.” None of it reaches the teacher standing in front of 38 teenagers who would rather watch a 15-second TikTok than learn anything.

You inherited a broken system. That’s true. But you promised to fix it. Two years in, the PISA scores haven’t moved, the teachers are more burned out than ever, and the curriculum still treats memorisation as mastery.

If PH wants to claim it governs differently, start with the one institution that touches every Malaysian family. Fund it properly. Free teachers from bureaucratic busywork. Implement a real digital literacy and device management framework. Reform the curriculum so it rewards thinking, not regurgitation.

Or keep doing what you’re doing — and watch an entire generation scroll its way to irrelevance.

Sources: FMT | PISA 2022 | World Bank — Malaysia Education | Malaysiakini

item.title: School Bullying Cases Surge 17% — 7,681 Cases in 2024 Despite 'Reforms'
item.description:
Said by: Fadhlina Sidek, Education Minister

Category: Education

Date: 2025-10-18

Schools recorded 7,681 bullying cases in 2024, up from 6,528 in 2023 — a 17% increase. Teachers report students are ‘ruder, more restless, less curious.’ Education Ministry’s disciplinary reforms are clearly not working.

item.title: Education Minister Under Fire — 'System Failed Both Teachers and Students' Says Own PH Minister
item.description:
Said by: Nik Nazmi Nik Ahmad / Fadhlina Sidek (Education Minister)

Category: Education

Date: 2025-10-17

📌 What Happened

Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek faced open criticism from multiple quarters — including her own coalition colleague, Natural Resources Minister Nik Nazmi, who publicly stated that the ’education crisis stems from a system that failed both teachers and students.’ With 7,681 bullying cases in 2024 (up 17%), 10,160 SPM no-shows, 25% math failure rates, and 30% of SPM candidates failing at least one subject, the system is in freefall.

🔥 Why It Matters

When your own cabinet colleague publicly criticises your portfolio, it means the problem has become undeniable. The numbers are catastrophic: the best-performing subject in SPM is ‘Quran and Sunnah’ at 84% pass rate, while mathematics languishes at 75% and science at 74%. A system that produces religious knowledge more effectively than basic numeracy is not preparing students for the 21st century.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

The government’s response has been cosmetic: remove exams (UPSR and PMR abolished), lower pass thresholds (20% reportedly passes math), and run communication campaigns. They’ve essentially hidden the problem by removing the measurement tools. As one analyst wrote: ’the journey seems to be just to promote incompetence from one stage to the other until students reach SPM.’ When you abolish failing tests instead of fixing failing schools, you’re choosing ignorance over reform.

📜 Previous Cases

The Malaysia Education Blueprint 2013-2025 was supposed to be the comprehensive solution. It was a beautifully designed document that changed almost nothing. Before that, there was the Education Development Master Plan 2006-2010. Before that, the Smart Schools initiative. Decades of ’transformation plans’ have produced Malaysia’s worst PISA results in history. The pattern is clear: announce plan → fund PR campaign → ignore implementation → announce new plan.

🎯 PH Callout

Your minister has been in the job since 2022. Results have gotten WORSE, not better. Instead of accountability, you give her photo opportunities. Instead of funding schools, you fund JAKIM (RM2.6B) and J-Kom propaganda (RM85.5M). Malaysian children deserve a world-class education, not a world-class communications plan about education. Fire your education minister or fix the system — preferably both.

Sources

  • The Star — Education Crisis
  • TheIns — How Malaysia’s Education Is Failing
  • Fulcrum — Think of Implementation

item.title: Najib's Lawyer: He May RETURN to Politics If Pardoned
item.description:
Said by: Muhammad Shafee Abdullah, Najib’s Lawyer

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-10-16

Najib’s lawyer openly floated his client’s return to politics if pardoned. UMNO demanded his pardon at their assembly. Anwar is stuck between needing UMNO’s support and maintaining anti-graft credentials.

item.title: RM200 Million SMUE Fund Allegedly Misappropriated — MACC Probes Selangor Senior Citizens' Scheme
item.description:
Said by: Amirudin Shari / MACC

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-10-16

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission launched an investigation into the alleged misappropriation of RM200 million from Selangor’s Skim Mesra Usia Emas (SMUE) — the state’s elderly welfare scheme. Multiple officials linked to the scheme were questioned. The SMUE fund was designed to provide financial assistance to Selangor’s senior citizens, making the alleged misappropriation particularly egregious — money meant for the elderly allegedly diverted elsewhere under PH’s watch.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: RM200 million is not a rounding error — it’s a massive sum allegedly stolen from a welfare programme for senior citizens. Selangor is PH’s crown jewel, the richest state, run continuously by PKR/PH since 2008. If RM200 million can go missing from an elderly welfare fund in a PH-controlled state, what does that say about PH’s claim to be the ‘clean governance’ coalition? The fund was supposed to help the most vulnerable. Instead, it may have helped insiders.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH has governed Selangor for nearly 18 years. This isn’t an inherited BN problem — this happened on PH’s watch, under PH’s appointees, in PH’s state. The party that built its brand on anti-corruption is facing a corruption probe in its safest state. Amirudin’s office has been conspicuously quiet about the investigation. No public statements demanding accountability. No suspension of officials. No independent audit announced. Silence.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: This joins the sand mining probe, the MBI asset freeze, and the unexplained wealth allegations as part of a pattern. Selangor under PH isn’t a model of clean governance — it’s a state where MACC keeps finding things. At some point, one investigation is an outlier; four investigations is a pattern.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: RM200 million from a senior citizens’ fund. In your state. Under your watch. With your appointees. If BN did this, you’d hold a national rally. Since it’s your state, you hold a press conference about waste management. Demand a full public audit of SMUE. Suspend the officials. And tell the rakyat where the money went.

Sources: FMT | Facebook/FMT

item.title: 77% of Malaysians Struggle With Rising Living Costs — But Anwar Says Economy Is 'Strong'
item.description:
Said by: Consumer Frontiers International survey

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-15

The 77% vs The GDP

In October 2025, a survey found that 77% of Malaysians said they struggled with rising living costs. In the same month, the government was citing GDP growth and investment figures as evidence that the economy was strong.

The Facts

Fact: Consumer Frontiers International found 77% of Malaysians struggled with rising costs despite rising wages.

Fact: DOSM reported average monthly disposable household income increased 3.2% to RM7,584 in 2024.

Fact: But economist Lee Heng Guie warned: “We must also recognise that the cost of living continues to rise.” Real gains were being eroded by inflation.

Fact: The Star headlined it: “Rising cost of living dampens income gains.”

Why It Matters

  • GDP growth and approved investments are macro numbers. They do not reflect what 77% of the population is experiencing.
  • When three-quarters of your citizens say they’re struggling, citing investment figures is tone-deaf.
  • The gap between government statistics and lived experience is the gap that creates protest movements. *Sources: Business Today · *The Star

item.title: Non-Muslims Fund RM2.6B JAKIM Through Taxes — 'Don't Insult Us as Pendatang'
item.description:
Said by: Non-Muslim taxpayers / Civil Society

Category: Religion

Date: 2025-10-15

RM2.6B Budget 2026 Islamic affairs allocation sparked debate. Non-Muslims said they accept funding Islamic affairs through taxes but pleaded: ‘don’t insult us as pendatang and kafir.’ Unity Ministry got RM711M vs JAKIM’s RM2B+.

item.title: Rafizi Warns: Farhash Allegations Undermine PM's Anti-Corruption Credentials
item.description:
Said by: Rafizi Ramli, Economy Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-10-15

📌 What Happened

Rafizi publicly urged Anwar to confront allegations against Farhash Wafa, warning that Malaysia’s CPI flatlined at 50 points — the same score as 2015 during the peak 1MDB scandal. Said it’s ’extremely dangerous if the administration is seen as inconsistent in combating corruption.'

🔥 Why It Matters

When your own Economy Minister publicly warns that your anti-corruption credentials are collapsing, the problem is undeniable. Rafizi effectively told Anwar: your refusal to act against Farhash is measurably damaging the country’s corruption score. A CPI of 50 under Anwar is the same as under Najib during 1MDB — that comparison alone is devastating.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Rafizi’s warning was ignored; he was later sidelined from cabinet. The minister who told the truth was punished, while the aide accused of corruption was protected. This is not reform — it is the old playbook in new packaging.

🎯 PH Callout

Your most competent minister warned you publicly that protecting Farhash was destroying your anti-corruption credentials. Your response was to sideline the minister and protect the aide. In any reform government, that’s backwards. In yours, it’s standard operating procedure.

Sources

  • The Vibes — Rafizi on Farhash

item.title: Farhash Tries to GAG MalaysiaNow — Courts Reject Injunctions THREE Times
item.description:
Said by: Farhash Wafa Salvador / Courts

Category: Social

Date: 2025-10-12

Farhash filed three attempts to prevent MalaysiaNow from publishing Sabah mining scandal reports. Shah Alam High Court and Court of Appeal both rejected the injunctions. The PM’s former aide tried to gag the press three times and failed three times.

item.title: JAKIM Budget Hits Record RM2.6 Billion — Highest in Malaysian History
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister / Finance Minister

Category: Religion

Date: 2025-10-11

Budget 2026 allocated RM2.6B for Islamic affairs — the highest in Malaysian history. Trajectory: RM1.9B (2024), RM2B (2025), RM2.6B (2026). Unity Ministry got only RM711M by comparison.

item.title: Anwar Marks His Own Homework — PM Who Is Also Finance Minister Presents Budget and Claims It's Great
item.description:
Said by: Opposition / Budget Analysts

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-10-10

📌 What Happened

Budget 2026 was presented by Anwar Ibrahim — as Finance Minister — to parliament, where he sits as Prime Minister. He then defended his own budget in parliament, praised its achievements, and dismissed criticism. The person who wrote the exam is also the person who grades it.

🔥 Why It Matters

In any functional democracy, the finance function has oversight independent from the political leadership. When one person controls both the political agenda and the national wallet, there is no meaningful check on fiscal decisions. Budget allocations become political tools without independent scrutiny. Parliament’s role as a budget oversight body is undermined when the PM/FM controls both the executive and the fiscal agenda.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Anwar criticised Najib SPECIFICALLY for holding both PM and FM portfolios, arguing it enabled 1MDB by removing oversight. Then Anwar took BOTH portfolios himself. When asked about this, his response was essentially ’trust me, I’m different.’ That’s not institutional reform — it’s personal exceptionalism. The same structural vulnerability that enabled 1MDB exists under Anwar.

📜 Previous Cases

Najib as PM+FM: 1MDB happened. Mahathir as PM: the Finance Minister role provided some separation. Under good governance principles, fiscal oversight requires institutional independence, not personal trust. Singapore separates the roles. The UK separates the roles. Most functional democracies separate the roles.

🎯 PH Callout

You were elected because the last PM who held both portfolios stole billions. Your solution to preventing the next 1MDB was… to replicate the exact same structural vulnerability. You’re marking your own homework, and unsurprisingly, you keep giving yourself A+. Appoint a separate Finance Minister or stop pretending institutional reform matters to you.

Sources

  • Reuters — Malaysia Budget & Living Costs

item.title: Gig Economy Workers Still Without Protections — Grab, Foodpanda Riders Risk Lives for Poverty Wages
item.description:
Said by: Labour Advocates / i-Saraan Programme

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-10

Gig workers remain without basic labour protections — no minimum wage, no mandatory accident insurance, no collective bargaining. Government’s i-Saraan Plus offers voluntary EPF matching of up to RM600/year, a band-aid on a gaping wound.

item.title: Housing Affordability Crisis: B40 and M40 Locked Out as Prices Remain Beyond Reach
item.description:
Said by: Multiple economists and NAPIC

Category: Housing

Date: 2025-10-07

The Promise of a Roof That Never Comes

Despite Anwar’s government capping affordable housing at RM300,000, the reality for B40 and M40 Malaysians remains bleak. The housing affordability crisis — one of the most serious socio-economic problems of the 21st century — has only deepened under PH.

The Facts

Fact: Using B40 median income as a benchmark, homes need to be priced below RM300,000 to be considered affordable. The government set this as the ceiling price.

Fact: National house prices increased by a cumulative 15.2% from 2019 to Q3 2025, but in real (inflation-adjusted) terms, the increase was just 4.2%.

Fact: 54.3% of KL residential transactions were below RM500,000, while 22.8% were above RM1 million — showing a deeply split market.

Fact: Research describes an “unsustainable dynamic, locking up a significant portion of the people — particularly the M40 and higher B40 — out of homeownership.”

Why It Matters

  • Setting a price cap is not the same as building homes at that price. Where are the units?
  • Young Malaysians are being priced out of their own cities. This is a generational crisis.
  • PH’s housing promises — like so many others — exist on paper but not on the ground. *Sources: The Edge · *Research

item.title: Tiong King Sing Serves Alcohol at Official Tourism Gala, Then Lies About Who Organised It
item.description:
Said by: Tiong King Sing, Tourism, Arts and Culture Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-10-05

📌 WHAT HAPPENED

Tourism, Arts and Culture Minister Tiong King Sing came under fire in October 2025 after alcoholic beverages were served at a gala dinner during the Global Travel Meet — an event organised under the Tourism Malaysia banner. Video clips showed beer and wine being served to Muslim guests, prompting several to leave early.

Tiong initially denied responsibility, claiming the private sector organised the catering and that it was “not part of Tourism Malaysia’s official programme.” But former Health Minister Khairy Jamaluddin posted screenshots on Instagram showing Tourism Malaysia was listed as the organiser of the gala dinner, mocking Tiong with “Liar, liar pants on fire.”

Anwar eventually issued a “stern warning” to Tiong but did not sack him.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

This is a minister lying on record about his own ministry’s event, getting caught with documentary evidence, and facing zero consequences beyond a “stern warning.” In a government that promised integrity and accountability, a minister who lies publicly and gets caught should face real consequences — not a pat on the wrist.

The incident also exposed the deep contradictions in Anwar’s coalition management. Tiong is from GPS (Gabungan Parti Sarawak), a critical coalition partner. Sacking him would risk losing Sarawak’s support. So accountability takes a back seat to coalition arithmetic.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

Tiong’s defence was a masterclass in gaslighting. The Tourism Malaysia logo was on the event backdrop. Tourism Malaysia staff were present. But somehow it was the “private sector’s” fault. When Khairy produced the receipts, Tiong pivoted to expressing “deep regret” without actually apologising.

And Anwar’s response? A “stern warning.” The same Anwar who once promised that his ministers would be held to the highest standards. A minister who lies publicly about his own event gets a slap on the wrist. If this were a BN minister under Najib, PH would have demanded a resignation within 24 hours.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

Tiong King Sing is the same minister who screamed “Do you know who I am?” at an airport in February 2025 — a pattern of entitlement and arrogance. He was also previously embroiled in controversies linked to alcohol consumption before this gala incident. The man has a track record of embarrassing the government and facing no real consequences.

Under BN, ministers who lied about public events were torn apart by the then-opposition. PH’s selective amnesia about accountability standards is a recurring theme.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

PH promised Kerajaan Madani — a civilised government built on integrity. A minister who lies about who organised an official event, gets caught red-handed, and faces only a “stern warning” is not integrity. It’s impunity. Either your standards apply to all your ministers — including coalition partners from Sarawak — or they mean nothing. Tiong should have been dropped from Cabinet. The fact that he wasn’t tells Malaysians everything they need to know about where coalition survival ranks versus accountability.

Sources: Malay Mail | CNA | SCMP | MalaysiaNow | The Star

item.title: Chinese and Tamil Schools Used as Political Football Every Budget — Promised Millions, Delivered Scraps
item.description:
Said by: Dong Zong / Tamil Foundation / MCA / MIC

Category: Education

Date: 2025-10-01

📌 What Happened

Every Malaysian budget cycle produces the same theatre: Chinese and Tamil school communities lobby desperately for maintenance, construction, and development funds. Politicians announce allocations with fanfare. Then the actual disbursement falls short, is delayed, or comes with conditions that limit use. The cycle repeats annually, with non-Malay communities performing gratitude for inadequate allocations.

🔥 Why It Matters

1.3 million students attend Chinese-medium schools (SJKC) and hundreds of thousands attend Tamil-medium schools (SJKT). These schools educate a significant portion of the non-Malay population and consistently outperform national schools in certain metrics. Yet they receive systematically less government funding per student, forcing communities to self-fund infrastructure that should be the government’s responsibility.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

PH, which depends heavily on Chinese and Indian votes, should have resolved this decades-old inequity. Instead, PH plays the same game as BN: use budget announcements as photo opportunities with Chinese and Indian community leaders, generate grateful headlines, then under-deliver. The structural disparity in per-student funding between national schools (SK) and vernacular schools remains because addressing it would require either more money (which the government claims it doesn’t have) or admitting that the current system is discriminatory (which it won’t).

📜 Previous Cases

BN used vernacular school allocations as political patronage — offering funds in exchange for community support. MCA and MIC existed primarily to secure these allocations, perpetuating dependency. PH was supposed to break this cycle by funding schools equitably regardless of medium of instruction. Instead, PH has maintained the BN model: Chinese and Indian schools get what PH decides to give them, when PH decides to give it.

🎯 PH Callout

Non-Malay Malaysians didn’t vote PH to continue BN’s patronage model with better optics. They voted for EQUAL treatment. Fund vernacular schools at the same per-student rate as national schools. Not as a favour. Not as a budget announcement photo op. As a constitutional obligation under Article 152 and basic educational equity. Or admit that ‘Madani for all’ has an asterisk: *terms and conditions apply based on ethnicity.

Sources

  • New Mandala — Rescuing Malaysian Higher Education
  • FMT — Decline of Malaysia’s Schooling System

item.title: Sports Budget Slashed — Athletes Struggle While Government Spends Billions on GLCs
item.description:
Said by: National Sports Council / Athletes

Category: Other

Date: 2025-10-01

📌 What Happened

Malaysian athletes consistently complain about inadequate funding, poor training facilities, and insufficient support systems, even as the government finds billions for GLC bailouts, political appointments, and civil service perks. The sports development budget has been squeezed as fiscal pressures mount, leaving athletes in Olympic and Asian Games sports to rely heavily on private sponsorship or self-funding.

🔥 Why It Matters

Training facilities in many sports are outdated compared to regional competitors like Thailand, Indonesia, and Vietnam. The national football team’s performance has been a particular source of national embarrassment, with facilities and youth development lagging despite the sport’s massive popular following. When Malaysian athletes succeed — as they occasionally do in badminton, diving, or cycling — the government is quick to claim credit. When they fail, the systemic underfunding that made failure inevitable goes unexamined.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

The contrast between the RM2.6 billion JAKIM budget for religious affairs and the significantly smaller allocation for sports development tells you everything about government priorities. Malaysia aspires to be a sporting nation but funds it like an afterthought.

🎯 PH Callout

You want gold medals but fund athletes like they’re an expense line to be minimised. You want national pride in sports but spend more on religious bureaucracy than athletic development. Pick one: either fund sports properly or stop pretending to care when the medals don’t come.

Sources

  • New Mandala — Higher Education

item.title: Tax Revenue Collection Efficiency Remains Poor — Shadow Economy Estimated at 20-30% of GDP
item.description:
Said by: Ministry of Finance / IMF Estimates

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-01

Shadow economy estimated at 20-30% of GDP. PH abolished GST in 2018 and never replaced it with an equally effective broad-based tax. The result: chronic structural revenue shortfall forcing RM75B in annual borrowing.

item.title: RON95 Subsidy Savings Slashed — From RM8 Billion Target to Actual RM950 Million
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim / Reuters

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-09-23

RON95 subsidy savings target slashed three times: RM8B to RM4B to RM950M actual. Barely 12% of the original target. The government spent more political capital debating the reform than it saved in ringgit.

item.title: Anthony Loke Admits Malaysian Society Is 'Polarised' — But Blames Opposition for Playing Up Race
item.description:
Said by: Anthony Loke, Transport Minister

Category: Social

Date: 2025-09-11

Polarised, But Don’t Blame Us

In September 2025, Transport Minister Anthony Loke told Malaysians in Hong Kong that society had become “more polarised” with intensifying race rhetoric. His diagnosis was accurate. His prescription — blame the opposition — was intellectually dishonest.

The Facts

Fact: At an event in Hong Kong, Loke acknowledged that Malaysia was increasingly divided along racial, class, and education lines, with social media amplifying hate speech and misinformation.

Fact: He attributed the polarisation primarily to “the opposition playing up such issues for political capital” — as though the government bore no responsibility for the fractures.

Fact: This came from a government that reshuffled its cabinet along racial lines in December 2025, giving UMNO more positions to shore up Malay support while reducing DAP’s presence.

Fact: JAKIM’s proposed interfaith guidelines in 2024 — which restricted how non-Muslims could engage in religious dialogue — divided communities and were widely criticised by civil society groups as discriminatory.

Fact: The government has made no move to reform race-based affirmative action policies (NEP-era quotas in education, procurement, and housing), despite PH’s manifesto promising a “needs-based” approach.

Fact: Anwar’s government has relied heavily on racial and religious rhetoric when convenient — including Anwar’s own Malay-first speeches at UMNO general assemblies, his defence of Malay-Muslim “special rights,” and his coalition’s use of the Sedition Act against those who question racial policies.

Fact: The SCMP report noted that despite Loke’s optimism about “overcoming challenges,” Malaysia’s racial polarisation metrics — measured by survey data on inter-ethnic trust — have worsened since 2023.

Why It Matters

  • Admitting the country is polarised while blaming only the opposition is the political equivalent of diagnosing the disease and prescribing someone else’s medicine. The government controls policy, appointments, media regulation, and the machinery of state. If polarisation is worsening on your watch, you own it.
  • The government’s own actions have actively contributed to division: a cabinet reshuffle that prioritised racial coalition management over competence, interfaith guidelines that alienated minorities, continued race-based policies that PH promised to reform, and a PM who code-switches between reform rhetoric for international audiences and ethno-religious rhetoric for domestic ones.
  • Loke made these remarks in Hong Kong — not in Malaysia. Malaysian politicians are remarkably candid about domestic failures when speaking to foreign audiences. At home, the same problems are denied or deflected. The gap between what they say abroad and what they do domestically is the real measure of governance.
  • The opposition absolutely plays the race card. PN’s ethno-religious populism is real and dangerous. But blaming PN for polarisation while your own government maintains race-based policies, panders to religious conservatives, and uses sedition laws against those who challenge racial orthodoxy is not analysis — it is deflection.
  • If even a DAP minister — from the party that was supposed to represent multiracial reform — cannot honestly diagnose why Malaysia is polarised, then PH has fully absorbed the BN playbook: acknowledge the symptom, blame the enemy, change nothing. *Source: *SCMP

item.title: Kampung Sungai Baru Residents Forcibly Evicted — Government Sides with Developer
item.description:
Said by: Zaliha Mustafa, Federal Territories Minister

Category: Housing

Date: 2025-09-11

Kampung Sungai Baru residents near KLCC forcibly evicted. Compensation offered was RM400/sqft — far below market value of RM1,000+/sqft. Even the Selangor Sultan weighed in supporting residents. A Malay-led government evicting Malay residents to benefit developers.

item.title: Farhash Wafa Cleared by MACC Over Sabah Mining — Muda Slams 'Pleasing the PM'
item.description:
Said by: Azam Baki, MACC Chief Commissioner

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-09-09

📌 What Happened

MACC cleared Anwar’s former aide Farhash Wafa over the Sabah mining scandal, despite Bloomberg alleging Anwar ordered MACC not to investigate. Muda accused MACC chief Azam Baki of ’trying to survive by pleasing the PM.'

🔥 Why It Matters

When a PM’s own political aide is accused of corruption in a mining deal and the anti-corruption agency clears them without a credible public explanation, the agency’s independence is in question. Bloomberg’s allegation — that Anwar directly intervened to stop the investigation — is explosive. If true, it means the anti-corruption crusader is obstructing anti-corruption investigations. If false, the government has not provided evidence to refute it.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Either way, MACC’s credibility is shattered. An anti-graft agency that cannot investigate the PM’s inner circle is not an anti-graft agency — it is a political tool. The pattern is clear: allies get cleared, critics get investigated.

🎯 PH Callout

Your anti-corruption agency cleared your aide while Bloomberg reports you told them not to investigate. You haven’t denied the Bloomberg report with evidence. You haven’t explained why MACC’s clearing is credible. You’ve just moved on and hoped nobody would notice. We noticed.

Sources

  • MalaysiaNow — Farhash Cleared
  • Malaysiakini — MACC & Farhash

item.title: Malaysia's Healthcare System: A 'Ticking Bomb' — 2.83% of GDP While Countries Spend Double
item.description:
Said by: P4H Network / University of Malaya Working Paper / The Star

Category: Health

Date: 2025-09-08

📌 What Happened

Multiple independent analyses have declared Malaysia’s healthcare system a ’ticking bomb.’ Public health expenditure sits at just 2.83% of GDP in 2023, far below WHO-recommended levels and regional peers. The doctor-to-patient ratio of 1:417 masks the true crisis in the public sector, which handles 80% of hospital beds with 54% of doctors. The country is 11,000 specialists short. Junior doctors work 84-hour weeks.

🔥 Why It Matters

Even T20 (top income) families are now switching to public hospitals because private healthcare has become unaffordable. When the rich start using government hospitals, the entire healthcare financing model has broken down. Public hospitals face overcrowding, equipment shortages, and staff burnout simultaneously.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

The government’s response? ‘Targeted subsidies’ and a healthcare ‘reset plan.’ But experts warn this risks ’leaving the poor behind.’ The University of Malaya working paper called for a ‘paradigm shift’ from treating health funding as consumption to treating it as ‘strategic national investment.’ Instead, the government increases JAKIM’s budget and civil service pensions while hospitals crumble.

📜 Previous Cases

Malaysia’s healthcare was once the pride of the nation. The rot began with decades of underfunding across BN and now PH administrations. Contract doctors were promised permanent posts — still waiting. Medical graduates face uncertain futures while the government fast-tracks political appointees to cushy GLC positions.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised healthcare reform. Instead, you’ve presided over a system where Malaysians are literally dying on waiting lists for specialist care — 14 cardiothoracic surgeons for 33 million people. Your response is another committee, another ‘plan,’ another press conference. Fix your hospitals first.

Sources

  • P4H — Healthcare Ticking Bomb
  • UM Working Paper
  • The Star — Healthcare Crisis

item.title: T20 Families Now Using Public Hospitals — The Rich Can't Afford Private Healthcare Anymore
item.description:
Said by: Federation of Malaysian Consumers Associations / The Star

Category: Health

Date: 2025-09-08

📌 What Happened

The Federation of Malaysian Consumers Associations (FOMCA) sounded alarms that even T20 families (top 20% income earners) are switching to public hospitals because private healthcare has become prohibitively expensive. FOMCA senior manager Saral James Maniam called it a ‘big red flag.’

🔥 Why It Matters

When Malaysia’s wealthiest citizens find private healthcare unaffordable, the entire healthcare financing model has collapsed. These families flood already-overwhelmed public hospitals, creating longer queues and worse outcomes for B40 and M40 patients who have no alternative. The system is experiencing a doom loop: rising private costs → T20 shift to public → public system overwhelmed → quality drops → nobody gets adequate care.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Health Minister Dzulkefly Ahmad’s response was ’targeted subsidies’ — essentially more means-testing bureaucracy rather than addressing root causes. Malaysia spends 2.83% of GDP on public health while Thailand spends 3.8%, Vietnam 3.0%, and developed countries 6-10%. The government KNOWS the system is underfunded but refuses to allocate more because the money goes to civil service pensions, JAKIM, political appointments, and debt servicing.

🎯 PH Callout

When even the RICH can’t afford to be sick in Malaysia, your healthcare system hasn’t just failed the poor — it’s failed everyone. Stop announcing ’transformation plans’ and start transforming hospitals. Every day you delay is measured in patient suffering.

Sources

  • The Star — Healthcare Crisis Hitting All Income Groups
  • P4H — Healthcare Ticking Bomb

item.title: Anwar's Coalition Contradictions: Reformist PM, Corrupt DPM, and a Party That Enabled 1MDB
item.description:
Said by: Multiple analysts and commentators

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-09-01

The Impossible Coalition

Anwar Ibrahim governs Malaysia through a coalition that makes reform structurally impossible. The man who campaigned for decades against corruption now depends on the party that enabled the world’s largest kleptocracy.

The Cast of Characters

  • Anwar Ibrahim (PKR): Built his career on reformasi, anti-corruption, and clean governance.
  • Ahmad Zahid Hamidi (UMNO): Deputy PM. Faced 47 corruption charges. All dropped.
  • DAP: The coalition’s most consistent reform voice. Systematically sidelined in the December 2025 reshuffle.
  • UMNO: Najib’s party. Threatened to review coalition ties when a DAP MP celebrated Najib’s court loss.

The Structural Problem

As Fair Observer noted, Anwar’s coalition is fundamentally unstable because its members have opposing interests:

  • PH voters want reform, accountability, and institutional change.
  • UMNO wants protection for its leaders, patronage for its base, and Malay-first policies.
  • The harder Anwar pushes reform, the more he risks losing UMNO. The more he accommodates UMNO, the more he loses the reform base.

The Result

What you get is not reform. What you get is management. Coalition maintenance disguised as governance. Every major decision — the Zahid NFA, the cabinet reshuffle, the UUCA backtrack, the JAKIM guidelines U-turn — makes sense only through the lens of keeping the coalition together, not making Malaysia better.

*Source: *Fair Observer
