📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Under BN, the Anti-Fake News Act was PH’s primary evidence of authoritarian internet control. PH repealed it in 2018. Under PH 2.0, the replacement regime is more comprehensive: website blocking through MCMC, platform licensing, the Online Safety Act, CMA amendments with RM10 million penalties, and self-censorship driven by regulatory uncertainty. Each individual measure is framed as protecting children, combating scams, or fighting misinformation. Collectively, they constitute a censorship apparatus that exceeds anything BN built.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Bloomberg, Rest of World, ARTICLE 19, and Freedom House all agree: you’re building an internet censorship regime. Creators are self-censoring. Websites are blocked. Platforms face RM10 million penalties. The MSC Bill of Guarantees promised no internet censorship. You’ve broken that promise more comprehensively than any previous government. Withdraw the excessive penalties. Unblock critical news sites. Protect the free internet that gave you power. Or accept that you’ve built the censorship infrastructure every future authoritarian government will thank you for.

Sources: Bloomberg | Rest of World | ARTICLE 19

item.title: 2024 Floods Displaced Hundreds of Thousands — Northern States Devastated Again
item.description:
Said by: ReliefWeb / East Asia Forum

Category: Other

Date: 2024-12-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In December 2024, severe flooding in northern states displaced hundreds of thousands of Malaysians. Kelantan, Terengganu, and Pahang bore the brunt of the monsoon season. RM15 billion had been approved for flood mitigation after the devastating 2021 disaster, but progress was glacial. The same states that flood every year flooded again, with the same communities bearing the same losses. East Asia Forum documented how the 2024 floods exposed continued governance failures in disaster preparedness and infrastructure investment.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The same states flood repeatedly — Kelantan, Terengganu, Pahang, and parts of Johor and Selangor. The problem is not the rain. The problem is governance failure. Flood mitigation infrastructure has been promised, funded, and announced under every government. Implementation consistently falls short. Communities that were told “never again” after 2021 found themselves in evacuation centres again in 2024. When the same disaster hits the same people in the same places year after year, it is not a natural disaster — it is a policy choice.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: RM15 billion was approved for flood mitigation. Where did it go? Which projects were completed? Which are behind schedule? Which were never started? The government has not published a comprehensive implementation report. Communities that should have new drainage, retention ponds, and river deepening projects are still sandbagging their homes every November. The money exists. The projects were announced. The floods came anyway. That gap between allocation and implementation is the space where governance failure lives.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: The 2021 floods killed 54 people and displaced over 400,000. The government approved RM15 billion for mitigation. The 2022 floods displaced tens of thousands. The 2023 floods displaced more. The 2024 floods displaced hundreds of thousands. Each year, the government announces relief, promises mitigation, and moves on — until the next monsoon. The cycle has continued under BN, PN, and PH. No government has broken it because breaking it requires sustained, expensive infrastructure investment in areas that don’t generate political returns proportional to the cost.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: RM15 billion approved. Hundreds of thousands displaced again. The same states. The same communities. The same pattern. Publish a project-by-project audit of the flood mitigation budget. Show which projects were completed, which are delayed, and which were never started. Hold contractors accountable for delays. Pre-position relief resources before monsoon season. Or keep treating annual floods as annual surprises — while the rakyat keeps losing everything they own, every year, in the same places.

Sources: ReliefWeb | East Asia Forum

item.title: Asia Maior: Anwar Sidelines 'Open Discussion on Multiple Lines of Disenfranchisement'
item.description:
Said by: Asia Maior Academic Journal

Category: Politics

Date: 2024-12-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In late 2024, the academic journal Asia Maior published an assessment of Anwar Ibrahim’s governance, concluding that he faced challenges “without substantially interrogating a public discourse that has historically sidelined open discussion on multiple socio-economic lines of disenfranchisement.” The analysis documented how Anwar’s reformist rhetoric coexisted with a status quo reality — maintaining the same patronage structures, ethnic frameworks, and institutional arrangements that he had spent decades promising to dismantle.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When an academic journal assesses that your government avoids “open discussion on multiple lines of disenfranchisement,” it is saying that you have chosen political safety over substantive reform. The lines of disenfranchisement in Malaysia are well-documented: ethnic quotas in education and public sector employment, gender inequality in legal rights, wealth concentration among politically connected elites, and East Malaysian marginalisation. PH promised to address all of these. Asia Maior’s assessment suggests none have been substantively confronted.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar built his political career on challenging the very structures Asia Maior identifies as untouched. Reformasi was supposed to mean reforming the ethnic patronage system, opening public discourse, and creating a Malaysia based on merit rather than identity. Three years into government, the discourse hasn’t opened — it has been managed. The patronage system hasn’t been dismantled — it has been rebranded. PH’s approach to disenfranchisement is identical to BN’s: acknowledge it in speeches, avoid it in policy, and hope the economy grows fast enough that nobody notices.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Every Malaysian academic assessment follows a similar trajectory. International scholars note the gap between PH’s rhetoric and reality with increasing precision. The Rosa Luxemburg Foundation documented “corruption, racial divides, and economic discontent.” East Asia Forum documented reform aspirations clashing with political realities. Asia Maior documented the avoidance of open discourse. Each assessment comes from a different angle but reaches the same conclusion: PH talks reform but practices status quo governance.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Academic journals are documenting what Malaysians already know: your reform rhetoric doesn’t match your governance reality. “Multiple lines of disenfranchisement” remain unaddressed because addressing them would cost you politically. Open the discourse Anwar promised. Commission an independent review of ethnic quotas, public sector hiring, and wealth distribution. Publish the data. Let Malaysians debate the findings. Or keep avoiding the conversation while scholars document your avoidance — and watch the reform mandate evaporate.

Sources: Asia Maior

item.title: Orang Asli Land Rights Violations Continue — Indigenous Communities Displaced for Logging and Plantations
item.description:
Said by: Amnesty International / Indigenous Rights Groups

Category: Social

Date: 2024-12-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Throughout 2024, Orang Asli and indigenous communities in Peninsular Malaysia continued to face systematic violations of customary land rights. Ancestral forests were cleared for logging concessions and palm oil plantations, often without free, prior, and informed consent. Amnesty International flagged indigenous rights as one of seven areas where Malaysia fell short of its human rights obligations. Orang Asli make up just 0.2% of Malaysia’s civil service despite comprising approximately 0.7% of the population — even proportional representation is denied.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Orang Asli are Malaysia’s most marginalised community. Their customary land rights — recognised by courts but systematically violated by state governments — are the foundation of their identity, livelihood, and cultural survival. When ancestral forests are cleared for plantations, Orang Asli don’t just lose land — they lose food sources, water catchments, medicinal plants, and burial grounds. The PH government, which claims to champion human rights and social justice, has done nothing to strengthen legal protections for indigenous land rights or enforce existing court rulings.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH includes politicians who have advocated for indigenous rights in opposition. Baru Bian — a prominent land rights lawyer and PH MP — has fought indigenous land cases for decades. Yet in government, PH has not introduced legislation to codify customary land rights, has not enforced existing court rulings protecting indigenous territories, and has not increased Orang Asli representation in the civil service or decision-making bodies. The 0.2% civil service representation figure means Orang Asli are virtually invisible in the government that claims to speak for all Malaysians.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Indigenous land dispossession has been ongoing since independence. Under BN, logging concessions were issued to politically connected companies with no consultation. Under PH 1.0, a brief moratorium on certain concessions was announced before the Sheraton Move. Under PH 2.0, the pattern has resumed. Court rulings recognising customary land rights — including landmark Federal Court decisions — are routinely ignored by state governments. The legal framework exists to protect indigenous rights. The political will to enforce it does not.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Orang Asli land is still being taken. Court rulings are still being ignored. Your civil service has 0.2% Orang Asli representation. Amnesty flagged it. Indigenous rights groups are documenting it. And your government is silent. Codify customary land rights in federal legislation. Enforce existing court rulings. Increase indigenous representation in government. Or keep talking about “Madani civilisation” while Malaysia’s original inhabitants watch their ancestral forests being turned into palm oil plantations — under your watch.

Sources: Amnesty International | Survival International | Malaysiakini

item.title: Rosa Luxemburg Foundation: 'Corruption, Racial Divides, Economic Discontent' Under Anwar
item.description:
Said by: Rosa Luxemburg Stiftung Analysis

Category: Politics

Date: 2024-12-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In late 2024, the Rosa Luxemburg Stiftung (RLS), a German political foundation, published a detailed assessment of Malaysia under Anwar Ibrahim. The report concluded: “High-level corruption persists, rising racial tensions with politicians exploiting sensitive issues, government response focused on managing incidents through archaic laws rather than promoting open dialogue.” RLS documented how the Anwar government maintained the same patronage structures and ethnic frameworks it had promised to dismantle, while using repressive laws to manage dissent rather than addressing root causes.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When an international political foundation with no partisan stake in Malaysian politics describes your government as maintaining “high-level corruption” and managing racial tensions through “archaic laws,” it is a clinical diagnosis of governance failure. RLS is not an opposition mouthpiece — it is an academic institution that analyses political economies globally. Its assessment aligns with HRW, Amnesty International, Asia Maior, and East Asia Forum — a consensus among international observers that PH’s reform agenda has been abandoned.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH’s defence against every international assessment is the same: “they don’t understand Malaysian context.” But when five different international institutions — from different continents, with different methodologies — all reach the same conclusion, the problem is not their understanding. It is your governance. RLS specifically noted that the government’s response is to manage incidents rather than promote open dialogue. That’s not reform — it’s crowd control. Managing racial tensions through the Sedition Act is not addressing racism. It is suppressing the conversation while the underlying problems fester.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: International assessments of Malaysian governance have followed a consistent arc under PH 2.0. HRW: “Reform Agenda Stalled” (2024), “Rights Backslide” (2025). Amnesty: “Betrayed reform pledges” (2024). RSF: 34-place drop in press freedom (2024). Asia Maior: avoids “open discussion on multiple lines of disenfranchisement.” East Asia Forum: “reform aspirations clashed with political realities.” RLS: “corruption, racial divides, economic discontent.” Each assessment arrives independently at the same destination: PH talks reform but governs like BN.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: International observers have reached a consensus: your reform agenda has been abandoned. Corruption persists. Racial tensions are managed through archaic laws, not addressed through open dialogue. Economic discontent is growing. You can dismiss one critical report. You cannot dismiss five from five different institutions on five different continents. Either recommit to the reform agenda that got you elected — or accept that the international community has classified your government as what it is: a continuation of the status quo with better English.

Sources: Rosa Luxemburg Stiftung

item.title: Worst Floods in a Decade — 90,000 Displaced, Ministers' Leave Frozen Too Late
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Other

Date: 2024-11-29

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In late November 2024, Malaysia experienced its worst floods in a decade. Over 90,000 people were displaced across 9 states, with Kelantan, Terengganu, and Pahang worst affected. Anwar froze ministers’ leave — but only after the crisis had already escalated to emergency levels. The annual monsoon flooding, which occurs with clockwork regularity every year, once again exposed inadequate infrastructure and disaster preparedness despite billions allocated for flood mitigation after the catastrophic 2021 floods.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Monsoon floods are not surprises. They happen every year, in the same states, during the same months. A government that is caught unprepared by predictable annual flooding is not unlucky — it is incompetent. The 90,000 displaced figure represents real families losing homes, possessions, and livelihoods. Freezing ministers’ leave after the crisis escalates is reactive management, not disaster preparedness. A competent government would have had pre-positioned resources, evacuation plans, and ministerial coordination in place before the monsoon arrived.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: After the catastrophic 2021 floods under Ismail Sabri, the government approved RM15 billion for flood mitigation. Under PH, implementation has been glacial. Mitigation projects are behind schedule. Drainage infrastructure in flood-prone areas remains inadequate. The same communities that flooded in 2021, 2022, and 2023 flooded again in 2024. The money was approved. The projects were announced. The floods came anyway. When RM15 billion in mitigation spending doesn’t prevent 90,000 displacements, either the money was misspent or the projects weren’t built. Both are governance failures.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia floods every year. Under Najib, the 2014 floods displaced over 200,000 — and Najib was caught playing golf in Hawaii. Under Ismail Sabri, the 2021 floods killed 54 people and displaced over 400,000. Under PH, the 2024 floods displaced 90,000 — better than 2021 but still catastrophic for the affected communities. The pattern is identical across administrations: inadequate preparation, slow response, promises of mitigation spending, glacial implementation, and the same communities flooding again the next year.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Monsoon floods are predictable. You had a year to prepare since last year’s floods. You had RM15 billion allocated for mitigation. And 90,000 people were still displaced. Freezing ministers’ leave after the crisis hits is not disaster management — it’s damage control. Publish an audit of the RM15 billion flood mitigation allocation: how much was spent, on what, and why it didn’t prevent this year’s displacements. Pre-position resources before next year’s monsoon. Or keep reacting to predictable disasters and wondering why the rakyat doesn’t trust you to protect them.

Sources: ReliefWeb | CNA | Bernama

item.title: Anwar's Overseas Trips: 75% Funded by Private Companies — Then Government Says Actually No
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister (and Fahmi Fadzil)

Category: Politics

Date: 2024-11-22

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In November 2024, Anwar told Parliament that private companies like Petronas and Proton covered approximately 75% of his overseas travel costs under a new cost-sharing strategy. The next day, Communications Minister Fahmi Fadzil “clarified” that corporate executives “paid their own way” while the government covered Anwar’s delegation. The government had spent RM1.6 million to charter a plane for a four-country trip. When questioned further, Anwar called it a “crazy era” that transparency was being punished. CNA analysts warned the practice “could expose vulnerabilities in Malaysia’s political financing framework.”

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The PM said one thing in Parliament — private companies covered 75% of travel costs. His Communications Minister said something different the next day — companies paid for their own executives, not Anwar’s delegation. Which is it? If private companies are co-funding PM travel, there are legitimate questions about access and influence. Companies that fly with the PM get face time, relationship-building, and perceived government endorsement. That access has value — and when it’s purchased through travel funding, it raises conflict-of-interest concerns.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Calling critics ungrateful for questioning government spending is not transparency — it is deflection. Anwar styled the cost-sharing as fiscal responsibility, but the messaging collapse within 24 hours suggests the arrangement was either poorly structured or poorly understood by his own cabinet. A government that can’t keep its story straight on travel expenses for one news cycle is not demonstrating transparency — it is demonstrating dysfunction. And RM1.6 million to charter a plane for four countries is not exactly belt-tightening, regardless of who pays.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: PM travel expenses have been contentious under every administration. Najib’s use of a government jet for personal trips and the purchase of a luxury jet were signature scandals. PH criticised every ringgit of Najib’s travel spending. Under PH 2.0, Anwar has taken over 100 overseas trips in three years — more than any previous Malaysian PM in the same period. The cost-sharing arrangement was supposed to answer criticism about travel frequency. Instead, it created a new controversy about corporate influence.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your PM told Parliament one thing. Your Communications Minister said something different the next day. Then your PM complained that transparency is being punished. That’s not transparency — that’s contradictory messaging followed by self-pity. Publish a clear, detailed accounting of every overseas trip: government cost, corporate participation, and what business outcomes resulted. If the cost-sharing is legitimate, prove it with data. If it’s not, stop doing it. But don’t tell Parliament one figure and have your minister contradict it within 24 hours — and expect Malaysians to call that governance.

Sources: CNA | Malay Mail | FMT

item.title: Nga Kor Ming Blows RM4 Million on 4 'BMW-Class Smart Toilets' While Rakyat Struggles With Cost of Living
item.description:
Said by: Nga Kor Ming, Housing and Local Government Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-11-14

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In November 2024, Housing and Local Government Minister Nga Kor Ming announced that RM4 million had been allocated to build four “BMW-class” smart toilets in Kuala Lumpur and Putrajaya — RM1 million per toilet. He made the announcement at the Toilet of the Year Award (TOTYA) 2024 ceremony, describing it as part of a broader RM180 million allocation over three years for public toilet upgrades. By June 2025, former MCA vice-president Ti Lian Ker demanded an update, noting zero progress reports since the announcement.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: RM1 million per toilet. The average Malaysian household income is around RM7,000-8,000 per month. The government is spending the equivalent of over 10 years of a household’s income on a single toilet, while telling the rakyat to tighten their belts. The RM180 million national toilet upgrade is a legitimate use of funds. But turning four toilets into “BMW-class” showpieces at RM1 million each — in KL and Putrajaya, the places that already have the best infrastructure — is the kind of tone-deaf spending that makes people lose faith in reform governments.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Nga Kor Ming is the same minister who threatened a journalist for asking about event naming, and whose MyKiosk programme failed MACC review for lacking a needs assessment. “BMW-class” toilets — the branding alone tells you this isn’t about serving the public, it’s about making the minister look good. And when asked for an update months later? Silence. No progress report. No accountability. Just a vanity announcement followed by a black hole of implementation. Meanwhile, regular public toilets across Malaysia remain filthy and broken. But sure, build luxury toilets in areas that already have the best infrastructure.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has a long history of inflated government procurement. The RM600 toilet seats and RM1,200 screwdrivers of the BN era were bread-and-butter corruption stories. PH screamed about wasteful spending for decades. Now they’re spending RM1 million per toilet and calling it innovation. Nga himself has a pattern of grand announcements with poor follow-through — the MyKiosk programme being the most recent example of MACC flagging his ministry’s projects for governance failures.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You promised fiscal discipline and responsible governance. You told Malaysians every ringgit would be spent wisely under Kerajaan Madani. Then you spend RM1 million per toilet and can’t provide a progress report when asked. The rakyat can’t afford eggs, but your minister is building BMW toilets. If this is reform, the rakyat would like a refund.

Sources: FMT | NST | Bernama

item.title: Rice Shortages Deepen Food Security Fears — Anwar Wants 'More Monitoring'
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-11-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In late 2024, sporadic rice shortages hit Malaysia alongside devastating floods. Supermarket shelves emptied of local rice varieties in multiple states. Anwar’s response was to call for “frequent monitoring and enforcement” — the government’s default when it has no plan. Malaysia imports over 30% of its rice supply, and the domestic padi industry has been neglected for decades. Global rice prices hit multi-year highs due to India’s export restrictions, El Niño impacts on Southeast Asian harvests, and supply chain disruptions.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Rice is the staple food for 33 million Malaysians. When rice shelves are empty, food security is not theoretical — it is immediate. Malaysia’s 30%+ import dependency means any global supply disruption directly threatens domestic food security. The padi industry has been underfunded, fragmented, and politically managed for decades. Smallholder farmers receive insufficient support. Irrigation infrastructure is crumbling. Yields per hectare lag behind Thailand, Vietnam, and Indonesia. The rice shortage was predictable — and the government was not prepared.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: “Monitoring and enforcement” is what you do when you have no plan. It is the bureaucratic equivalent of watching the problem get worse while looking busy. PH promised food security reform. Three years later, Malaysia’s rice import dependency hasn’t decreased, domestic production hasn’t meaningfully increased, and the government’s response to shortages is enforcement — punishing traders for hoarding rather than addressing the structural supply deficit. You can’t enforce rice into existence. You need to grow it, and that requires investment the government hasn’t made.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has faced periodic rice shortages under every government. Under BN, the response was always the same: release strategic reserves, blame hoarders, promise investment in padi. Under PH 1.0, food security was briefly elevated as a policy priority before the Sheraton Move. Under PH 2.0, the government launched a “food security” initiative that generated press conferences but no measurable improvement in self-sufficiency ratios. The structural problem — declining padi acreage, aging farmers, insufficient irrigation, and reliance on imports — remains identical to what it was 20 years ago.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Rice shelves are empty. Your response is “monitoring.” Malaysia imports 30%+ of its rice and you’ve done nothing to reduce that dependency in three years. Invest in padi infrastructure — irrigation, mechanisation, yield improvement. Support smallholder farmers with guaranteed minimum prices. Build strategic reserves that can absorb global supply shocks. Or keep “monitoring” while Malaysians can’t find rice at the supermarket — and hope they don’t notice that “monitoring” is not food security.

Sources: Bernama | FMT | Reuters

item.title: Foreign Worker Levy Multi-Tier System — Announced 2023, Still Not Fully Implemented
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-10-18

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In September 2023, Anwar announced a multi-tier levy system for foreign workers designed to reduce Malaysia’s dependency on imported labour. The system was repeated in Budget 2025 with further details. The Malaysian Employers Federation (MEF) complained the system was still unfair and insufficiently implemented. Anwar’s promise to cut foreign worker dependency to 15% of the workforce by 2025 was not met. As of late 2025, Malaysia still employed approximately 2.2 million documented foreign workers, with an estimated 1-2 million undocumented — a dependency that has not meaningfully changed under PH.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Foreign worker dependency suppresses local wages, particularly for B40 Malaysians who compete directly with imported labour in construction, agriculture, manufacturing, and services. The multi-tier levy was supposed to make it progressively more expensive for employers to hire foreign workers, incentivising automation, productivity improvements, and local hiring. Two years after announcement, the system is still not fully implemented. Meanwhile, industries continue importing workers at levels that undercut the wage floor for Malaysian citizens.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Announcing a policy and implementing it are two different things — and PH excels at the former while failing at the latter. The multi-tier levy was announced in 2023, repeated in 2024, and included in Budget 2025. At each stage, it was presented as a new initiative. The 15% target was quietly dropped when it became clear it wouldn’t be met. MEF’s complaints suggest that even the implemented portions are poorly designed. The foreign worker lobby — representing plantation companies, construction firms, and manufacturers with political connections — has successfully delayed meaningful reform while the announcements continue.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Every Malaysian government has promised to reduce foreign worker dependency. Under Mahathir 1.0, the promise was made in the 1990s. Under Najib, various levy increases were announced. Under PH 1.0, a freeze on new foreign worker permits was briefly implemented before being reversed. Under PH 2.0, the multi-tier levy was announced with fanfare but implementation has been glacial. The pattern is consistent: announce reform, face industry pushback, delay implementation, announce it again in the next budget. Meanwhile, 3-4 million foreign workers remain in the country.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You announced the multi-tier levy in 2023. It’s 2026 and it’s still not fully implemented. Your 15% target was missed. MEF says the system is unfair. Industries keep importing workers at the same rate. Either implement the levy with teeth — real enforcement, meaningful cost increases for employers who over-rely on foreign labour — or admit that the announcement was for headlines, not for policy. Malaysian workers deserve local wage protection. They’re not getting it because your government is more afraid of upsetting the plantation lobby than protecting the rakyat.

Sources: Bernama | FMT | MEF

item.title: MACC Seizes RM6M and Freezes Assets Linked to Selangor Menteri Besar Inc in Sand Mining Corruption Probe
item.description:
Said by: Amirudin Shari / MACC

Category: Politics

Date: 2024-10-18

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In October 2024, the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) seized RM6 million and froze assets linked to Selangor Menteri Besar Incorporated (MBI) in connection with a sand mining corruption probe. MACC Chief Commissioner Azam Baki confirmed that five firms linked to a Selangor politician were under investigation for alleged corruption involving a sand mining concession. The probe uncovered a network of contracts and payments that raised questions about the integrity of Selangor’s sand mining approvals.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Selangor MBI is the state’s investment arm — directly under the Menteri Besar’s purview. When MACC seizes millions linked to the MB’s own corporate vehicle, it implicates the governance of the state’s highest office. Sand mining is one of Selangor’s most corruption-prone sectors, involving environmental destruction, river pollution, and billions in revenue. The fact that MACC had to intervene means internal checks failed.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Amirudin has been MB since 2018. Sand mining corruption in Selangor has been documented for years. Environmental groups have warned about illegal mining destroying riverbanks and poisoning water sources. Under Amirudin’s watch, the problem grew until MACC had to step in. That’s not governance — that’s negligence. The MB either didn’t know (incompetence) or didn’t act (complicity). Neither is acceptable.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: In September 2025, MACC confirmed new developments in the MBI corruption probe. A former Selangor GLC officer was charged over RM1.7 million in graft. Earlier, activist Chegubard lodged a MACC report alleging RM10 billion in irregularities linked to the Selangor Maritime Gateway project. The pattern is consistent: Selangor under PH is a corruption hotspot that the government refuses to acknowledge.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: MACC is investigating your state investment arm for corruption. Your own GLC officers have been charged. Your sand mining sector is a mess. And your response is silence. If this happened under a BN menteri besar, PKR would be holding nightly ceramah demanding resignation. Under Amirudin? Crickets.

Sources: Malay Mail | Yahoo News

item.title: Forest City Special Financial Zone — 0% Tax for the Ultra-Rich While Rakyat Pays 8% SST
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim Administration / Securities Commission

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-10-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In October 2024, the PH government launched the Forest City Special Financial Zone (SFZ) in Johor, offering 0% corporate tax for up to 15 years for qualifying businesses, 0% tax for ultra-high-net-worth family offices, and incentives for fintech, fund management, and digital asset companies. The SFZ was designed to rescue Forest City — a RM400 billion Chinese-backed property development that sat largely empty — by transforming it into a financial hub. Meanwhile, ordinary Malaysians pay 8% SST on everyday services including healthcare, education, and rent.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The SFZ creates a two-tier tax system: 0% for the ultra-rich who park their wealth in Forest City, and 8% SST for ordinary Malaysians buying groceries, seeing a doctor, or sending their children to school. The optics are devastating — the government is simultaneously expanding SST to cover basic necessities while offering tax-free status to billionaires’ family offices. This is not investment attraction — it is a wealth shelter branded as economic development.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Forest City was a failed property project. Billions of dollars in Chinese investment produced a ghost city that Mahathir himself mocked. Instead of letting the market price in the failure, the Anwar government is using public policy — tax exemptions, regulatory incentives, infrastructure spending — to rescue a private development. The beneficiaries are property developers and ultra-wealthy investors. The cost is borne by Malaysian taxpayers who lose the revenue that tax-exempt businesses would otherwise contribute. Socialising losses and privatising gains is the definition of crony capitalism — and PH promised to end it.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has a long history of special economic zones that promised transformation and delivered tax giveaways. The Iskandar Development Region offered tax incentives for over a decade — Johor’s inequality worsened. Labuan’s offshore financial centre attracted shell companies more than genuine investment. Cyberjaya was supposed to be Malaysia’s Silicon Valley — it became an office park. Each zone follows the same pattern: generous tax incentives, minimal clawback mechanisms, and benefits that accrue to capital rather than labour. Forest City SFZ adds another chapter to this pattern.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You’re offering 0% tax to billionaires’ family offices while charging 8% SST on the rakyat’s healthcare and education. You’re rescuing a failed Chinese property project with public policy incentives. You’re socialising the losses and privatising the gains — exactly what you accused BN of doing. Publish a cost-benefit analysis of the SFZ. Show how many Malaysian jobs it will create versus how much tax revenue Malaysia loses. Explain why ultra-wealthy family offices deserve 0% tax when hawkers pay SST. Or admit that the SFZ is a bailout for developers, not an economic strategy for Malaysia.

Sources: Nikkei Asia | Reuters | Straits Times

item.title: GISBH Child Abuse Scandal — Amirudin Says Abuses Were 'Concealed by Shifting to Business'
item.description:
Said by: Amirudin Shari, Selangor Menteri Besar

Category: Social

Date: 2024-09-15

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Following the explosive GISBH (Global Ikhwan Services and Business Holdings) scandal involving sexual and physical abuse of children in welfare homes, Amirudin stated that the organisation had ‘concealed abuses by shifting to business’ — suggesting that GISBH’s transition from the banned Al-Arqam movement into a legitimate business empire allowed it to hide criminal activity. Hundreds of children were rescued from GISBH-linked premises across Malaysia, with many found in Selangor.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: GISBH operated openly in Selangor for years. Its premises were known. Its business operations were visible. Its welfare homes housed hundreds of children. The Selangor state government — which oversees local authority licensing, land use, welfare inspections, and Islamic affairs — either failed to detect the abuse or failed to act on it. Amirudin’s explanation that GISBH ‘concealed’ itself by becoming a business is an admission that his state government was unable to monitor organisations operating openly within its borders.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: You don’t get to govern Selangor for six years, have a massive child abuse ring operating in your state, and then say ’they hid it well.’ State welfare departments are supposed to inspect children’s homes. Local authorities issue business licences. JAIS oversees Islamic organisations. If all these agencies missed what was happening in GISBH homes, that’s not concealment — that’s institutional failure. And institutional failure in Selangor is Amirudin’s responsibility.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Al-Arqam was banned in 1994 — thirty years ago. Its transformation into GISBH was well-documented. Journalists and activists had raised concerns about the organisation for years. The pattern of authorities ignoring well-documented abuses until they explode into public scandal is a Malaysian governance tradition that PH was supposed to end.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Children were abused in welfare homes in your state, under your governance, by an organisation that has been on the radar for thirty years. Your excuse is that they ‘concealed’ themselves by becoming a business. That is not an explanation — it is a confession that your welfare inspection, licensing, and monitoring systems are broken. Fix them, or the next GISBH is already operating in Selangor while you make speeches about development.

Sources: Yahoo News

item.title: Halal Certification Controversy — Non-Muslims Caught in Crossfire of Religious Overreach
item.description:
Said by: Ahmad Zahid Hamidi, DPM / Teresa Kok, MP

Category: Religion

Date: 2024-09-15

Debate erupted over forcing non-Muslim businesses to get halal certification. Kelantan required it for premises serving Muslims. Teresa Kok argued it should remain voluntary. Zahid had to clarify no cabinet decision was made.

item.title: Hannah Yeoh Fangirls Over Thai Shuttler Who Just Eliminated Malaysia's Lee Zii Jia — Tone-Deaf at the Olympics
item.description:
Said by: Hannah Yeoh, Youth and Sports Minister (then)

Category: Social

Date: 2024-08-07

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In August 2024 at the Paris Olympics, Malaysian shuttler Lee Zii Jia was knocked out in the men’s singles semi-final by Thailand’s Kunlavut Vitidsarn — a devastating loss for Malaysian fans hoping for an Olympic medal. Days later, Youth and Sports Minister Hannah Yeoh posted selfies with Kunlavut on Instagram, praising him. SCMP reported she was “smashed on social media for ‘fangirl’ post.” Mothership called it “insensitive.” Yeoh defended her post, saying Malaysia is “not a sore loser” and that it was her duty to “instil a culture of healthy competition.”

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Timing matters in politics. A Sports Minister’s job at the Olympics is to support the national team. Taking selfies with the athlete who just crushed your country’s medal hopes — and posting them on social media — shows a fundamental lack of political instinct. Nobody would have objected if Yeoh had congratulated Kunlavut privately or weeks later. The issue is posting fangirl selfies while the wound was still fresh, while millions of Malaysians were still processing the disappointment. Read the room.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Yeoh’s defence — promoting sportsmanship — is a classic politician’s deflection. Nobody accused her of lacking sportsmanship. They accused her of lacking sensitivity. There’s a difference. Imagine a Japanese Sports Minister posting selfies with an American swimmer who just eliminated Japan’s favourite — it wouldn’t happen, because competent politicians understand that the public’s emotions matter during the Olympics. Yeoh’s tone-deafness isn’t new. It’s a pattern of a politician who confuses personal branding with ministerial responsibilities.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: This wasn’t Yeoh’s only Olympics controversy. The Malaysian Olympic attire design for Paris 2024 also sparked public criticism, with many questioning why the national kit looked uninspired. Yeoh, as Sports Minister, bore responsibility for the overall presentation of Team Malaysia. Instead of a proud Olympic moment, Malaysia got two controversies. Yeoh’s tenure as Sports Minister was marked by a series of tone-deaf moments that suggested disconnection from public sentiment rather than deliberate insensitivity.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You sent Hannah Yeoh to Paris to support Malaysia’s Olympic athletes. She came back with selfies with the guy who beat our best shuttler. The Sports Minister’s job is to champion Malaysian sport, not audition for a Thai fan club. The fact that she couldn’t understand why Malaysians were upset tells you everything about how disconnected PH ministers are from public sentiment. Political instinct isn’t optional — it’s the job.

Sources: SCMP/Straits Times | MalaysiaNow | Mothership | The Vibes

item.title: Fahmi Fadzil Forces Social Media Licensing on Platforms — Critics Call It PH's Version of Internet Censorship
item.description:
Said by: Fahmi Fadzil, Communications Minister

Category: Social

Date: 2024-07-28

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 28 July 2024, the PH government announced that social media platforms with more than 8 million users in Malaysia would be required to apply for a regulatory licence from MCMC, effective 1 August. Communications Minister Fahmi Fadzil said the measure was needed to combat cyber offences including online scams, child exploitation, and deepfakes. By late 2025, Fahmi was threatening legal action against non-compliant platforms. In January 2026, MCMC temporarily blocked X’s Grok AI feature over deepfake concerns. ARTICLE 19 called on Malaysia to “halt hasty imposition of mandatory electronic verification,” warning about implications for privacy and free speech.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Social media licensing gives the government a lever to pressure platforms into compliance with content removal requests. While the stated goals — combating scams, protecting children — are legitimate, the mechanism creates obvious censorship risks. When a government can threaten to revoke a platform’s operating licence, it has enormous power over what content stays up and what comes down. In a country with a history of using the Communications and Multimedia Act to prosecute critics, handing MCMC more regulatory power over social media is a loaded gun.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH rode social media to power. Facebook, Twitter, YouTube, and WhatsApp were the platforms that allowed PH to circumvent BN’s control of mainstream media. Without social media, the 1MDB scandal might never have reached the Malaysian public at the scale it did. Now in power, PH is licensing the same platforms, threatening legal action against those that don’t comply, and blocking AI features. The “child safety” framing is clever but transparent — the licensing framework applies to all content, not just child safety. PH repealed the Anti-Fake News Act and called themselves defenders of internet freedom. Then they introduced a licensing regime that gives MCMC even more power than the Act they repealed.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Under BN, the Anti-Fake News Act 2018 was PH’s exhibit A for authoritarian internet control. PH repealed it in 2018. Now PH has replaced it with something arguably more powerful: a licensing regime that gives MCMC ongoing regulatory leverage over platforms, rather than just post-hoc prosecution. PH also opposed MCMC’s use of Section 233 of the CMA to prosecute critics under BN. Under PH, the same section continues to be used, and platforms themselves can now be held liable through the licensing framework.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You repealed the Anti-Fake News Act and called yourselves defenders of internet freedom. Then you introduced a licensing regime that gives MCMC even more power over social media than BN ever had. You’re licensing the internet while claiming to protect it. The child safety argument is legitimate — but the licensing framework goes far beyond child safety. Be honest about what you’re building: a regulatory tool that can pressure platforms into compliance with government content demands. That’s not reform. That’s BN’s internet controls with a PH label.

Sources: Reuters | ARTICLE 19 | Malay Mail

item.title: Diesel Price Jumps 56% Overnight — Anwar Says Critics Will Attack Him Regardless
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-06-10

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 10 June 2024, diesel prices in Peninsular Malaysia jumped from RM2.15 to RM3.35 per litre — a 56% increase overnight. Finance Minister II Amir Hamzah Azizan announced the price float on 9 June, effective the next morning — one day’s notice for a shock that rippled through logistics, food prices, and small businesses. Sabah, Sarawak, and Labuan remained at RM2.15. Anwar told reporters: “We must also know that whatever we do, we will be severely criticised with all sorts of slander and lies.” His response to citizens facing a 56% cost increase was to complain about the quality of criticism he was receiving.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The diesel subsidy bill had ballooned to RM14.3 billion annually, and most economists agreed the blanket subsidy was regressive. The policy may be economically necessary, but the execution was politically negligent. A 56% price hike with one day’s notice is a shock to households, small businesses, fishermen, and farmers who cannot absorb overnight cost increases. Phased implementation over 6-12 months would have achieved the same fiscal goal with a fraction of the pain. The Diplomat noted that diesel increases have cascading effects — logistics, food transport, construction, agriculture, and fishing all depend on diesel.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar’s dismissal of all criticism as “slander and lies” is not just tone-deaf — it is contemptuous. Many of the people struggling with higher diesel costs are PH voters. They are not slandering the PM — they are telling him they cannot afford to fill their trucks. The two-tier pricing system created an immediate arbitrage problem — diesel smuggling from Sabah and Sarawak increased, adding enforcement costs. The government promised RM14.3 billion in savings would fund public transport and targeted aid. But PADU was sidelined. Public transport expansion outside KL remains negligible. The money was saved, but the promised reinvestment is largely invisible to the people who sacrificed.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: When Najib attempted fuel subsidy reform in 2014, PH was among the loudest critics. When petrol prices fluctuated under the managed float system, PH accused BN of burdening the people. Now in government, PH implemented an even more aggressive subsidy cut — 56% overnight versus Najib’s gradual float — and called critics slanderers. The hypocrisy of implementing tougher measures than those you attacked your predecessors for is the defining feature of PH’s economic governance.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You raised diesel by 56% overnight with one day’s notice. Your response to criticism was to call it slander. Your promised reinvestment — public transport, targeted aid — hasn’t materialised. PADU was sidelined. Buses didn’t appear. If the rakyat must tighten their belts, show them you’re tightening yours first. Publish where the RM14.3 billion in savings went. Deliver the public transport improvements you promised. Phase future subsidy reforms gradually. Or keep imposing overnight shocks and dismissing the pain as slander.

Sources: Reuters | UCA News | The Diplomat

item.title: Diesel Price Jumps 56% Overnight as PH Removes Subsidies — Rakyat Told to 'Tighten Belts'
item.description:
Said by: Amir Hamzah Azizan, Finance Minister II; Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-06-10

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 10 June 2024, the PH government removed diesel subsidies in Peninsular Malaysia, causing the price to jump from RM2.15 to RM3.35 per litre overnight — a 56% increase. Finance Minister II Amir Hamzah Azizan framed it as “targeted subsidy rationalisation” and announced a Subsidised Diesel Control System for eligible sectors. The impact was immediate: transport costs surged, food prices climbed, and businesses passed the costs on to consumers. The government promised B40 and M40 households would be protected through BUDI MADANI cash assistance, but payments were widely criticised as insufficient.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Subsidy reform is necessary — Malaysia was spending RM14.3 billion on diesel subsidies in 2023, much of which leaked to smuggling and foreign vehicles. The fiscal case is strong. But the execution was brutal. A 56% overnight price increase hits working Malaysians hard, especially in logistics-dependent sectors. Research showed businesses across multiple industries faced immediate cost pressures, passing costs directly to consumers — exactly the outcome PH promised to prevent. Sabah and Sarawak were exempted, creating a two-tier system that was as much about coalition management as regional sensitivity.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH spent years attacking BN’s subsidy policies — both for being too generous (fiscal waste) and for any attempt to reform them (burdening the rakyat). Classic opposition have-your-cake-and-eat-it. Now in power, PH removed diesel subsidies with a 56% overnight hike and told Malaysians it was for their own good. The BUDI MADANI cash assistance was modest compared to cascading cost increases. The savings were supposed to fund public transport and targeted transfers. By 2026, public transport outside KL remains minimal, and PADU — the targeting mechanism — was sidelined. The money was saved. The reinvestment was not delivered.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: When Najib attempted fuel subsidy reform in 2014, PH was among the loudest critics. The broader pattern is consistent: PH promises populist policies in opposition, then implements the same tough economic measures in government that they attacked their predecessors for. Subsidy reform, GST (abolished, replaced with less efficient SST), and toll abolition (still hasn’t happened) all follow the same trajectory. AMRO’s analysis recommended a phased approach — PH chose shock therapy instead.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Subsidy reform was necessary. Nobody disputes that. But you could have done it gradually, transparently, and with adequate cushioning. Instead, you did it fast and hard, while your ministers still enjoy subsidised fuel cards. Publish where the RM14.3 billion in annual savings went. Deliver the public transport improvements you promised. If the rakyat must tighten their belts, show them you’re tightening yours first. Or keep implementing overnight shocks while promising reinvestment that never arrives.

Sources: MOF Malaysia | Fulcrum | LSE | AMRO

item.title: Dual 5G Network: Billions Wasted on Political Compromise — One Network Would Have Sufficed
item.description:
Said by: Telecom Analysts / Consumers

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-06-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In mid-2024, the PH government shifted Malaysia’s 5G rollout from a single wholesale network (Digital Nasional Berhad, DNB) to a dual-network model — allowing a second 5G network to be built alongside DNB. The move was widely seen as a political compromise to accommodate major telecom operators with political connections who had opposed the single-network model. The dual-network approach doubles infrastructure costs, splits spectrum efficiency, and delays rural coverage expansion — all to solve a political problem, not a technical one.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: 5G infrastructure costs billions. A single wholesale network — the model adopted by countries like South Korea and parts of Europe — is more spectrum-efficient and avoids duplicating expensive infrastructure. The dual-network model means Malaysia will build two parallel 5G networks where one would have sufficed. The additional cost — estimated in the billions — comes from public funds and consumer pricing. Rural Malaysians, who were supposed to benefit from 5G coverage expansion, will wait longer because resources are now split between two networks instead of concentrated on reaching underserved areas.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The single-network model was working. DNB had achieved over 80% population coverage by mid-2024. The dual-network shift was not driven by technical failure or consumer demand — it was driven by telecom operators who wanted to maintain their competitive advantage and the political actors who supported them. The government framed it as “promoting competition.” But you don’t need two 5G networks to have competition — you need one efficient network with fair wholesale access for all operators. Duplication is waste, not competition.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s telecom sector has a long history of political patronage. Under Mahathir 1.0, telecom licences were distributed to politically connected companies. Under BN, spectrum allocation benefited government-linked entities. The dual-network decision fits the same pattern: technical policy shaped by political interests rather than engineering logic. South Korea built one of the world’s fastest 5G networks using a competitive but coordinated approach. Malaysia is building two slower networks to keep politically connected telcos happy.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You chose politics over engineering. One 5G network would have cost less, delivered more coverage, and reached rural areas faster. Instead, you approved a second network to accommodate telecom operators with political leverage. Publish a cost-benefit analysis comparing single vs. dual networks. Disclose which operators lobbied for the change and what access they were promised. Or keep spending billions on duplicated infrastructure while rural Malaysians wait for connectivity that one network could have delivered years ago.

Sources: Nikkei Asia | The Edge | Reuters

item.title: MCMC Blocks Critical News Sites — MalaysiaNow, Asia Sentinel, UtusanTV Censored
item.description:
Said by: Freedom House Report 2024

Category: Social

Date: 2024-06-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In mid-2024, MCMC blocked news outlets critical of the government including MalaysiaNow, UtusanTV, TV Pertiwi, and Asia Sentinel. A new Code of Ethics effectively grants or bars media access based on compliance — creating a de facto licensing system for journalism. A man was sentenced to 6 months for a Facebook post insulting a Sultan. Freedom House’s 2024 report documented Malaysia’s internet freedom as declining under PH, flagging the blocking of news sites and the expansion of MCMC’s content moderation powers as key concerns.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Blocking entire news outlets — not individual articles, but entire publications — is authoritarian behaviour regardless of which party does it. MalaysiaNow, Asia Sentinel, and UtusanTV were blocked because they published content the government found inconvenient, not because they were publishing illegal material. When the government controls which news outlets Malaysians can access, it controls the information environment. Freedom House explicitly flagged this as a decline in internet freedom — under a government that promised to protect it.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar once relied on independent media to survive politically. Without Malaysiakini, Sarawak Report, and international outlets covering 1MDB, his political comeback would not have been possible. Now his government is blocking news sites that publish critical coverage. The Code of Ethics creates a carrot-and-stick system where compliant media gets access and critical media gets blocked. This is not regulation — it is state management of the press. The irony of a former political prisoner censoring the press that helped free him is beyond parody.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Under BN, Sarawak Report was blocked for its 1MDB coverage. The Malaysian Insider was shut down. The Printing Presses and Publications Act required annual licences for print media. PH condemned all of this as authoritarian. Under PH, the approach is subtler but equally effective: instead of banning publications through licensing, you block them through MCMC and control access through the Code of Ethics. The end result is identical — the government decides which media the public can consume.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You survived because independent media told your story when the government wanted to silence you. Now you’re the government, and you’re silencing independent media. Unblock MalaysiaNow, Asia Sentinel, and UtusanTV. Withdraw the Code of Ethics that functions as a gatekeeping mechanism. Let journalists do their jobs without government approval. Or keep blocking the press while claiming to protect press freedom — and hope Malaysians don’t notice the contradiction.

Sources: Freedom House

item.title: Police Excessive Force at Protests — Reformasi Leader Now Uses Riot Police Against His Own Supporters
item.description:
Said by: Lawyers for Liberty / Suaram / Bersih

Category: Social

Date: 2024-06-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Throughout 2024, multiple incidents of police using excessive force and pre-emptive arrests against peaceful protesters were documented under the Anwar government. Lawyers for Liberty, Suaram, and Bersih all issued condemnations. Police conducted home visits to warn potential rally participants, arrested organisers before events, and deployed disproportionate force at demonstrations. The Peaceful Assembly Act — which PH had promised to reform — was used to justify suppression of the same civil liberties PH once championed.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Anwar Ibrahim built his entire political career on street protests. The Reformasi movement began as a mass protest. Bersih rallies were PH’s political oxygen. The right to peaceful assembly was the foundational civil liberty that made PH’s rise to power possible. Now PH uses the same PDRM apparatus — the same riot police, the same PAA provisions, the same intimidation tactics — to suppress protesters demanding the reforms PH promised. The transformation from protest movement to protest suppressor took less than two years.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH promised to reform the Peaceful Assembly Act to remove prior notification requirements and police veto powers. Instead, police are using the Act more aggressively than under BN for equivalent events. Lawyers for Liberty documented cases where police cited Nik Nazmi’s own 2013 case law — law that PKR fought and won — to justify suppressing assembly rights under PH’s government. The party that created the legal precedent for protest rights is now ignoring that precedent. This isn’t policy drift — it’s deliberate repression.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Under BN, police crackdowns on Bersih rallies were front-page international news. Tear gas at Bersih 2.0 (2011), water cannons at Bersih 3.0 (2012), and mass arrests at subsequent rallies were cited by HRW, Amnesty, and RSF as evidence of Malaysia’s authoritarian governance. PH used every single crackdown to build sympathy, recruit supporters, and fundraise. The international community recognised PH as a democratic movement fighting for fundamental freedoms. Now the same international community is documenting PH doing exactly what BN did.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You were the protest movement. Your leader was jailed for it. Your MPs were charged for it. Your supporters were tear-gassed for it. And now you’re doing it to others. Reform the Peaceful Assembly Act as promised. Issue a directive prohibiting pre-emptive arrests and home visits for rally participants. Hold PDRM accountable for excessive force. Or accept that Reformasi ended the day you started using riot police against the same people who marched for your freedom.

Sources: FMT | Suaram | Lawyers for Liberty

item.title: Press Cards as Weapons — New Code of Ethics Lets Government Decide Who Is a 'Real' Journalist
item.description:
Said by: Freedom House / Journalists’ Union

Category: Social

Date: 2024-06-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In mid-2024, the government released an updated Code of Ethics for Journalists that effectively creates a de facto licensing system for journalism in Malaysia. Media access to government events, press conferences, and official sources is increasingly dependent on compliance with the code — meaning outlets that publish critical reporting risk losing access. Freedom House documented this as a form of soft censorship. Self-censorship becomes the mechanism: critical stories risk loss of access, advertising boycotts from government-linked companies, or MCMC investigations.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When the government decides who qualifies as a “real” journalist, it controls the information environment without needing to formally censor anyone. The Code of Ethics doesn’t ban publications — it gates access. Journalists who comply get press cards, access to ministers, and invitations to official events. Journalists who don’t comply lose all of this. The result is soft censorship: editors learn which stories cost them access, and they adjust accordingly. No law is broken. No outlet is formally banned. The press is simply managed.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH campaigned explicitly on press freedom. Anwar, a political prisoner whose case was partly championed by press freedom organisations worldwide, should understand better than anyone the importance of a free press. Instead, his government has created a code of ethics that functions as a gatekeeping mechanism — deciding who gets to be a journalist and who doesn’t, based on compliance with government-set standards. Every journalist who self-censors to keep their access is a journalist PH has silenced without the bad optics of a formal ban. Subtle censorship is still censorship.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Under BN, the Printing Presses and Publications Act required annual licences for print media — direct censorship that PH promised to repeal. They didn’t repeal it. Under PH, the approach is subtler but equally effective: instead of requiring licences for publications, you require compliance for access. The Sedition Act, the PPPA, and now the Code of Ethics form a layered system of media control that is more sophisticated than BN’s but achieves the same outcome — a press that knows the boundaries and stays within them.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: A free press protects democracy. You know this — you were a political prisoner partly rescued by press freedom. Now you’ve created a system where journalists need government approval to be “real” journalists. Withdraw the Code of Ethics as a gatekeeping mechanism. Guarantee access to all accredited journalists regardless of editorial line. Repeal the PPPA as promised. Or keep managing the press through access control — and accept that your press freedom record is indistinguishable from BN’s.

Sources: Freedom House | SCMP

item.title: 30% of SPM Candidates FAIL At Least One Subject — A Pass Grade of 20% in Math Is Not Education, It's a Lie
item.description:
Said by: Education Ministry Data / Independent Analysis

Category: Education

Date: 2024-05-27

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In SPM 2023 results released in May 2024, approximately 30% of candidates failed at least one subject. Mathematics had the highest failure rate at 25.9%, followed by science at 25%. The best-performing subject was Quran and Sunnah at 84%. Reports indicate the pass threshold for mathematics may be as low as 20% — meaning a student who gets 4 out of 5 questions wrong still “passes.” The government had previously abolished UPSR (Year 6) and PMR (Year 9) examinations, removing the only diagnostic checkpoints that could identify struggling students early.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When you set the pass mark at 20%, you’re not measuring education — you’re manufacturing the illusion of it. A student who “passes” mathematics with a 20% score cannot do basic arithmetic reliably. These students enter the workforce, attempt further education, or become statistics in the 12.28% youth unemployment figure. The education system is producing graduates with certificates but without competence. Under BN, Malaysia maintained mediocre but stable PISA scores (440 in math in 2018). Under PH, scores crashed 31 points to 409. The percentage of students below basic proficiency increased from 41% to 59%.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government abolished UPSR and PMR — removing the diagnostic checkpoints that would catch struggling students at ages 12 and 15 — then acts surprised when 30% fail at SPM. As one analyst put it: “The journey seems to be just to promote incompetence from one stage to the other until students reach SPM.” When the government removes tests and lowers pass marks, it’s not reforming education — it’s hiding failure. The education system is producing religious knowledge better than mathematics, science, or English — and the government’s response is more blueprints, more jargon, and more press conferences.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: The Malaysia Education Blueprint 2013-2025, continued by PH, has been a decade-long failure by its own metrics. PISA scores have declined. SPM failure rates remain high. Youth unemployment suggests the system doesn’t prepare students for work. Every education minister launches a “transformation.” None transforms anything. The fundamental problem — a curriculum designed around political and ethnic considerations rather than educational excellence — remains untouched because reforming it would require confronting interests no government has been willing to challenge.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your education system produces religious knowledge better than mathematics. Your students are 8 years behind Singapore and 3 years behind Vietnam. You abolished the tests that would have caught this earlier. You lowered the pass mark to hide the failure. And you call this “Madani civilisation”? A civilisation that can’t teach its children to count is not a civilisation — it’s a civilisational collapse in slow motion. Restore diagnostic checkpoints. Raise pass standards to meaningful levels. Reform the curriculum for competence, not compliance. Or keep producing SPM certificates that employers don’t trust and the world doesn’t recognise.

Sources: TheIns | Fulcrum | ERIC

item.title: KFC Closes 100+ Outlets Amid Pro-Palestine Boycott — Government Watches From the Sidelines
item.description:
Said by: QSR Brands Holdings (KFC operator)

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-05-03

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In May 2024, KFC Malaysia temporarily closed over 100 outlets amid boycotts linked to the Gaza conflict. Thousands of Malaysian workers — mostly low-income B40 families — faced reduced hours or outright job losses. McDonald’s global sales fell 0.1% in 2024 and 1% in Q1 2025, driven largely by boycotts in Muslim-majority countries including Malaysia. The boycott movement was fueled by social media activists and opposition politicians — particularly PAS and PN — who framed consumption at Western fast-food chains as complicity in the Gaza conflict. The government’s response was conspicuously, strategically absent — no minister endorsed or opposed the boycott.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The government’s silence was calculated cowardice. Supporting the boycott would anger investors, franchise operators, and the business community. Opposing it would anger the Malay-Muslim base and hand PN a political weapon. So the government said nothing while thousands of its own citizens lost income. QSR Brands — the KFC operator — is a Malaysian-owned company that employs Malaysian workers, pays Malaysian taxes, and sources from Malaysian suppliers. The boycott punished Malaysian businesses and workers for the perceived sins of a foreign brand.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: No government relief programme was targeted at affected F&B workers. Madani’s promise to prioritise B40 welfare evaporated the moment it conflicted with political convenience. These workers — cashiers, cooks, delivery riders — were collateral damage in a geopolitical conflict they had no role in. Opposition politicians who championed the boycott faced no scrutiny for the economic damage they encouraged. PAS MPs who called for boycotts offered no alternative employment plan for displaced workers. The government, by staying silent, let the opposition set the narrative and absorbed none of the accountability.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Consumer boycotts in Malaysia have historically been driven by ethno-religious mobilisation rather than economic analysis. The 1969 “Buy British Last” campaign, various anti-Chinese boycotts, and periodic anti-Israel product campaigns have all followed the same pattern: moral outrage drives consumer action, workers bear the cost, and politicians exploit the sentiment without managing the consequences. Moral clarity on Palestine does not require destroying the livelihoods of your own citizens. A responsible government would have separated legitimate solidarity from economic self-harm.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Thousands of B40 workers lost income because you were too afraid to take a position. Supporting Palestine is a moral imperative. Destroying Malaysian livelihoods is not. You could have funded Palestinian aid while protecting Malaysian workers. You could have spoken clearly: “boycott is your right, but these are Malaysian businesses employing Malaysian families.” Instead, you chose silence — the path of least political resistance. Cowardice is not governance.

Sources: Al Jazeera | TIME

item.title: Press Freedom Plunges 34 Spots to 107th — 'Difficult Situation' Under PH
item.description:
Said by: Reporters Without Borders (RSF)

Category: Social

Date: 2024-05-03

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In May 2024, Reporters Without Borders (RSF) released the 2024 World Press Freedom Index, in which Malaysia plunged 34 places — from 73rd to 107th — under the PH government. RSF classified Malaysia as a “difficult situation” for press freedom. The collapse was driven by sedition charges against journalists and activists, MCMC content blocking, social media licensing requirements, and the arrest of FMT journalist Rex Tan. Even after a partial recovery to 88th in 2025, Malaysia still trails its 2023 ranking under PH.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: A 34-place drop in press freedom under a government that promised media reform is not a statistical fluctuation — it is an indictment. PH’s 2022 manifesto included commitments to repeal the Sedition Act, reform the Printing Presses and Publications Act, and protect press freedom. Instead, under PH, journalists have been arrested, news sites blocked, and a social media licensing regime introduced. RSF’s “difficult situation” classification places Malaysia alongside countries PH claims to be morally superior to. The ranking reflects not just specific incidents but a systemic deterioration in the media environment.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH’s response to the ranking drop was to blame “inherited problems” and promise improvement. But the specific incidents RSF cited — Chegubard’s sedition charge, Rex Tan’s arrest, MCMC blocking MalaysiaNow and Asia Sentinel — all occurred under PH’s watch. These are not inherited problems; they are PH-created problems. The government that promised to protect journalists is now prosecuting them. The government that promised to unblock websites is now blocking more. Every press freedom commitment has been reversed.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Under Najib, Malaysia’s press freedom ranking hovered around 140th — driven by 1MDB-related media suppression, the blocking of Sarawak Report, and sedition charges against editors. PH 1.0 achieved a dramatic improvement to 101st in 2019. PH 2.0 initially maintained the improvement before the 2024 collapse. The trajectory is clear: PH delivers press freedom gains in opposition and early government, then reverts to suppression once media scrutiny becomes inconvenient. The 34-place drop happened in a single year — suggesting a deliberate policy shift, not gradual decline.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You dropped 34 places in press freedom in a single year. RSF calls Malaysia a “difficult situation.” Your manifesto promised media freedom. Your government delivered media suppression. Unblock MalaysiaNow and Asia Sentinel. Drop charges against journalists. Repeal the Sedition Act as promised. Withdraw the social media licensing regime. Or keep falling in the rankings — and stop pretending Malaysia is a democracy when the world’s press freedom watchdog classifies it as difficult.

Sources: RSF | Malaysiakini | FMT

item.title: Forest City Casino Saga — Anwar Threatens JAIL for Those Who Report on It
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2024-05-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In May 2024, Anwar Ibrahim called allegations about a casino at Forest City in Johor “malicious” and suggested those spreading the rumours could face jail. SCMP reported that Anwar and tycoons connected to the Forest City Special Financial Zone were “urged to walk back threats” after the aggressive response drew backlash. Chegubard — the activist who reported on the casino plan — was actually charged with sedition for his social media post. The government’s response to journalism about Forest City was not transparency or denial with evidence — it was threats of imprisonment.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When a Prime Minister threatens jail for people who report on a development project, it sends a devastating signal about press freedom and public accountability. Forest City is a politically sensitive project — a Chinese-backed development in Johor that the government is promoting as a Special Financial Zone with 0% tax incentives. Questions about what activities will be permitted there are legitimate public interest journalism, not sedition. The threat of jail for reporting is not a response to misinformation — it is a suppression of scrutiny.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: If the casino allegations were false, the government had every tool available to debunk them: issue a formal denial, publish the SFZ regulations, show that gaming is excluded. Instead, Anwar chose threats. That’s not the response of a government with nothing to hide — it’s the response of a government that wants questions to stop. SCMP’s reporting — that Anwar and tycoons were “urged to walk back threats” — suggests even allies recognised the response was disproportionate. When your reaction to journalism is more aggressive than your reaction to corruption, your priorities are transparent.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysian PMs have a long history of threatening critics instead of addressing allegations. Mahathir threatened journalists who questioned his policies. Najib’s government used the Official Secrets Act, Sedition Act, and MACC against critics of 1MDB. Muhyiddin used emergency ordinances to suppress dissent during COVID. PH promised to break this pattern. Instead, Anwar’s response to Forest City reporting was indistinguishable from his predecessors: threaten jail, charge the critic, and hope the story dies.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: The correct response to false allegations is evidence, not threats. If there’s no casino planned at Forest City, publish the SFZ regulations and prove it. If there is, own it and defend the policy. But threatening jail for reporters asking questions about a publicly funded special zone is authoritarian — and you know it, because you spent 25 years on the receiving end of exactly this tactic. Stop threatening journalists. Start answering questions.

Sources: SCMP | Malaysiakini

item.title: Chegubard Charged with SEDITION for Reporting Forest City Casino Claims — Reformasi's Free Speech Promise Dies
item.description:
Said by: Johor Sessions Court / Badrul Hisham (Chegubard)

Category: Social

Date: 2024-04-30

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 30 April 2024, activist Badrul Hisham Shaharin (Chegubard) was charged under Malaysia’s colonial-era Sedition Act for a social media post about casino plans at Forest City in Johor. The Sedition Act is the same law PH promised to repeal during its 2018 and 2022 election campaigns. As of March 2026, Chegubard’s appeal is still pending at the Johor Bahru Sessions Court. Instead of repealing the Sedition Act, the Anwar government is now weaponising it against critics — the exact same playbook used by BN against Anwar himself in the 1990s.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Every repressive law PH promised to abolish — the Sedition Act, the Printing Presses and Publications Act, SOSMA, Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act — is still on the books and being actively used by PH. Press freedom organisations condemned the prosecution as a chilling signal to journalists and activists: in Anwar’s Malaysia, questioning powerful interests — especially those connected to the Forest City special economic zone — can land you in court. The message is clear: free speech ends where powerful business interests begin.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar Ibrahim was himself charged under the Sedition Act. He knows what it feels like to face prosecution for speech. He built an entire political movement — Reformasi — on the injustice of being silenced by the state. Now his government is using the same law against a man who reported on casino plans at a development the government is actively promoting. The Sedition Act wasn’t just left unrepealed — it was expanded, with over 90 sedition-related arrests under PH. The man who was once a victim of these laws is now wielding them.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Under BN, the Sedition Act was used against opposition politicians, academics, lawyers, and journalists. Anwar, Karpal Singh, Azmi Sharom, and dozens of others were charged or investigated. PH condemned every single prosecution as authoritarian overreach. Under PH 2.0, the same law has been used against Chegubard, social media commentators, and critics of government policy. The only difference is the name on the charge sheet — the law, the process, and the chilling effect are identical.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You were jailed under these laws. You know what they feel like. You promised to repeal them. And now you’re using them against the same kind of activists who marched for your freedom. Repeal the Sedition Act — as you promised in 2018 and 2022. Drop the charges against Chegubard. Or admit that Reformasi was never about free speech — it was about freeing one man, not the system.

Sources: NST | SCMP

item.title: KK Mart Allah Socks — Stores Firebombed While Anwar Tells Public Not to Be Judge
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Religion

Date: 2024-03-25

Firebombs and a Lecture on Patience

In March 2024, socks bearing the word “Allah” were found for sale at KK Super Mart outlets. What followed was weeks of outrage, boycott calls, and three Molotov cocktail attacks on stores. Anwar’s government took days to respond. When the PM finally spoke, he told the public not to act as “prosecutor or judge.”

The Facts

Fact: On 13 March 2024, socks with the word “Allah” were discovered at a KK Super Mart in Sunway City. Only 14 pairs were found across three outlets.

Fact: Three KK Mart outlets were attacked with Molotov cocktails in Perak, Pahang, and Sarawak. No injuries were reported.

Fact: KK Mart’s owners and the supplier were charged with “wounding religious feelings.” They pleaded not guilty.

Fact: Anwar, on 25 March, told the public: “You’re neither prosecutor nor judge… the country would not be able to see any growth if the people decided to harp on certain issues.”

The Government’s Failure

  • The feeble response was the story. Critics noted Anwar and Deputy PM Zahid were slow to address the crisis, allowing it to spiral into vigilante violence.
  • Telling the public to calm down after petrol bombs have been thrown at shops is not leadership — it is damage control.
  • The socks were a supplier error. The violence was a governance failure. *Sources: The Guardian · Straits Times · BBC · *SCMP

item.title: Electricity Tariff Hike + SST on Electricity — Double Whammy on Rakyat's Monthly Bills
item.description:
Said by: Energy Commission / Consumer Groups

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-03-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In March 2024, the government imposed 8% SST on commercial electricity, adding costs that businesses pass directly to consumers through higher prices for goods and services.
